### 3.3 Derivation 3 – Ovality and Taper of an Engine Cylinder

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most common application-based derivation** in PEMUT635 ESE.

**Procedure (with a Dial Bore Gauge):**

**Step 1:** Measure bore diameter at the **TOP** of the cylinder, in two perpendicular directions:
- $D_{\text{top},A}$ (along crankshaft axis)
- $D_{\text{top},B}$ (perpendicular to crankshaft axis)

**Step 2:** Repeat at the **MIDDLE** and **BOTTOM** of the cylinder, in the same two perpendicular directions:
- $D_{\text{mid},A}$, $D_{\text{mid},B}$
- $D_{\text{bot},A}$, $D_{\text{bot},B}$

**Step 3:** Compute **Ovality** (per plane).

$$\text{Ovality}_{\text{top}} = \left\vert D_{\text{top},A} - D_{\text{top},B} \right\vert$$

$$\text{Ovality}_{\text{mid}} = \left\vert D_{\text{mid},A} - D_{\text{mid},B} \right\vert$$

$$\text{Ovality}_{\text{bot}} = \left\vert D_{\text{bot},A} - D_{\text{bot},B} \right\vert$$

**Step 4:** Compute **Taper** (per direction).

$$\text{Taper}_{A} = \left\vert D_{\text{top},A} - D_{\text{bot},A} \right\vert$$

$$\text{Taper}_{B} = \left\vert D_{\text{top},B} - D_{\text{bot},B} \right\vert$$

**Step 5:** Compare with manufacturer-specified limits (typically **0.02 – 0.05 mm** for petrol, **0.05 – 0.10 mm** for diesel).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **Ovality** and **Taper**. Remember: Ovality is in **one plane (perpendicular directions)**, Taper is **along the axis (top vs bottom)**.

### 3.4 Numerical Example – Brake Disc Runout

> [!NOTE]
> A **direct application** likely to be a 7-mark KTU question.

**Problem:** A brake disc is mounted on a vehicle and rotated. A Dial Test Indicator (DTI) reads:
- Maximum (FIR) = **0.12 mm**
- Minimum = **0.03 mm**
Determine the **Disc Runout**.

**Step 1:** Recall that "runout" is the **Total Indicator Reading (TIR)** — the difference between max and min.

$$\text{Runout} = \text{FRR} - \text{FIR}_{\text{min}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$\text{Runout} = 0.12 - 0.03 = 0.09 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Compare with limit (typically ≤ 0.05 – 0.10 mm for a passenger car disc).

$$\boxed{\text{Disc Runout} = 0.09 \text{ mm — within serviceable limit}}$$

> [!TIP]
> Some textbooks subtract only the FIR (max) reading as the runout. However, the **correct definition** is the **peak-to-valley difference** of the indicator's motion, which is **FRR − min reading**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix – Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| # | Given | To Find | Key Formula | Typical Answer Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Range = 10 mm, Revs = 10, Divs = 100 | LC | $\text{LC} = \frac{R_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{rev}} \cdot N_{\text{div}}}$ | 0.01 mm |
| 2 | Main dial at 67, small dial at 4 | Total reading | $R = (4 \times 1) + (67 \times 0.01)$ | 4.67 mm |
| 3 | Bore readings at 90° apart | Ovality | $\vert D_1 - D_2 \vert$ | 0.01 – 0.05 mm |
| 4 | Top and bottom bore readings | Taper | $\vert D_{\text{top}} - D_{\text{bot}} \vert$ | 0.01 – 0.05 mm |
| 5 | Crankshaft journal readings at multiple sections | Taper | $\vert D_1 - D_2 \vert$ | 0.005 – 0.02 mm |
| 6 | Max and min DTI readings | Runout | $\text{FRR} - \text{FIR}_{\text{min}}$ | 0.05 – 0.20 mm |
| 7 | Inch-based gauge (0.001 in LC) converted to mm | LC in mm | $0.001 \times 25.4$ | 0.0254 mm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Plunger-Type Dial Gauge

The diagram below shows the **functional architecture** mapping the physical interaction chain from the workpiece to the readable pointer deflection.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workpiece Surface] -->|Contact Pressure| B[Plunger Spindle]
    B -->|Linear Motion| C[Rack Gear]
    C -->|Tooth Engagement| D[Pinion Gear Shaft]
    D -->|Rotational Motion| E[Pointer Needle]
    E -->|Deflection Across| F[Graduated Bezel Dial]
    F -->|Visual Reading| G[Operator Eye]
    H[Hair Spring] -->|Backlash Removal| D
    I[Return Spring] -->|Restoring Force| B
    J[Jewelled Bearings] -->|Low Friction Support| D
    K[Magnetic Base Mount] -->|Stable Support| F
```

**Reading the diagram (left → right):**
- Workpiece presses the **plunger (B)**, which moves linearly.
- The **rack (C)** on the plunger rotates the **pinion (D)**.
- The **pointer (E)** on the pinion shaft deflects across the **graduated bezel (F)**.
- The **operator (G)** reads the value visually.
- The **hair spring (H)** keeps the rack in firm contact with the pinion, eliminating backlash.
- The **return spring (I)** ensures the plunger returns to its rest position when removed from the workpiece.
- The **jewelled bearings (J)** provide near-frictionless rotation.
- The **magnetic base mount (K)** holds the gauge rigidly.

### 4.2 Measurement-Setup Architecture (Brake Disc Runout Application)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Setup["Workshop Setup"]
        M[Vehicle Hub with Brake Disc Mounted] -->|Rotated by Hand| R[Full 360 deg Rotation]
        R -->|Probes Surface| DTI[Dial Test Indicator on Magnetic Stand]
    end
    DTI -->|Reads Max| MaxVal[Max FIR 0.12 mm]
    DTI -->|Reads Min| MinVal[Min Reading 0.03 mm]
    MaxVal --> Calc[Runout Calculation]
    MinVal --> Calc
    Calc -->|Subtract| Result[Final Runout 0.09 mm]
    Result --> Compare{Within Limit 0.10 mm?}
    Compare -->|Yes| OK[Disc Acceptable for Service]
    Compare -->|No| NO[Reface or Replace Disc]
```

### 4.3 Reading Interpretation Matrix (Visual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Small["Small Inner Dial Rev Counter"]
        S0[0 mm]
        S1[1 mm]
        S2[2 mm]
        S3[3 mm]
        S4[4 mm]
        S5[5 mm]
        S6[6 mm]
        S7[7 mm]
        S8[8 mm]
        S9[9 mm]
        S10[10 mm]
    end
    subgraph Large["Main Outer Dial 100 Divisions"]
        L0[0.00]
        L25[0.25]
        L50[0.50]
        L75[0.75]
        L99[0.99]
    end
    Small -->|Whole mm| Add[Add Together]
    Large -->|Fractional mm| Add
    Add --> Final[Final Reading in mm]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to draw a dial gauge, examiners award marks for:
> 1. Correct identification of the **plunger, rack, pinion, pointer, bezel, hairspring**.
> 2. Labelling the **graduation direction** (clockwise = positive).
> 3. Showing the **stem at the back** (for mounting).

### 4.4 Comparison Schematic – Plunger vs. Lever (DTI) Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P["Plunger Type Dial Gauge"]
        P1[Linear Plunger Motion] --> P2[Rack and Pinion] --> P3[Pointer Rotates in Plane of Dial]
    end
    subgraph L["Lever Type Dial Test Indicator DTI"]
        L1[Arc Lever Motion] --> L2[Internal Rack and Pinion Reversed] --> L3[Pointer Rotates Perpendicular to Lever]
    end
    P --> App1[Used on Magnetic Stands and Fixtures]
    L --> App2[Used for Runout on Shafts and Discs]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEMUT635]**
> *Define a Dial Gauge and state its least count and range for a standard industrial model.*

**Model Answer (Expected KTU Key Format):**
- A Dial Gauge is a precision measuring instrument that uses a rack-and-pinion mechanism to convert the linear displacement of a spring-loaded plunger into a magnified rotation of a pointer over a graduated dial.
- It is used to measure small linear dimensions, clearances, runout, taper, and ovality.
- **Least Count = 0.01 mm**.
- **Range = 0 to 10 mm** (standard industrial model).
- It works on the principle of **mechanical amplification** (M = R / r).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEMUT635]**
> *List any four applications of a dial gauge in vehicle maintenance.*

**Model Answer:**
1. Measurement of **cylinder bore ovality and taper** using a dial bore gauge.
2. Measurement of **brake disc and flywheel runout** using a Dial Test Indicator.
3. Measurement of **crankshaft journal taper and ovality** using a dial gauge mounted on V-blocks.
4. Measurement of **valve guide clearance** and **valve stem height**.
5. Measurement of **bearing clearances** in the engine and gearbox.
6. Checking **flatness of the cylinder head gasket surface** on a surface plate.

*(Any four — full 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEMUT635]**

### **Question 3A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a plunger-type dial gauge**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A dial gauge has a range of **0–10 mm** and the main dial has **100 graduations** per revolution with **10 full revolutions**. Find the **least count**, **range per revolution**, and the **reading** if the small dial shows **3 mm** and the main dial shows **62 graduations**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for 3A(a):**

**Construction (5 key components):**

1. **Plunger (Spindle):** A spring-loaded contact tip that moves linearly in contact with the workpiece.
2. **Rack:** A straight gear-cut strip attached to the plunger.
3. **Pinion and Pointer Assembly:** A small pinion gear meshing with the rack; the pointer is rigidly mounted on the pinion shaft.
4. **Bezel Dial:** A circular dial with 100 graduated markings.
5. **Hair Spring, Return Spring, Jewelled Bearings, Mounting Stem, and Lugs:** For backlash removal, restoring force, frictionless rotation, and rigid mounting.

**Working:**

- When the plunger contacts the workpiece, any surface variation causes linear movement of the plunger.
- This linear motion is transmitted to the rack, which rotates the pinion.
- The pinion shaft carries the pointer, which deflects across the graduated dial.
- The deflection is **magnified** by the ratio of the dial radius to the pinion pitch radius, allowing readings of **0.01 mm** accuracy.
- The hair spring removes backlash for bi-directional accuracy.
- The return spring pushes the plunger back to its rest position when not in contact.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

---

**Model Solution for 3A(b):**

**Given:**
- Total range: $R_{\text{total}} = 10 \text{ mm}$
- Divisions per revolution: $N_{\text{div}} = 100$
- Number of revolutions: $N_{\text{rev}} = 10$

**Step 1: Least Count**

$$\text{LC} = \frac{R_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{rev}} \times N_{\text{div}}} = \frac{10}{10 \times 100} = 0.01 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Range per Revolution**

$$R_{\text{rev}} = \frac{R_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{rev}}} = \frac{10}{10} = 1.0 \text{ mm per rev}$$

**Step 3: Reading**

$$R = (3 \times 1.0) + (62 \times 0.01) = 3.00 + 0.62 = 3.62 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Least Count = **0.01 mm**
- Range per Revolution = **1.0 mm**
- Reading = **3.62 mm**

---

### **Question 3B (14 Marks) – Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** Explain with a sketch the **construction of a Dial Bore Gauge** and describe the procedure to measure **ovality and taper of an engine cylinder bore**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A brake disc is checked with a Dial Test Indicator. The maximum reading is **0.15 mm** and the minimum reading is **0.04 mm**. Find the **disc runout** and comment on whether the disc is serviceable if the manufacturer's limit is **0.10 mm**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for 3B(a):**

**Construction of a Dial Bore Gauge:**

A Dial Bore Gauge consists of:
1. **Body** containing the dial indicator.
2. **Self-centering bridge** with three contact points — two fixed and one moving.
3. **Moving contact** is connected to the plunger of the dial indicator.
4. **Extension rods** of different lengths to set the gauge for various bore sizes.
5. **Setting ring** — a precision ring used for zero-setting the gauge.

**Procedure for Ovality and Taper Measurement:**

- **Step 1:** Select the extension rod matching the cylinder's nominal diameter.
- **Step 2:** Zero the gauge using the **setting ring**.
- **Step 3:** Insert the gauge into the cylinder bore at the **TOP** position.
- **Step 4:** Rock the gauge gently in the bore to find the **minimum reading** (true diameter at that plane). Record.
- **Step 5:** Rotate the gauge by **90°** in the SAME plane and rock again. Record.
- **Step 6:** Calculate **Ovality** for that plane as $\left\vert D_A - D_B \right\vert$.
- **Step 7:** Repeat Steps 3–6 at the **MIDDLE** and **BOTTOM** of the cylinder.
- **Step 8:** Calculate **Taper** as $\left\vert D_{\text{top}} - D_{\text{bot}} \right\vert$ for each direction.
- **Step 9:** Compare with manufacturer limits.

---

**Model Solution for 3B(b):**

**Given:** Max = 0.15 mm, Min = 0.04 mm, Manufacturer limit = 0.10 mm.

**Step 1:** Runout = Max − Min

$$\text{Runout} = 0.15 - 0.04 = 0.11 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Compare with limit.

$$0.11 \text{ mm} > 0.10 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Exceeds limit}$$

**Step 3:** Conclusion.

$$\boxed{\text{Disc runout of 0.11 mm exceeds the 0.10 mm service limit. The disc must be refaced or replaced.}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Most Commonly Lose Marks on Dial Gauge Questions (KTU Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing Ovality with Taper** — ovality is in **one plane** (90° apart), taper is **along the axis** (top vs bottom). Examiners specifically test this. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the hair spring and its function** in construction questions. Hair spring = **backlash removal**. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Wrong reading direction** — not stating whether the pointer moves **clockwise for positive displacement** or vice versa. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Skipping units** in numerical answers. Always write **mm**, not just "0.01". **Loss: 0.5 marks per question.**
> 5. **Using the wrong formula for runout** — some students use only the **FIR (Full Indicator Reading)** as runout. Correct: **TIR = Max − Min**. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 6. **Not zero-setting** before measurement in procedure questions. Examiners expect this step explicitly. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Dial Gauge in 60 Seconds**

- [x] **Definition:** Precision instrument using rack-and-pinion to convert linear plunger motion to amplified pointer rotation.
- [x] **Standard Least Count:** 0.01 mm (industrial standard).
- [x] **Standard Range:** 0 – 10 mm.
- [x] **Mechanical Magnification:** $M = R / r$ (typically 100–1000).
- [x] **Main Components:** Plunger, Rack, Pinion, Pointer, Bezel, Hair Spring, Jewelled Bearings, Stem.
- [x] **Hair Spring Function:** Removes backlash between rack and pinion — essential for bi-directional accuracy.
- [x] **Small Inner Dial:** Whole millimetres.
- [x] **Main Outer Dial:** Fractional mm (each division = LC).
- [x] **Reading Rule:** Final reading = (small dial × 1) + (main dial × LC).
- [x] **Three Main Types:** Plunger type, Lever type (DTI), Dial Bore Gauge.
- [x] **Ovality Formula:** $\left\vert D_1 - D_2 \right\vert$ (same plane, 90° apart).
- [x] **Taper Formula:** $\left\vert D_{\text{top}} - D_{\text{bot}} \right\vert$ (along axis).
- [x] **Runout Formula:** TIR = Max − Min (NOT just FIR).
- [x] **Critical Workshop Caution:** Always **zero-set** on a calibrated standard (surface plate, setting ring, slip gauge) before measurement.
- [x] **Unit Constant:** 1 inch = 25.4 mm (for unit-conversion questions).
- [x] **Mounting:** Use a **magnetic base** or **V-block fixture** — never hold the gauge by hand during measurement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plug Gauge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plug Gauge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Plug Gauge** is a fixed-limit, precision inspecting instrument (a type of *internal limit gauge*) used in engineering metrology and vehicle maintenance workshops to verify whether a machined internal feature—principally cylindrical **holes, bores, bushings, and bearing housings**—falls within its prescribed dimensional tolerance limits. It is manufactured as a hardened, ground, and lapped cylindrical steel pin (or a set of pins), and it does not provide any numerical reading; it only indicates whether the component is **"GO"** (acceptable) or **"NO-GO"** (rejected).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Plug gauges are designed strictly according to **Taylor's Principle of "GO" and "NO-GO" Gauging**, which states that gauges must check individual limit dimensions separately, never simultaneously. This is the single most important concept examiners test from this topic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **bouncer at a cinema entrance** with two differently-sized wristbands:

- The **GREEN (GO) wristband** is *just tight enough* — it must slide over your wrist easily. If it does not go on, the venue is too small (hole is undersized — reject).
- The The **RED (NO-GO) wristband** is *a little tighter*. If it manages to slide on, the venue is too large (hole is oversized — reject).

The plug gauge performs exactly this "wristband test" — but in reverse for an internal feature. The hole must be **large enough** to admit the GO pin, but **small enough** to reject the NO-GO pin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standards Used:**
> - **Wear Allowance (W)** — the extra material intentionally removed from the **NO-GO side only**, because the NO-GO member is the one that experiences repeated sliding contact during shop-floor inspection.
> - **Gauge Tolerance (h)** — a small permitted variation on the gauge itself, typically about **10%** of the workpiece tolerance.
> - Standard reference: **IS 3455 / ISO 1938-1** (Indian / ISO standards for plain limit gauges).

### 1.3 Vehicle Maintenance Context

In vehicle maintenance, plug gauges are routinely used to inspect:
- **Cylinder bore diameter** of engine blocks
- **Piston pin (gudgeon pin) bush** internal diameter
- **Valve guide** bore
- **Wheel bearing housing** inner races
- **Clutch release bearing** seat

This makes plug gauges an indispensable tool in a KTU mechanical / automobile workshop module.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Standard Double-Ended Plug Gauge

A plug gauge consists of two precision cylindrical members fixed coaxially on a single handle:

1. **GO Member** — The *longer* end with **full diameter**. It represents the **upper limit (MMC)** of the hole. It must slide freely through the hole under its own weight (no force).
2. **NO-GO Member** — The *shorter* end (typically less than 60% of the GO length), representing the **lower limit (LMC)** of the hole, **reduced by the wear allowance**. It must NOT enter the hole.
3. **Handle** — A knurled/ribbed aluminium or bakelite grip in the middle, isolated from the gauging members by a relief neck (chamfered recess) to prevent heat transfer from the operator's hand, which would distort precision dimensions.

### 2.2 Taylor's Principle — The 'Why' Behind the Design

Taylor's principle governs the design of all GO/NO-GO gauges:

- **GO gauge must check the MMC** (most material condition) — i.e., the smallest permissible hole. So **GO diameter = Upper Limit of Hole (ULH)**.
- **NO-GO gauge must check the LMC** (least material condition) — i.e., the largest permissible hole. So **NO-GO diameter = Lower Limit of Hole (LLH) − Wear Allowance**.
- Each limit is verified **separately** (the two members are on opposite ends), never combined.
- The GO member is made **long** (gives good contact, mimics a fastener's fit), while the NO-GO is made **short** to prevent wear from extensive sliding contact.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Design Logic

Let nominal hole size be **H** with total tolerance **T** (workpiece tolerance).

- **Upper Limit of Hole (ULH)** = $H + \dfrac{T}{2}$
- **Lower Limit of Hole (LLH)** = $H - \dfrac{T}{2}$

Plug gauge dimensions (with gauge tolerance $h$ and wear allowance $W$):

- **GO plug size** = ULH $\pm \dfrac{h}{2}$
- **NO-GO plug size** = (LLH $- W$) $\pm \dfrac{h}{2}$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready reference** for plug-gauge numerical problems. Memorize every row — KTU examiners frequently test 7-mark problems directly from these equations.

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Upper Limit of Hole | ULH | $H + T/2$ | Represents MMC side |
| Lower Limit of Hole | LLH | $H - T/2$ | Represents LMC side |
| GO Plug Diameter | $D_{GO}$ | ULH $\pm h/2$ | Always full size at nominal |
| NO-GO Plug Diameter | $D_{NOGO}$ | (LLH $- W$) $\pm h/2$ | Always smaller than LLH |
| Gauge Tolerance (approx) | $h$ | $\approx 10\%$ of $T$ | ISO empirical rule |
| Wear Allowance (approx) | $W$ | $\approx 5\%$ to $10\%$ of $T$ | Applied only to NO-GO |
| Plug Gauge Acceptance Rule | — | GO enters, NO-GO rejects | Taylor's Principle |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-throughput inspection** — One quick check replaces a micrometer reading, ideal for shop-floor line inspectors.
- **Operator-skill independent** — A semi-skilled technician can use a plug gauge reliably, unlike a bore-dial indicator.
- **Fits the NEP-2020 vision** of hands-on, experiential learning — KTU 2024 scheme emphasises workshop-based limit gauging in vehicle maintenance labs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relation Between Gauge Tolerance and Workpiece Tolerance

The gauge tolerance $h$ is derived from the workpiece tolerance $T$ using the **10% rule of ISO 1938-1**:

$$
h \approx 0.10 \times T
$$

**Reasoning:** The gauge must be **at least 10 times more accurate** than the workpiece it checks. If the workpiece tolerance is $T$, the gauge itself can vary only by a small fraction so that measurement uncertainty stays below **10% of $T$** (the conventional Gage R&R acceptance threshold).

Therefore, expanding:

$$
h = 0.10 \times T \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{h}{2} = 0.05 \times T
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Wear Allowance as a Function of Tolerance

The NO-GO member is the one that wears out the fastest because it is the side that repeatedly contacts the workpiece during limit-checking. Engineers add a safety margin $W$ so that a freshly-manufactured NO-GO gauge, when brand-new, will *not* erroneously enter a hole that is at its **largest acceptable** size.

Empirical relationship:

$$
W \approx 0.05 \times T \;\;\text{to}\;\; 0.10 \times T
$$

For KTU problems, the most commonly adopted value is:

$$
W = 0.05 \times T
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (Model KTU Style)

**Problem:** Design a plug gauge for a hole of nominal diameter **$\mathbf{50}$ mm** with tolerance grade **H7**.

Given:
- Nominal size $H = 50$ mm
- From standard tables, **H7 tolerance** $T = 25\ \mu m = 0.025$ mm
- Gauge tolerance $h = 10\% \text{ of } T = 0.0025$ mm
- Wear allowance $W = 5\% \text{ of } T = 0.00125$ mm

**Step 1 — Find the hole limits:**

$$
\text{ULH} = H + \frac{T}{2} = 50 + \frac{0.025}{2} = 50.0125 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{LLH} = H - \frac{T}{2} = 50 - \frac{0.025}{2} = 49.9875 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Find the GO plug diameter:**

$$
D_{GO} = \text{ULH} \pm \frac{h}{2} = 50.0125 \pm \frac{0.0025}{2} = 50.0125 \pm 0.00125
$$

$$
\Rightarrow D_{GO} \in \left[50.01125,\ 50.01375\right] \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Find the NO-GO plug diameter:**

$$
D_{NOGO} = (\text{LLH} - W) \pm \frac{h}{2} = (49.9875 - 0.00125) \pm 0.00125
$$

$$
= 49.98625 \pm 0.00125
$$

$$
\Rightarrow D_{NOGO} \in \left[49.98500,\ 49.98750\right] \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification with Taylor's Principle:**

- If GO ($D_{GO,\max} = 50.01375$) does **not** enter the hole, hole diameter $> 50.01375$? No — hole diameter $< 50.01375$, meaning **GO failed → hole is undersized → REJECT**. ✓
- If NO-GO ($D_{NOGO,\max} = 49.9875$) **enters** the hole, hole diameter $> 49.9875$? Yes — meaning **NO-GO entered → hole is oversized → REJECT**. ✓
- Accept the part only when GO enters **AND** NO-GO does not enter. ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Workshop Inspection

| **Condition** | **GO enters?** | **NO-GO enters?** | **Decision** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Yes | No | **ACCEPT** (within tolerance) |
| 2 | No | No | REJECT — Undersized hole |
| 3 | Yes | Yes | REJECT — Oversized hole |
| 4 | No | Yes | **Impossible** (geometric violation — re-measure) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Plug Gauge Inspection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["WORKPIECE HOLE\nNominal Dia H\nTolerance T"] --> B{"INSPECTOR PICKS UP\nPLUG GAUGE"}
    B --> C["INSERT GO END\nLong pin, full dia"]
    C --> D{"GO enters\nhole under\nits own weight?"}
    D -- NO --> E["REJECT\nHole undersized\nSmaller than MMC"]
    D -- YES --> F["GO CHECK PASSED\nHole >= ULH"]
    F --> G["INSERT NOGO END\nShort pin, dia minus wear"]
    G --> H{"NOGO enters\nhole?"}
    H -- YES --> I["REJECT\nHole oversized\nLarger than LMC"]
    H -- NO --> J["ACCEPT\nHole within ULH to LLH\nBoth limits satisfied"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Component Anatomy of a Double-Ended Plug Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PLG["DOUBLE ENDED PLUG GAUGE ASSEMBLY"]
        H1["GO MEMBER\nLong cylindrical pin\nDia equal to ULH\nLapped surface"] --> H2["RELIEF NECK\nChamfered recess\nPrevents heat transfer\nfrom handle"]
        H2 --> H3["HANDLE\nKnurled aluminium grip\nInsulated grip zone"]
        H3 --> H4["RELIEF NECK\nSame as above"]
        H4 --> H5["NO GO MEMBER\nShort cylindrical pin\nDia equal to LLH minus W\nLapped surface"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Engineering Schematic (Text Representation)

```
                GO end                                   NO-GO end
         (Long, full dia)                          (Short, dia minus W)
         ◄────────────►                              ◄──────►
       ┌───────────────┐ ┐ ┌──────────────────┐ ┌────┐ ┐
       │███████████████│ │ │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓HANDLE▓▓▓▓│ │████│ │
       │███████████████│ │ │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│ │████│ │
       └───────────────┘ │ └──────────────────┘ └────┘ ┘
        ↑ relief neck                                   ↑ relief neck
        ↑               ↑                                ↑
   Long gauging       Heat insulation                Short gauging
   surface            from hand                      surface (avoids wear)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Visual Cue for Exams:** Always draw the **GO side as longer** than the NO-GO side, with **both separated by a handle**. The knurled middle handle is mandatory in KTU labelled diagrams. Marks are often lost when students draw a single, undifferentiated pin.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a plug gauge and state the principle on which it is designed.

**Model Answer:**
A plug gauge is a fixed-limit inspecting instrument used to verify whether an internal cylindrical feature (hole) lies within its prescribed tolerance. It is designed on **Taylor's Principle of "GO" and "NO-GO" gauging**, which states that the **upper and lower limits** of the hole must be checked *separately* by two distinct gauge members — the GO member (representing the upper limit) and the NO-GO member (representing the lower limit minus wear allowance). **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Why is the GO member of a plug gauge made longer than the NO-GO member?

**Model Answer:**
The GO member is made **long** to provide maximum contact with the hole, ensuring accurate verification of the upper limit (MMC). The NO-GO member is kept **short** to **minimise wear** caused by repeated sliding contact during inspection, since the NO-GO side is the one that physically rubs against the workpiece each time a part is checked. A long NO-GO would wear rapidly and lose its calibrated size. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **omit the wear allowance** while calculating the NO-GO dimension, losing 2 marks immediately.
> - Students often write **GO = LLH** (instead of ULH) — this is the most common conceptual error. Memorise: GO = *upper* limit (max dia the hole can accept); NO-GO = *lower* limit (min dia still allowed).
> - **Always state Taylor's Principle by name** in 7-mark answers. Examiners award partial credit specifically for naming the principle.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** A hole of nominal diameter **40 mm** is to be manufactured with tolerance grade **H8**.

Given: Tolerance $T_{H8} = 39\ \mu m = 0.039$ mm. Take gauge tolerance $h = 10\%$ of $T$ and wear allowance $W = 5\%$ of $T$.

### (a) Explain Taylor's Principle and apply it to design the plug gauge dimensions. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State Taylor's Principle (2 marks):**
Taylor's Principle states that the **GO** gauge must check the **upper limit (MMC)** and the **NO-GO** gauge must check the **lower limit (LMC)** of the hole. Both checks must be performed by **separate members**, never combined into a single gauge contact.

**Step 2 — Identify the hole limits (2 marks):**

$$
\text{ULH} = 40 + \frac{0.039}{2} = 40.0195 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{LLH} = 40 - \frac{0.039}{2} = 39.9805 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute gauge tolerance and wear allowance (1 mark):**

$$
h = 0.10 \times 0.039 = 0.0039 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
W = 0.05 \times 0.039 = 0.00195 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply to plug gauge dimensions (2 marks):**

$$
D_{GO} = 40.0195 \pm \frac{0.0039}{2} = 40.0195 \pm 0.00195 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
D_{NOGO} = (39.9805 - 0.00195) \pm 0.00195 = 39.97855 \pm 0.00195 \text{ mm}
$$

### (b) Sketch a labelled diagram of a double-ended plug gauge and list any four applications in vehicle maintenance. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Labelled Sketch (3 marks):**

```
    GO end                        NO-GO end
  (longer pin)                    (shorter pin)
    │                               │
    ▼                               ▼
  ┌──────┐ ┐ ┌────────────┐ ┌──┐ ┐
  │██████│ │ │ HANDLE     │ │██│ │
  │██████│ │ │ (knurled)  │ │██│ │
  └──────┘ │ └────────────┘ └──┘ ┘
   relief     relief neck       relief
   neck                          neck
   ↑              ↑                ↑
 chamfer        grip            chamfer
```

**Labelled parts (1 mark):** GO member, NO-GO member, knurled handle, relief necks, lapped surfaces.

**Four applications in vehicle maintenance (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. Checking **engine cylinder bore** diameter for rebore decisions.
2. Inspecting **piston gudgeon pin bush** inner diameter.
3. Verifying **valve guide** internal diameter after reaming.
4. Checking **wheel hub bearing seat** bore during overhaul.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** Discuss the **types of plug gauges** and the **concept of wear allowance** in their design.

### (a) Classify plug gauges and explain any three types with neat sketches. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Classification (2 marks):**
Plug gauges are classified based on **function** and **geometry** into:

1. **Plain cylindrical plug gauge** (single or double ended) — for plain round holes.
2. **Taper plug gauge** — for checking tapered bores (e.g., Morse tapers in tailstocks).
3. **Thread plug gauge** — for checking internal threads (separate "GO" and "LO" plug forms).
4. **Ring plug gauge** — for checking external cylindrical dimensions (used in pairs with the plug gauge family concept).

**Explanation of three types (3 marks — 1 each):**

- **Plain plug gauge:** A simple cylindrical pin with GO and NO-GO ends. Used for plain holes such as cylinder bores.
- **Thread plug gauge:** Has threaded GO and NO-GO members that mate with the internal thread, checking pitch diameter and thread form.
- **Taper plug gauge:** A precisely tapered pin for verifying the angle and fit of tapered seats.

**Neat labelled sketch (2 marks):**
Three labelled sketches must accompany the description.

### (b) What is wear allowance? Why is it applied only on the NO-GO side and not on the GO side? Calculate the wear allowance for a hole of $\mathbf{30^{+0.03}_{0}}$ mm. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of wear allowance (2 marks):**
**Wear allowance** is the small amount of material intentionally removed from the **NO-GO** dimension of a plug gauge during manufacture, so that the gauge has an extended useful life even after the NO-GO surface begins to wear from repeated shop-floor use.

**Why only on NO-GO side (3 marks):**

- The **NO-GO** member is the one that physically *contacts* the workpiece during every inspection. Its diameter wears down over time.
- The **GO** member is supposed to *slide freely* through an acceptable hole — its diameter does not undergo abrasive sliding contact in the same way; instead, it is the GO member that *defines* the upper limit, and any wear on it would **falsely accept** undersized holes (a dangerous Type-II error).
- Therefore, GO is manufactured **exactly at ULH ± gauge tolerance** (no wear allowance), while NO-GO is manufactured at **LLH − W ± gauge tolerance**.

**Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $H = 30$ mm, $T = 0.03$ mm

$$
W = 5\% \text{ of } T = 0.05 \times 0.03 = 0.0015 \text{ mm} = 1.5\ \mu m
$$

$$
\boxed{W = 0.0015 \text{ mm}}
$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorise Before Exam:**

- **Plug gauge** = fixed-limit internal gauge with **GO** (full size, long) and **NO-GO** (short, reduced by wear) members.
- **Taylor's Principle** = GO checks **upper limit**; NO-GO checks **lower limit**; checks are **separate**, not simultaneous.
- **GO diameter** = ULH $\pm h/2$ — **no wear allowance** applied here.
- **NO-GO diameter** = (LLH $- W$) $\pm h/2$ — **wear allowance applied here only**.
- **Wear allowance** applied only on NO-GO side because NO-GO is the wearing surface during inspection.
- **Gauge tolerance** $h \approx 10\%$ of workpiece tolerance $T$.
- **Wear allowance** $W \approx 5\%$ of workpiece tolerance $T$ (KTU convention).
- **Acceptance rule:** GO must enter freely; NO-GO must **not** enter.
- **Vehicle applications:** cylinder bores, valve guides, piston-pin bushes, bearing seats, clutch hubs.
- **Common exam pitfall:** confusing GO with lower limit and NO-GO with upper limit. Always remember **"GO = Greater / Upper"** and **"NO-GO = Smaller / Lower"**.
- **Drawing rule:** Always show the **handle in the middle**, with the **GO side longer** than the **NO-GO side**, and **relief necks** separating them from the handle.
- **Standards reference:** IS 3455 / ISO 1938-1 govern the design and tolerance of plain plug gauges.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feeler Gauge
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Feeler Gauge

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Technical Definition

A **Feeler Gauge** (also known as a *Thickness Gauge* or *Leaf Gauge*) is a precision measuring instrument consisting of a set of thin, flat steel leaves (blades) of known, accurately calibrated thicknesses, used to measure the **clearance** (gap) or **play** between two mating surfaces that are in close physical proximity to each other. In the context of Vehicle Maintenance and Troubleshooting, it serves as the primary direct-reading tool for verifying small, critical mechanical tolerances defined by the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A feeler gauge is defined as a hand-held precision tool comprising a series of calibrated steel strips (leaves) used to determine gap widths and clearances in mechanical assemblies where direct measurement with calipers or micrometers is physically impossible or impractical.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine trying to measure the thickness of a single sheet of paper in a closed book without opening it. You cannot slide a ruler in, and even if you could, the gap is far too narrow. Now, imagine you have a collection of paper slips of *exactly* known thicknesses — say, slips that are 0.05 mm, 0.10 mm, 0.15 mm, and so on. You can slide these slips into the gap one by one. The slip that fits snugly (with a slight drag) tells you the gap size.

This is precisely what a **Feeler Gauge** does inside an engine:

- **The book** → An engine component (e.g., rocker arm and valve stem).
- **The thin slips of known thickness** → The calibrated steel leaves of the feeler gauge.
- **The snug fit with slight drag** → The correct measurement reading.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard feeler gauge set used in workshops typically ranges from **0.02 mm to 1.00 mm** in increments of 0.01 mm or 0.02 mm, with the imperial equivalent ranging from **0.001 inch to 0.025 inch**.

### 1.3 Physical Standards & Tolerances

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| Material of blades | High-carbon, hardened, and tempered spring steel (typically AISI 1095) |
| Blade length (standard) | 75 mm to 300 mm (workshop use) |
| Blade width | 6 mm to 13 mm |
| Thickness range (Metric) | **0.02 mm to 1.00 mm** |
| Thickness range (Imperial) | **0.001 inch to 0.040 inch** |
| Accuracy class | ± 0.002 mm to ± 0.005 mm per blade |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle (The "Why" and "How")

The feeler gauge operates on the **comparative thickness principle** — a tactile and visual feedback method rather than a calibrated reading mechanism.

**Step 1 — Blade Selection:** The technician inserts progressively thicker blades into the gap until one blade slides in with a uniform, light drag.

**Step 2 — Tactile Feedback:** The correct blade is identified by the "feel" — it should move smoothly with mild resistance, indicating an exact match between blade thickness and gap width.

**Step 3 — Reading:** The thickness value is read directly from the etched/stamped marking on the blade itself.

**Step 4 — Validation:** For high-precision measurements (e.g., valve clearance on a DOHC engine), two blades may be stacked ("piggy-backed") to achieve intermediate values not present in a single blade.

### 2.2 Construction Anatomy

- **Holder/Housing:** A pivoted brass or steel case that retains the blades when not in use and allows fan-out for individual selection.
- **Locking Screw:** Found on premium sets; holds the selected blade(s) in rigid position.
- **Blades (Leaves):** Tapered at the tip for easy insertion; each blade is individually marked with its nominal thickness.
- **Pivot Pin:** Allows the blades to rotate (fan) from the holder.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Relationship | Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Total clearance by stacked blades | $C = t_1 + t_2$ | Used for non-standard intermediate gap sizes |
| Conversion: Imperial to Metric | $1 \text{ inch} = 25.4 \text{ mm}$ | Cross-referencing manufacturer specs |
| Conversion: Metric to Imperial (thousandths) | $0.025 \text{ mm} = 0.001 \text{ inch (1 thou)}$ | Common in valve clearance specs |
| Comparison method error bound | $\Delta C \leq 2 \cdot \Delta t_{\text{blade}}$ | Stacking doubles error (uncertainty) |
| Drag force estimation (sliding fit) | $F_d \leq \mu \cdot N$ | Tactile feedback calibration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Rule:** When two blades are stacked, the measurement uncertainty **doubles** because each blade contributes its own tolerance. Hence, single-blade measurement is always preferred when the exact size is available.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In an automotive workshop, the feeler gauge is indispensable for:

- **Valve clearance adjustment** in overhead valve (OHV) and overhead camshaft (OHC) engines.
- **Spark plug gap setting** (typically 0.7 mm – 1.1 mm).
- **Ignition contact breaker point gap** measurement (vintage systems).
- **Piston ring end-gap measurement** inside the cylinder bore.
- **Clutch adjustment** and **brake shoe-to-drum clearance** verification.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Metric-to-Imperial Conversion (Single Blade)

A workshop manual specifies an **ignition contact point gap of 0.020 inch**, but the technician only possesses a metric feeler gauge. We must determine the equivalent metric thickness and select the correct blade.

**Given:**
$$G_{\text{imperial}} = 0.020 \text{ inch}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the inch-to-millimetre conversion factor:**
$$1 \text{ inch} = 25.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply the desired gap by the conversion factor:**
$$\begin{aligned}
G_{\text{metric}} &= G_{\text{imperial}} \times 25.4 \text{ mm/inch} \\
&= 0.020 \times 25.4 \\
&= 0.508 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Select the closest available blade:**

Standard metric feeler gauge sets contain blades in increments of 0.01 mm, so the closest available sizes are **0.50 mm** and **0.51 mm**.

$$\text{Closest match: } 0.50 \text{ mm (deviation } -0.008 \text{ mm)}$$

$$\text{Next closest match: } 0.51 \text{ mm (deviation } +0.002 \text{ mm)}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

The **0.51 mm blade** is selected, as it provides a deviation of only **+0.002 mm**, well within the typical OEM tolerance of ±0.01 mm.

### 3.2 Derivation: Stacked Blade Clearance Calculation

A DOHC engine specification calls for a valve clearance of **0.35 mm**. The workshop set has no 0.35 mm blade, only 0.30 mm and 0.05 mm blades. Determine the equivalent clearance measurement.

**Step 1 — Identify the required intermediate gap:**
$$C_{\text{required}} = 0.35 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Select two blades that sum to the required value:**
$$t_1 = 0.30 \text{ mm}, \quad t_2 = 0.05 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the additive stacking principle:**
$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{stacked}} &= t_1 + t_2 \\
&= 0.30 + 0.05 \\
&= 0.35 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the resulting measurement uncertainty:**

If each blade has a tolerance of $\pm\,0.002$ mm:
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_{\text{stacked}} &= \Delta t_1 + \Delta t_2 \\
&= (\pm\,0.002) + (\pm\,0.002) \\
&= \pm\,0.004 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Compared with a hypothetical single-blade tolerance of $\pm\,0.002$ mm, the error is **doubled**. This is acceptable as long as the OEM tolerance window exceeds ±0.004 mm.

### 3.3 Derivation: Piston Ring End-Gap Calculation

The piston ring is inserted into a cylinder bore of nominal diameter $D = 80.00$ mm. The OEM specification requires an end-gap of $G = 0.25 \text{ mm to } 0.50 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 — Position the ring squarely in the bore** using the piston skirt to push it down to mid-stroke depth (to avoid taper distortion).

**Step 2 — Insert blades progressively:**

$$\begin{aligned}
0.20 \text{ mm blade: fits loosely, slides freely} \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Too thin} \\
0.30 \text{ mm blade: drags slightly} \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Correct measurement} \\
0.40 \text{ mm blade: cannot enter without force} \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Too thick}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Reading:**

The end-gap is recorded as **0.30 mm**, which lies within the OEM window of 0.25 mm to 0.50 mm. ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical Feeler Gauge Applications

| Application | Typical Clearance Range | OEM Tolerance | Recommended Blade Increment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spark plug gap (petrol) | 0.6 mm – 1.1 mm | ± 0.05 mm | 0.05 mm |
| Valve clearance (OHV petrol) | 0.15 mm – 0.30 mm (intake) | ± 0.025 mm | 0.025 mm |
| Valve clearance (OHC diesel) | 0.20 mm – 0.50 mm | ± 0.025 mm | 0.025 mm |
| Ignition contact gap | 0.40 mm – 0.50 mm | ± 0.025 mm | 0.025 mm |
| Piston ring end-gap (small engine) | 0.15 mm – 0.40 mm | ± 0.05 mm | 0.05 mm |
| Clutch pedal free-play | 0.5 mm – 1.0 mm | ± 0.1 mm | 0.05 mm |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Feeler Gauge Set — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["USER MECHANICAL"] --> nodeB["WORKSHOP TASK"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["GAP IDENTIFICATION"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["BLADE SELECTION"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"THICKNESS MATCH?"}
    nodeE -->|YES| nodeF["READ MEASUREMENT"]
    nodeE -->|NO| nodeG["STACK BLADES"]
    nodeG --> nodeE
    nodeF --> nodeH["VALIDATE AGAINST OEM SPEC"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"WITHIN TOLERANCE?"}
    nodeI -->|YES| nodeJ["ACCEPT COMPONENT"]
    nodeI -->|NO| nodeK["ADJUST OR REJECT"]
```

### 4.2 Internal Construction of a Feeler Gauge Set

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1["PIVOT PIN"] --> p2["HOLDER BODY"]
    p2 --> p3["BLADE 1 0.02 MM"]
    p2 --> p4["BLADE 2 0.03 MM"]
    p2 --> p5["BLADE 3 0.05 MM"]
    p2 --> p6["BLADE N 1.00 MM"]
    p3 --> p7["LOCKING SCREW"]
    p4 --> p7
    p5 --> p7
    p6 --> p7
```

### 4.3 Measurement Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sA["CLEAN CONTACT SURFACES"] --> sB["SELECT APPROXIMATE BLADE"]
    sB --> sC["INSERT BLADE INTO GAP"]
    sC --> sD{"SLIDES FREELY?"}
    sD -->|YES| sE["TRY THICKER BLADE"]
    sE --> sC
    sD -->|NO| sF{"CANNOT ENTER?"}
    sF -->|YES| sG["TRY THINNER BLADE"]
    sG --> sC
    sF -->|NO| sH["LIGHT DRAG MATCH FOUND"]
    sH --> sI["RECORD MEASUREMENT"]
    sI --> sJ["CROSS CHECK WITH OEM SPEC"]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
*Define a feeler gauge. List any two automotive applications where it is used.*

**Model Answer:**
A feeler gauge is a hand-held precision measuring tool consisting of a set of calibrated steel strips (blades) of known thicknesses, used to measure small clearances or gap widths between two mating parts in mechanical assemblies.

**Two automotive applications:**
1. **Valve clearance measurement** between the rocker arm and the valve stem tip in OHV/OHC engines.
2. **Spark plug gap setting** by measuring the distance between the centre and ground electrodes.

*(Acceptable alternatives: ignition contact point gap, piston ring end-gap, clutch pedal free-play.)* **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the typical thickness range of blades available in a standard metric feeler gauge set.*

**Model Answer:**
A standard metric feeler gauge set contains blades ranging from **0.02 mm to 1.00 mm**, typically in increments of 0.01 mm or 0.02 mm. Some premium sets extend up to 1.50 mm or 2.00 mm for specialised industrial applications. The imperial equivalent ranges from 0.001 inch to 0.040 inch (1 thou to 40 thou). **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Methodology and Application Focused*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model — Part B Module 1 Choice]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working principle of a feeler gauge used in vehicle maintenance. Discuss the material properties required for the blades and justify why hardened spring steel is preferred. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Describe the step-by-step procedure to measure the **valve clearance** of a four-cylinder OHV petrol engine using a feeler gauge. State the typical OEM-specified clearance value and the consequences of incorrect clearance. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working Principle (7 marks):**

A feeler gauge consists of the following parts:

1. **Holder/Housing (1 mark):** A brass or steel case containing a pivot pin, allowing the blades to be fanned out for selection and folded back for storage.

2. **Blades/Leaves (2 marks):** A set of flat, thin, rectangular strips of hardened spring steel. Each blade is precision-ground to a specific thickness and is etched/stamped with its nominal value at the tip. The blades are tapered at one end to facilitate insertion into narrow gaps.

3. **Locking Mechanism (1 mark):** A thumb-screw on premium sets that locks the selected blade rigidly in position.

**Material Justification (2 marks):**
- **Hardness (HRC 58–62):** Resists wear and abrasion during repeated insertion.
- **Spring temper:** Allows the blade to flex without permanent deformation when sliding into curved gaps.
- **Dimensional stability:** Maintains calibrated thickness over the tool's lifetime.
- **Corrosion resistance:** Allows storage in humid workshop environments.

**Working Principle (1 mark):**
The blade whose thickness matches the gap width will slide in with a mild, uniform drag. This tactile indication provides the measurement reading directly from the etched value.

---

**(b) Valve Clearance Measurement Procedure (7 marks):**

**Tools & Specs:** Feeler gauge set (0.05 mm – 1.00 mm); service manual specifying OEM clearance (typical: **0.15 mm–0.20 mm intake, 0.25 mm–0.30 mm exhaust** for a warm OHV petrol engine).

**Step-by-Step Procedure (5 marks):**

1. Bring engine to top dead centre (TDC) on compression stroke for cylinder No. 1. The cam lobe for that cylinder's valve must point **upward** (base circle contacting the lifter).
2. Clean the contact area between rocker arm tip and valve stem top using a clean rag.
3. Select a feeler blade of nominal OEM thickness and insert it **parallel** to the rocker arm length, between the rocker arm tip and the valve stem.
4. The blade should slide with a **light, uniform drag** — not loose, not binding.
5. If loose, try progressively thicker blades; if it cannot enter, try thinner blades.
6. For exhaust valves (typically higher clearance), repeat the same procedure.
7. If adjustment is needed, loosen the lock nut on the rocker arm adjuster, rotate the adjuster screw until correct feel is achieved, then re-tighten the lock nut **while holding the adjuster stationary**.
8. Re-verify the clearance after tightening — it may shift slightly.
9. Rotate the crankshaft by 90° (¼ turn) and repeat for the remaining cylinders following the **firing order**.

**Consequences of Incorrect Clearance (2 marks):**
- **Too tight (insufficient clearance):** Valve does not fully close → compression loss → burnt valve seat → engine damage.
- **Too loose (excessive clearance):** Tapping noise → increased cam/lifter wear → inaccurate valve timing → power loss.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Conversion and Comparison Focused*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Part B Module 1 Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of an example, explain how you would convert an imperial feeler gauge specification into its metric equivalent for setting the **ignition contact breaker point gap** of a vintage vehicle. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **stacked-blade measurement** using a feeler gauge. Derive the relationship for measurement uncertainty when two blades of individual tolerance $\pm\,0.002$ mm are stacked. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Imperial-to-Metric Conversion (7 marks):**

**Background (1 mark):** Vintage British and American vehicles (e.g., 1960s Morris, Ford Cortina Mk1) often specify contact breaker point gaps in **thousandths of an inch (thou)**.

**Worked Example (5 marks):**

**Given:** Required point gap $G = 0.020$ inch.

**Step 1:** Convert using $1 \text{ inch} = 25.4 \text{ mm}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
G_{\text{mm}} &= 0.020 \times 25.4 \\
&= 0.508 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Identify available metric blade (standard set): The closest are **0.50 mm** and **0.51 mm**.

**Step 3:** Selection by minimum deviation:
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{0.50} &= \vert\, 0.508 - 0.50 \,\vert = 0.008 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta_{0.51} &= \vert\, 0.508 - 0.51 \,\vert = 0.002 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Select the **0.51 mm blade** (smaller deviation).

**Step 5:** Verify by insertion — open the contact set, insert blade between points, gap should equal blade thickness.

**Practical Note (1 mark):** If neither 0.50 nor 0.51 mm is available, a stacked combination of 0.30 mm + 0.20 mm = 0.50 mm may be used (knowing the doubled uncertainty).

---

**(b) Stacked-Blade Measurement (7 marks):**

**Concept (3 marks):**
When the required intermediate gap value is not present as a single blade in the set, two or more blades may be **stacked (piggy-backed)** on top of each other and inserted as a single unit. The resulting measurement equals the arithmetic sum of individual blade thicknesses.

**Derivation of Uncertainty (4 marks):**

Let the two blades have true thicknesses $t_1$ and $t_2$ with individual uncertainties $\Delta t_1$ and $\Delta t_2$.

The combined clearance:
$$C = t_1 + t_2$$

By the principle of **uncertainty propagation for additive quantities**:
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= \frac{\partial C}{\partial t_1} \Delta t_1 + \frac{\partial C}{\partial t_2} \Delta t_2 \\
&= (1) \cdot \Delta t_1 + (1) \cdot \Delta t_2 \\
&= \Delta t_1 + \Delta t_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Substituting values:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_1 &= \pm\,0.002 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta t_2 &= \pm\,0.002 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta C &= \pm\,0.002 + \pm\,0.002 \\
&= \pm\,0.004 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The stacked measurement has **twice** the uncertainty of a single-blade measurement. Hence, stacking is justified only when the OEM tolerance band exceeds $\pm\,0.004$ mm. For tighter specs (e.g., fuel injection shim settings), a single blade is mandatory.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT state the range as 0.01 mm – 1.00 mm.** The lower limit is **0.02 mm** for standard sets. Students commonly lose 1 mark here.
> 2. **Always mention the material** (hardened spring steel) when describing construction — examiners allocate marks for material science linkage.
> 3. **For stacked blades, write out the uncertainty derivation** explicitly; simply stating "error doubles" without the propagation equation earns only partial credit.
> 4. **Valve clearance procedure must include the re-verification step after lock-nut tightening** — most students forget this and lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Conversion factor is exactly 25.4 mm/inch**, not 25 mm or 2.54 cm. Precision matters.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A feeler gauge is a set of calibrated steel leaves used to measure small clearances.
- **Standard metric range:** **0.02 mm to 1.00 mm** in 0.01 mm or 0.02 mm increments.
- **Standard imperial range:** **0.001 inch to 0.040 inch** (1 thou to 40 thou).
- **Material:** Hardened and tempered spring steel (AISI 1095 typical); HRC 58–62.
- **Working principle:** Match-by-drag (tactile feedback); light uniform drag = correct blade.
- **Key automotive applications:** Valve clearance, spark plug gap, ignition contact gap, piston ring end-gap, clutch free-play.
- **Conversion factor (must memorise):** $1 \text{ inch} = 25.4 \text{ mm}$.
- **Stacking rule:** Clearance = sum of individual blade thicknesses; **uncertainty also adds**.
- **OEM valve clearance (typical petrol OHV):** Intake **0.15–0.20 mm**, Exhaust **0.25–0.30 mm**.
- **OEM spark plug gap (typical petrol):** **0.7–1.1 mm** depending on ignition system.
- **Always re-verify** clearance after tightening any lock nut — metal-on-metal seating shifts the reading.
- **Preferred over micrometers** for inaccessible gaps where a fixed anvil/spindle cannot reach.
- **Limitation:** Not suitable for high-speed rotating gaps or surfaces with burrs/oxidation that distort the drag feel.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine Service, Repair and Overhauling: Basic Engine Parts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Engine Service, Repair and Overhauling: Basic Engine Parts ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Engine Service, Repair, and Overhauling — Formal Definition In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT635)**, **Engine Service, Repair, and Overhauling** refers to the systematic set of maintenance procedures performed on an internal combustion engine to restore its original performance, reliability, and emission characteristics. These procedures are graded on a maintenance severity pyramid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone)** > - **Engine Service (Routine Maintenance):** Scheduled preventive tasks such as oil change, filter replacement, spark plug cleaning, valve clearance adjustment, and timing inspection. Performed at manufacturer-specified intervals (e.g., every **5,000 km** or **6 months**). > - **Engine Repair:** Corrective maintenance targeted at a specific failed component (e.g., replacing a burnt valve, fixing a water pump, or rewiring a sensor). The engine is *not* fully dismantled. > - **Engine Overhauling (Major Repair):** Complete disassembly of the engine down to the bare block. All worn parts are inspected, measured, reconditioned (re-bored, re-ground, or re-machined), or replaced. The engine is reassembled to "like-new" or "zero-hour" condition. ### 1.2 Basic Engine Parts — Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy: "The Engine is a Living Organ"** > Think of the engine as the *heart* of a vehicle. Just like the human heart pumps blood, the engine converts chemical energy (fuel) into mechanical energy (rotation). The **cylinder block** is the *skeleton*, the **pistons** are the *muscles*, the **crankshaft** is the *spine* that converts up-down motion to rotation, and the **valves** are the *lips* that let air-fuel in and exhaust out. The **lubrication system** is the bloodstream, and the **cooling system** is the sweat that prevents overheating. Service keeps it healthy; repair fixes a sickness; overhauling is a complete organ transplant-level restoration. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$ - **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) range for healthy engines:** **$220 \text{ g/kWh} - 280 \text{ g/kWh}$** - **Standard compression ratio for petrol (SI) engines:** **8:1 to 12:1** - **Standard compression ratio for diesel (CI) engines:** **14:1 to 22:1** - **Standard firing order for 4-cylinder inline engines:** 1-3-4-2 or 1-2-4-3 - **Normal operating coolant temperature:** **$85^{\circ}\text{C} - 95^{\circ}\text{C}$** - **Normal engine oil pressure at idle (warm):** **$1.0 \text{ bar} - 2.5 \text{ bar}$** (varies by engine) ### 1.4 Classification of Basic Engine Parts (KTU Module 1 Framework) | S.No | Sub-System | Constituent Parts | Primary Function | |------|------------|-------------------|------------------| | 1 | **Stationary Parts** | Cylinder block, Cylinder head, Crankcase, Manifolds | Form the structural housing; contain combustion | | 2 | **Reciprocating Parts** | Piston, Piston rings, Wrist pin (Gudgeon pin), Connecting rod | Convert fuel energy to linear motion | | 3 | **Rotating Parts** | Crankshaft, Camshaft, Flywheel, Balancer shaft, Damper pulley | Convert reciprocating motion to rotation; store energy | | 4 | **Valvetrain Parts** | Intake valve, Exhaust valve, Valve springs, Rocker arms, Push rods, Tappets, Cam lobes | Time the opening/closing of intake & exhaust ports | | 5 | **Auxiliary Parts** | Gaskets, Seals, Bearings (main & big-end), Timing belt/chain, Tensioner | Seal, support, synchronize, and protect components | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Engine Maintenance Pyramid (Why Service is Graded) Engine maintenance is *not* a single action. It is a layered discipline, ordered by intervention depth: 1. **Level 1 — Daily/Weekly Checks:** Engine oil level, coolant level, visual leak inspection, belt tension. 2. **Level 2 — Routine Service (Minor):** Oil + filter change, air filter cleaning, spark plug check (every **5,000 - 10,000 km**). 3. **Level 3 — Intermediate Service:** Timing belt inspection, valve clearance adjustment, PCV valve, fuel filter (every **20,000 - 40,000 km**). 4. **Level 4 — Major Service:** Coolant flush, brake fluid change, comprehensive inspection (every **60,000 km+**). 5. **Level 5 — Repair:** Component-specific replacement. 6. **Level 6 — Overhauling:** Full engine teardown, machining, reassembly. > [!TIP] > **The "Why" Behind Grading:** Each level exists because of the engineering trade-off between *vehicle downtime*, *cost to owner*, and *risk of component failure*. The KTU examiner expects students to recognize that overhauling is the *last* resort, not the first. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Engine Nomenclature & Geometry) The following formulas are **mandatory** for KTU Part B derivations. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application | |---|--------------|----------|-------------------|-------------| | 1 | **Compression Ratio (CR)** | $CR = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ | $V_s$ = swept volume, $V_c$ = clearance volume | Determines SI vs CI engine classification | | 2 | **Swept Volume (per cylinder)** | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L$ | $D$ = bore diameter, $L$ = stroke length (m) | Engine displacement calculation | | 3 | **Total Displacement Volume** | $V_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L \cdot N_{cyl}$ | $N_{cyl}$ = number of cylinders | Total engine capacity (cc/litre) | | 4 | **Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP)** | $IMEP = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_d}$ | $W_{net}$ = net work per cycle (J), $V_d$ in m³ | Engine performance indicator | | 5 | **Engine Power (Brake)** | $P_b = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | $N$ = rpm, $T$ = torque (N·m) | Output shaft power | | 6 | **Indicated Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{m_f \cdot CV}$ | $m_f$ = fuel mass, $CV$ = calorific value (J/kg) | Combustion quality check during service | | 7 | **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{Actual air mass}}{\text{Theoretical air mass}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Indicates valve/port health | | 8 | **Mechanical Efficiency** | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$ | $BP$ = brake power, $IP$ = indicated power | Indicates friction losses in bearings/piston rings | | 9 | **Piston Speed (Mean)** | $\bar{V}_p = \dfrac{2 L N}{60}$ | $L$ in m, $N$ in rpm | Limiting factor for high-speed engines | | 10 | **Firing Frequency (4-stroke)** | $f_{fire} = \dfrac{N}{120} \cdot N_{cyl}$ | $N$ in rpm | Determines vibration & smoothness | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Substitution Note for Tables:** All set notation, absolute values, and conditional bars in the equations above are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking the markdown table grid during KTU PDF conversion. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compression Ratio (CR)** is the single most important parameter that determines whether an engine is petrol (SI) or diesel (CI). When a KTU examiner asks "Why is CR of SI engine lower than CI engine?", the answer is **knocking (auto-ignition) resistance**. Petrol has a low octane number, so high CR causes uncontrolled detonation. Diesel relies on *controlled* auto-ignition. - **IMEP** is used by service engineers to detect a *loss of compression*. A drop in IMEP with the same fuel input indicates worn piston rings, burnt valves, or head gasket leakage. - **$\eta_{mech}$** is the key parameter for overhauling decisions. If $\eta_{mech}$ falls below **$\sim 0.85$** for a worn engine, overhauling is economically justified. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Total Engine Displacement from Bore, Stroke, and Number of Cylinders This is a **standard KTU Part B derivation** worth 7 marks. **Given:** - Bore diameter of cylinder, $D$ (in metres) - Stroke length of piston, $L$ (in metres) - Number of cylinders, $N_{cyl}$ **To Prove / Find:** Total displacement volume, $V_d$ (in m³ or cc) **Step 1 — Geometric understanding:** Each cylinder is a hollow circular bore. The piston travels a distance equal to the stroke length. The volume swept by the piston in *one* cylinder is the volume of a cylinder of diameter $D$ and height $L$. $$ V_s = \text{Area} \times \text{Height} $$ **Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of cylinder:** The cross-section is a circle. The area of a circle is $\pi r^2$, where $r$ is the radius. Since $D = 2r$, we substitute: $$ A = \pi r^2 = \pi \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} $$ **Step 3 — Volume swept per cylinder:** Multiplying the cross-sectional area by the stroke length $L$: $$ V_s = A \times L = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times L $$ **Step 4 — Total displacement for $N_{cyl}$ cylinders:** All cylinders share the *same* bore and stroke (in standard inline engines), so total displacement is simply $N_{cyl}$ times the swept volume of one cylinder: $$ V_d = N_{cyl} \times V_s = N_{cyl} \times \dfrac{\pi D^2 L}{4} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{V_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L \cdot N_{cyl}} $$ **Conversion to cubic centimetres (cc):** Since $1 \text{ m}^3 = 10^6 \text{ cc}$, multiply the m³ value by $10^6$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Compression Ratio for a 4-Stroke Engine **Given:** Swept volume $V_s$ and clearance volume $V_c$. **Step 1 — Total volume when piston is at BDC (Bottom Dead Centre):** This is the *maximum* volume in the cylinder. It is the sum of swept volume and clearance volume: $$ V_{BDC} = V_s + V_c $$ **Step 2 — Minimum volume when piston is at TDC (Top Dead Centre):** This is *only* the clearance volume, since the piston has compressed all the swept volume out: $$ V_{TDC} = V_c $$ **Step 3 — Definition of compression ratio:** Compression ratio is the ratio of the *maximum* volume to the *minimum* volume: $$ CR = \dfrac{V_{BDC}}{V_{TDC}} = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{CR = 1 + \dfrac{V_s}{V_c} = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students frequently write $CR = V_s / V_c$ which is wrong by a factor of 1. The correct form always includes the $+V_c$ in the numerator. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Engine Parts and Their Failure Modes (Service Reference) This matrix is a **high-yield KTU topic**. The examiner can ask: *"List 6 basic engine parts and their common failure modes during operation."* | S.No | Engine Part | Material of Construction | Common Failure Mode | Service/Repair Action | |------|-------------|--------------------------|---------------------|------------------------| | 1 | **Cylinder Block** | Cast iron / Aluminium alloy | Cracking, bore wear, ovality | Re-bore, sleeve insertion, welding | | 2 | **Cylinder Head** | Aluminium alloy | Warping, valve seat recession, cracking | Surface grinding, valve seat cutting | | 3 | **Piston** | Al-Si alloy (eutectic) | Crown carbon deposits, skirt wear, ring groove wear | Decarbonizing, replacement | | 4 | **Piston Rings** | Cast iron, chrome-plated | Wear, breakage, loss of tension | Replacement (always new on overhaul) | | 5 | **Connecting Rod** | Forged steel / forged aluminium | Bending, big-end bore ovality, bolt stretch | Replacement, big-end resizing | | 6 | **Crankshaft** | Forged steel / cast iron | Journal wear, cracking, deflection | Re-grinding to undersize, replacement | | 7 | **Camshaft** | Chilled cast iron / forged steel | Lobes wear, journal wear, cam nose pitting | Replacement (re-grinding uneconomical) | | 8 | **Valves (I/E)** | Heat-resistant steel (intake) / Stellite (exhaust) | Burnt head, stem wear, seat pitting | Refacing, seat cutting, replacement | | 9 | **Valve Springs** | Spring steel | Loss of tension, fatigue breakage | Replacement (mandatory on overhaul) | | 10 | **Timing Belt/Chain** | Reinforced rubber / steel chain | Tooth skip, elongation, breakage | Replacement at prescribed interval | | 11 | **Main & Big-End Bearings** | Steel-backed babbitt / aluminium-tin | Spalling, fatigue, copper exposure | Replacement (always new on overhaul) | | 12 | **Gaskets (Head Gasket)** | Multi-layer steel (MLS) / composite | Blow-by, coolant-oil mixing | Replacement (mandatory on overhaul) | | 13 | **Flywheel** | Cast iron | Ring gear wear, friction surface scoring | Ring gear replacement, re-facing | | 14 | **Tappets / Lifters** | Chilled cast iron | Flattened face, jamming | Replacement or cleaning | | 15 | **Rocker Arms** | Forged steel | Pivot wear, pad wear | Replacement | | 16 | **Push Rods** | Steel tube with hardened ends | Bending, tip wear | Straightening or replacement | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** Students write "re-grind" for parts that *cannot* be re-ground (e.g., aluminium pistons, polymer timing belts, valve springs). The examiner deducts 1 mark for every such incorrect recommendation. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (For KTU 7-Mark Problems) **Question:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of **80 mm**, a stroke of **90 mm**, and a compression ratio of **9:1**. Calculate (a) the total displacement in cc, and (b) the clearance volume per cylinder in cc. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert dimensions to metres:** $$ D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m}, \quad L = 90 \text{ mm} = 0.090 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate swept volume per cylinder:** $$ V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.080)^2 \times 0.090 $$ $$ V_s = 0.7854 \times 0.0064 \times 0.090 $$ $$ V_s = 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \text{ per cylinder} $$ **Step 3 — Total displacement for 4 cylinders:** $$ V_d = 4 \times V_s = 4 \times 4.524 \times 10^{-4} = 1.8096 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 4 — Convert to cc (multiply by $10^6$):** $$ V_d = 1.8096 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^6 = 1809.6 \text{ cc} \approx 1.81 \text{ L} $$ **Step 5 — Find clearance volume using CR formula:** $$ CR = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} \implies 9 = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} $$ $$ 9 V_c = V_s + V_c \implies 8 V_c = V_s $$ $$ V_c = \dfrac{V_s}{8} = \dfrac{4.524 \times 10^{-4}}{8} = 5.655 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 = 56.55 \text{ cc} $$ **Final Answers:** - (a) Total displacement ≈ **1809.6 cc** (or **1.81 L**) - (b) Clearance volume per cylinder ≈ **56.55 cc** ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 4-Stroke SI Engine The Mermaid diagram below maps the functional interactions of the basic engine parts — specifically how the **stationary, reciprocating, rotating, and valvetrain subsystems** work together to convert fuel into rotary motion. This block-level architecture is the KTU-recommended way to represent the engine when a physical cross-section drawing is not feasible. ```mermaid graph TD subgraph STN[STATIONARY SUBSYSTEM] A1[Cylinder Block
Cast Iron or Al Alloy] A2[Cylinder Head
Al Alloy] A3[Intake Manifold] A4[Exhaust Manifold] A5[Crankcase Lower Half
Oil Sump] end subgraph REC[RECIPROCATING SUBSYSTEM] B1[Piston
Al-Si Alloy] B2[Piston Rings
Top Compression, Oil Control] B3[Gudgeon Pin
Wrist Pin] B4[Connecting Rod
Forged Steel] end subgraph ROT[ROTATING SUBSYSTEM] C1[Crankshaft
Forged Steel] C2[Flywheel
Cast Iron] C3[Damper Pulley
Rubber-Steel] end subgraph VAL[VALVETRAIN SUBSYSTEM] D1[Camshaft
Chilled Cast Iron] D2[Tappet or Lifter] D3[Push Rod] D4[Rocker Arm] D5[Intake Valve] D6[Exhaust Valve] D7[Valve Spring] end subgraph AUX[AUXILIARY SUBSYSTEM] E1[Timing Chain or Belt] E2[Tensioner] E3[Main Bearings] E4[Big End Bearing] E5[Head Gasket MLS] end A1 --> B1 B1 --> B3 B3 --> B4 B4 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C1 --> C3 A2 --> D5 A2 --> D6 D7 --> D5 D7 --> D6 D4 --> D5 D4 --> D6 D3 --> D4 D2 --> D3 D1 --> D2 E1 --> D1 E1 --> C1 E3 --> C1 E4 --> B4 E5 --> A2 A3 --> D5 D6 --> A4 ``` --- ### 4.2 Engine Service Decision Flowchart (Block Diagram) This flowchart captures the *logic* a service engineer follows when an engine is brought for maintenance — mapping the decision tree from inspection to overhauling. ```mermaid graph TD S0[Engine Brought for Service] --> S1{Visual Inspection
Leaks, Smoke, Noise} S1 --> S2[Oil and Coolant Level Check] S2 --> S3{Compression Test
and Leak Down Test} S3 --> S4{Compression Loss
Greater Than 20 Percent} S4 --> S5[No Major Fault
Routine Service Only] S4 --> S6[Suspect Rings Valves
or Head Gasket] S6 --> S7{Remove Engine
Required} S7 --> S8[Repair at Vehicle
Top Side Work] S7 --> S9[Engine Removal
Partial Teardown] S9 --> S10{Bore Wear
Greater Than 0.10 mm} S10 --> S11[Component Level
Repair] S10 --> S12[Full Engine
Overhauling] S12 --> S13[Rebore Regrind
New Bearings and Gaskets] S13 --> S14[Engine Reassembly
and Dyno Test] ``` > [!TIP] > **How to Read These Diagrams in KTU Exams:** The KTU examiner awards 1-2 marks for a *labelled, logical* block diagram. Always include arrows indicating flow direction, use rectangles for processes, and diamonds for decisions. Do not waste time drawing a perfect physical cross-section in a 14-mark question — the block diagram above is academically equivalent and faster. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define the term "Engine Overhauling". How is it different from routine engine service?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Engine overhauling is the process of complete disassembly of an engine, followed by inspection, measurement, reconditioning (re-boring, re-grinding, or re-machining), and replacement of worn parts with new or re-manufactured components, finally reassembling the engine to its original "zero-hour" specification. (2 marks) **Difference from routine service:** | Aspect | Routine Service | Overhauling | |--------|----------------|-------------| | Disassembly | None / minor | Complete teardown | | Cost | Low | High (3-5x) | | Frequency | Every 5,000 - 10,000 km | Every 100,000 - 150,000 km | | Parts replaced | Filters, oil, plugs | Bearings, rings, gaskets, seals | (1 mark for the comparative distinction) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *List any six basic parts of an internal combustion engine and state the function of each in one line.* **Model Answer (6 items × 0.5 mark = 3 marks):** 1. **Cylinder Block** — Houses the cylinders and supports the crankshaft and head. 2. **Piston** — Receives combustion pressure and transmits force to the connecting rod. 3. **Connecting Rod** — Links the piston to the crankshaft, converting linear to rotary motion. 4. **Crankshaft** — Converts reciprocating motion of the piston into rotary motion. 5. **Camshaft** — Operates the intake and exhaust valves at correct timing. 6. **Flywheel** — Stores rotational energy and smooths out power delivery between firing strokes. 7. **Valve Springs** *(optional)* — Close the valves when cam lobe rotates away. --- ### Part B Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 1, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine. List any **8** basic engine parts with their functions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke engine has a bore of **85 mm** and a stroke of **100 mm**. The clearance volume per cylinder is **70 cc**. Calculate: (i) Swept volume per cylinder, (ii) Compression ratio, (iii) Total displacement in litres. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:** **Construction (3 marks):** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine consists of: - A **cylinder block** (cast iron / aluminium) with 4 parallel bores. - A **cylinder head** mounted on top, containing intake and exhaust ports, valves, spark plug, and camshaft housing. - **Pistons** fitted in each bore, connected via **gudgeon pin** to the **connecting rods**. - The lower end of the connecting rod is bolted to the **crankshaft** through big-end bearings. - A **crankcase** (lower half of block) holds the crankshaft in **main bearings**. - A **camshaft** (overhead or side) drives the **valves** through tappets, push rods, and rocker arms. - A **flywheel** is bolted to the rear end of the crankshaft. - **Intake and exhaust manifolds** are bolted to the head. **Working — 4 strokes (3 marks):** 1. **Intake Stroke:** Piston moves TDC → BDC. Intake valve opens. Air-fuel mixture is drawn in. 2. **Compression Stroke:** Piston moves BDC → TDC. Both valves close. Mixture is compressed (~9:1). 3. **Power Stroke:** Spark plug fires near TDC. Combustion raises pressure to ~30-40 bar. Piston is pushed to BDC, producing work. 4. **Exhaust Stroke:** Piston moves BDC → TDC. Exhaust valve opens. Spent gases are pushed out. **8 Basic Parts with Functions (1 mark — tabulated):** | Part | Function | |------|----------| | Cylinder Block | Structural housing for cylinders | | Cylinder Head | Seals the top; houses valves and spark plug | | Piston | Receives combustion force | | Connecting Rod | Transmits force from piston to crank | | Crankshaft | Converts reciprocating to rotary motion | | Camshaft | Times the valve operation | | Valves (I/E) | Control gas exchange | | Flywheel | Stores energy, smooths rotation | --- **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** - $N_{cyl} = 4$, Bore $D = 85 \text{ mm} = 0.085 \text{ m}$, Stroke $L = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m}$, $V_c = 70 \text{ cc} = 70 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$ **Step (i) — Swept volume per cylinder:** $$ V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.085)^2 \times 0.100 $$ $$ V_s = 0.7854 \times 0.007225 \times 0.100 = 5.674 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 567.4 \text{ cc} $$ **Step (ii) — Compression ratio:** $$ CR = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = \dfrac{567.4 + 70}{70} = \dfrac{637.4}{70} = 9.105 $$ $$ \boxed{CR \approx 9.1 : 1} $$ **Step (iii) — Total displacement:** $$ V_d = N_{cyl} \times V_s = 4 \times 567.4 = 2269.6 \text{ cc} \approx 2.27 \text{ L} $$ $$ \boxed{V_d \approx 2.27 \text{ L}} $$ --- ### Part B Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Differentiate between **engine service, engine repair, and engine overhauling** with suitable examples. State **when** an engine should be overhauled. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **functional architecture** of a 4-stroke SI engine. List the **stationary, reciprocating, and rotating** parts separately. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:** **Service (2 marks):** Scheduled preventive maintenance done at fixed intervals. Example: Oil + filter change every 5,000 km, spark plug cleaning every 10,000 km. No dismantling of major assemblies. **Repair (2 marks):** Corrective maintenance to fix a *specific* failed component. Example: Replacing a leaking water pump, a burnt ignition coil, a cracked manifold. Partial disassembly only. **Overhauling (2 marks):** Complete engine teardown and rebuild. Example: Re-boring worn cylinders, regrinding the crankshaft, replacing piston rings, main bearings, and gaskets.
