| Off-Road / Construction | Diesel | Every 5,000 km | Every 15,000 km | Every 6 months | N/A |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Fuel System PM Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Arrives at Workshop] --> B[Receive Vehicle and Document Complaints]
    B --> C[Visual Inspection of Fuel System]
    C --> D{Any Leakage Detected?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Mark Leak Points and Estimate Repair]
    D -- No --> F[Check Fuel Quality Sample]
    F --> G{Water or Sediment Present?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Drain Fuel Tank and Flush]
    G -- No --> I[Replace Inline Fuel Filter]
    I --> J[Test Fuel Pump Pressure]
    J --> K{Pressure Within Spec?}
    K -- No --> L[Replace Fuel Pump]
    K -- Yes --> M[Clean Injectors Ultrasonically]
    M --> N[Perform Injector Spray Pattern Test]
    N --> O{Spray Pattern OK?}
    O -- No --> P[Replace Faulty Injectors]
    O -- Yes --> Q[Reassemble System and Bleed Air]
    Q --> R[Road Test for 10 km]
    R --> S[Final Documentation and Handover]
    L --> S
    P --> S
    H --> S
    E --> S
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Component-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StorageSubsystem [Storage Subsystem]
        TANK[Fuel Tank]
        BREATH[Fuel Tank Breather Valve]
    end
    subgraph DeliverySubsystem [Delivery Subsystem]
        PUMP[Fuel Pump]
        LINE[High Pressure Fuel Line]
        FILTER[Inline Fuel Filter]
    end
    subgraph MeteringSubsystem [Metering Subsystem]
        RAIL[Fuel Rail]
        INJ[Mpfi Injector]
        THROTTLE[Throttle Body]
    end
    subgraph CombustionSubsystem [Combustion Subsystem]
        MANIFOLD[Intake Manifold]
        ENGINE[Engine Cylinder]
    end
    TANK --> PUMP
    PUMP --> FILTER
    FILTER --> LINE
    LINE --> RAIL
    RAIL --> INJ
    INJ --> MANIFOLD
    MANIFOLD --> ENGINE
    BREATHER --> TANK
    THROTTLE --> MANIFOLD
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: PM Decision Tree for Injector Service

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Injector Removed from Engine] --> S1[Visual Inspection of Nozzle Tip]
    S1 --> S2{Carbon Deposits Visible?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Ultrasonic Cleaning for 15 Minutes]
    S2 -- No --> S4[Static Flow Rate Test]
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5{Flow Rate Within Plus Minus 5 Percent?}
    S5 -- No --> S6[Replace Injector]
    S5 -- Yes --> S7[Spray Pattern Test at 3 Bar]
    S7 --> S8{Pattern Symmetrical?}
    S8 -- No --> S6
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Injector Approved for Reuse]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** The first diagram represents the **macro PM workflow** as expected in KTU 14-mark questions. The second diagram represents the **functional architecture** of an MPFI system. The third diagram represents the **decision logic** for injector refurbishment.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define preventive maintenance of a fuel system. List any four fuel system components that require scheduled PM.*

**Model Answer:**
Preventive maintenance of a fuel system is the scheduled execution of cleaning, inspection, and replacement tasks performed at manufacturer-recommended intervals to ensure the fuel delivery system operates at peak efficiency and prevents unscheduled breakdowns.

Four components requiring scheduled PM are:
1. Fuel filter
2. Fuel injector
3. Fuel pump
4. Fuel tank (sediment and water removal)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for petrol and diesel engines. What is meant by the lambda ($\lambda$) value?*

**Model Answer:**
The stoichiometric air-fuel ratio is **14.7:1** for petrol and **14.5:1** for diesel engines.

The lambda ($\lambda$) value is the ratio of the actual air-fuel ratio to the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio. It is a dimensionless number that indicates whether the mixture is rich ($\lambda < 1$), stoichiometric ($\lambda = 1$), or lean ($\lambda > 1$).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI) system in a petrol engine. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The MPFI system delivers precisely metered fuel into each cylinder's intake port via individual injectors. The functional architecture consists of the following sub-blocks:

1. **Fuel Tank:** Stores fuel; equipped with a submersible electric fuel pump and an in-tank strainer.
2. **Fuel Pump:** Pressurizes fuel to typically **2.5 – 4.0 bar** depending on system design.
3. **Fuel Filter:** Removes particulates > **10 µm** with efficiency > **95%**.
4. **Fuel Rail:** Distributes pressurized fuel uniformly to all injectors.
5. **Fuel Pressure Regulator:** Maintains constant rail pressure by returning excess fuel to the tank.
6. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU):** Computes injection duration based on MAP, MAF, coolant temperature, and oxygen sensor inputs.
7. **Injectors:** Electromagnetic solenoid valves that atomize fuel into a fine cone spray.
8. **Throttle Body:** Controls airflow mass entering the manifold.

**(b)** Describe the step-by-step preventive maintenance procedure for the fuel injectors of an MPFI engine. Mention the tools used and quality acceptance criteria. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Removal:** Disconnect battery, depressurize fuel line by removing fuel pump relay and cranking engine. Disconnect injector electrical connectors and fuel rail.

**Step 2 — Visual Inspection:** Check nozzle tip for carbon deposits, cracks, or damaged O-rings.

**Step 3 — Ultrasonic Cleaning:** Place injectors in an ultrasonic cleaner with a specialized cleaning solution for **15 minutes** at **50 °C** to dissolve varnish and carbon.

**Step 4 — Back-flushing:** Reverse-flow the injector to dislodge stubborn deposits using the cleaning machine.

**Step 5 — Static Flow Rate Test:** Actuate the injector at a fixed pulse width; collect fuel in a graduated cylinder for 60 seconds. Acceptance: flow rate within **±5%** of rated value (typically **120 – 250 mL/min**).

**Step 6 — Spray Pattern Test:** Inject fuel against a calibrated pattern card at **3 bar**. Acceptance: symmetrical conical pattern with no dribble.

**Step 7 — Leak-Down Test:** Hold pressure and observe for any drip in 60 seconds. Acceptance: zero drops.

**Tools Used:** Injector puller, ultrasonic cleaner, flow bench, O-ring kit, torque wrench.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the components of a diesel fuel system and describe the preventive maintenance schedule for each component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Diesel fuel system components and their PM schedule:

1. **Fuel Tank and Sediment Bowl:** Drain water and sediment every **10,000 km**; clean tank every **2 years**.
2. **Lift Pump:** Check delivery pressure (typically **0.3 – 0.5 bar**) and replace diaphragm every **40,000 km**.
3. **Primary Fuel Filter (Water Separator):** Drain collected water daily in heavy vehicles; replace element every **15,000 km**.
4. **Secondary Fuel Filter:** Replace every **15,000 – 20,000 km** depending on dust conditions.
5. **Injection Pump (Inline / Rotary / Common Rail):** Inspect timing marks every **50,000 km**; full recalibration by authorized service center every **100,000 km**.
6. **Injectors:** Test spray pattern and opening pressure every **20,000 km**; ultrasonic clean every **30,000 km**; replacement at **100,000 km** for heavy commercial vehicles.
7. **Fuel Lines:** Inspect for cracks and leaks every service interval.

**(b)** A fuel filter is tested with the following data:
- Contaminant count at inlet: $N_{\text{in}} = 5000$ particles/mL
- Contaminant count at outlet: $N_{\text{out}} = 150$ particles/mL

Calculate the filter efficiency. Comment on whether the filter is acceptable per KTU / industry standards. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$N_{\text{in}} = 5000$ particles/mL
$N_{\text{out}} = 150$ particles/mL

**Formula:**

$$
\eta_f = \dfrac{N_{\text{in}} - N_{\text{out}}}{N_{\text{in}}} \times 100
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
\eta_f = \dfrac{5000 - 150}{5000} \times 100
$$

**Computation:**

$$
\eta_f = \dfrac{4850}{5000} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_f = 0.97 \times 100 = 97\,\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The filter efficiency is **97%**, which exceeds the industry acceptance threshold of **95%** for particles > **10 µm**. The filter is **acceptable for continued service**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the multiplication by 100** when computing filter efficiency (common 1-mark loss).
> 2. **Mixing up SI and CI stoichiometric ratios** (14.7 vs 14.5) — students often write 14.7 for diesel, which is incorrect.
> 3. **Skipping the conclusion/comment** in numerical problems — KTU examiners allot **1 mark** explicitly for the qualitative conclusion.
> 4. **Not drawing the functional block diagram** in 7-mark questions — diagrams carry a minimum of **2 marks**.
> 5. **Writing "every 5000 km" without specifying the vehicle category** — PM intervals are vehicle-dependent.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** is **proactive**, not reactive — schedule it before failure occurs.
- **Filter Efficiency Formula:** $\eta_f = \dfrac{N_{\text{in}} - N_{\text{out}}}{N_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ — efficiency must exceed **95%**.
- **Stoichiometric A/F:** Petrol = **14.7**, Diesel = **14.5**.
- **Lambda ($\lambda$) Interpretation:** $<1$ rich, $=1$ stoichiometric, $>1$ lean.
- **Fuel Filter PM:** Replace every **10,000 – 20,000 km** depending on vehicle class.
- **Injector Cleaning:** Ultrasonic bath at **50 °C** for **15 minutes**; flow rate within **±5%**.
- **Vapor Lock:** Occurs when fuel line negative pressure exceeds **−40 kPa**.
- **Diesel Bug:** Microbial growth in biodiesel; treat with biocide every **6 months**.
- **Safety First:** Petrol flash point is **−43 °C** — no naked flames near fuel system work.
- **Common Tools:** Pressure gauge, flow bench, ultrasonic cleaner, O-ring kit, torque wrench.
- **BS-VI PM Intervals:** Strictly every **10,000 km or 6 months** for passenger vehicles.
- **Documentation:** Always record PM in service logbook for warranty compliance.
- **KTU Favourite Topics:** Filter efficiency numerical, A/F ratio derivation, MPFI block diagram, injector PM steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Symptoms, Possible Faults, Repair and Replacement Procedures of Components in Fuel System
Common Services to be Carried Out
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Common Services to be Carried Out ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Common Services to be Carried Out** in automotive maintenance refers to the **standardized set of scheduled inspection, replacement, lubrication, adjustment, and cleaning operations** performed on a vehicle at manufacturer-specified intervals to ensure **operational reliability, passenger safety, emission compliance, and optimal performance** throughout the vehicle's service life. These services form the backbone of the **Periodic Maintenance Schedule (PMS)** prescribed by vehicle Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) and are governed by service intervals expressed in **kilometers (km), running hours, or calendar months**, whichever occurs first. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Under the 2024 scheme, students must demonstrate competence in identifying, sequencing, and justifying every common service procedure, including the tools/equipment used, safety precautions, and service intervals. Expect direct questions worth **3 to 14 marks** in End Semester Examinations (ESE). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of your vehicle as a human body going for a routine health check-up.** Just as a doctor checks your blood pressure, cholesterol, eyes, and heart at regular intervals — **even when you "feel fine"** — your car needs a periodic health check-up regardless of whether it shows visible symptoms. **Skipping an oil change is like skipping brushing your teeth for a year** — the damage accumulates silently and becomes catastrophically expensive. Common services are the **preventive medicine** of the automotive world, designed to catch wear, contamination, and degradation **before** they cascade into major component failure. ### 1.3 Classification of Common Services Common services are broadly classified into **six functional families** as per KTU module framework: | S.No | Service Family | Primary Purpose | Typical Interval (Petrol Car) | |------|---------------|-----------------|-------------------------------| | 1 | **Engine Lubrication Services** | Reduce friction, cool, clean, seal | Every **5,000 – 10,000 km** | | 2 | **Filtration Services** | Remove contaminants from fluids & air | Every **10,000 – 20,000 km** | | 3 | **Ignition System Services** | Ensure complete combustion | Every **20,000 – 40,000 km** | | 4 | **Brake System Services** | Maintain stopping performance | Every **10,000 – 30,000 km** | | 5 | **Cooling System Services** | Prevent overheating & corrosion | Every **40,000 – 60,000 km** | | 6 | **Wheel & Tyre Services** | Ensure traction & handling | Every **10,000 km** | > [!NOTE] > **Industry Standard Metric:** Most modern passenger vehicles follow a **"1-3-5-7" rule** — service at 1 month, 3 months, 5,000 km, and every 7,500 km thereafter. Commercial vehicles follow stricter schedules due to high-load operation, typically at **10,000 km or 3 months**, whichever is earlier. ### 1.4 Service Classification by Trigger Type > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners frequently test the **distinction between time-based and mileage-based services** — this is a high-yield 3-mark question. - **Time-Based Services:** Independent of vehicle usage (e.g., coolant replacement every **2 years** even if low mileage) - **Mileage-Based Services:** Triggered by odometer reading (e.g., engine oil every **10,000 km**) - **Condition-Based Services:** Triggered by diagnostic readings (e.g., brake pad replacement when thickness < **2 mm**) - **Predictive Services:** Based on sensor data & telematics (modern connected vehicles) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Engine Lubrication Service (Oil Change) The single most critical common service. Engine oil degrades through three mechanisms: 1. **Thermal Oxidation** — heat breaks down molecular chains 2. **Contamination** — soot, metal particles, combustion byproducts 3. **Additive Depletion** — detergents, anti-wear agents get used up **Standard Procedure (7 Steps):** 1. Warm engine to operating temperature (oil flows better) 2. Position vehicle on **lift or ramp** (safe working height: **1.0 – 1.5 m**) 3. Place **drain pan** (capacity **8 – 12 litres**) beneath oil pan 4. Remove **drain plug** (torque to spec, typically **25 – 35 Nm**) 5. Allow complete drainage (**minimum 10 minutes**) 6. Replace **oil filter** (torque: hand-tight + **3/4 turn**) 7. Refill with **specified grade** oil (e.g., **5W-30, 10W-40**) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always mention the **SAE Viscosity Grade** and **API/ACEA Performance Standard** when answering oil-related questions. Example answer: *"Use SAE 5W-30 API SN+ grade engine oil, capacity 4.2 litres with filter."* ### 2.2 Filtration Services Three primary filters are serviced routinely: **A. Air Filter Service** - Inspection interval: every **5,000 km**; replacement: **20,000 – 40,000 km** - Cleaning method: low-pressure compressed air (**max 5 bar**) blown **opposite to airflow direction** - Visual indicator: hold against light — if light does not pass, replace **B. Oil Filter Service** - Always replaced **with every oil change** (never reused) - Modern spin-on type; non-return valve prevents drain-back **C. Fuel Filter Service** - Petrol engines: replace every **30,000 – 60,000 km** - Diesel engines: replace every **15,000 – 30,000 km** (water separator also drained) > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often say "replace air filter every oil change." This is incorrect — the air filter has a **4x longer life** than engine oil in normal conditions. ### 2.3 Ignition System Service (Petrol Engines) Includes **spark plug service** and **ignition coil inspection**: - **Spark Plug Inspection** every **20,000 km**; replacement every **40,000 – 100,000 km** (depending on electrode type: copper, platinum, iridium) - **Gap measurement** using **feeler gauge** or **wire-type gap tool**: - Standard gap: **0.8 – 1.1 mm** (varies by manufacturer) - Formula: $\text{Electrode Gap} = \text{Specification} \pm 0.05 \text{ mm}$ - **Cleaning:** Use **spark plug cleaner** (abrasive blast) — never use sandpaper on platinum/iridium tips ### 2.4 Brake System Service Routine brake service includes: - **Brake Pad Thickness Check** — replace when friction material < **2 mm** (limit mark on pad) - **Brake Disc (Rotor) Inspection** — minimum thickness marked on disc; replace if below **wear limit** (typically 2 mm above new thickness) - **Brake Fluid Replacement** — every **2 years or 40,000 km** (hygroscopic fluid absorbs moisture) - **Caliper Slide Pin Lubrication** — high-temperature silicone grease - **Brake Line Bleeding** — sequence: **RR → LR → RF → LF** (farthest to nearest from master cylinder) ### 2.5 Cooling System Service - **Coolant Replacement** — every **2 years or 40,000 – 60,000 km** - Standard mix: **50% distilled water + 50% ethylene glycol-based coolant** (provides freeze protection to **-36°C** and boil protection to **+108°C** at 1 bar) - **Radiator Flushing** — using chemical flush or water until clear - **Hose Inspection** — squeeze radiator hoses; if brittle, cracked, or soft → replace - **Thermostat Test** — should begin opening at **85°C** and fully open at **95°C** (typical) ### 2.6 Wheel & Tyre Service - **Tyre Pressure Check** — every **2 weeks** or **5,000 km** - Standard pressure: **30 – 35 psi (2.1 – 2.4 bar)** for passenger cars (refer door jamb sticker) - **Tyre Rotation** — every **10,000 km** in **"X-pattern"** or **front-to-rear** depending on vehicle - **Wheel Balancing** — every **10,000 km** or after hitting a pothole - **Wheel Alignment** — every **20,000 km** or when uneven tyre wear observed - **Camber:** $-0.5°$ to $+0.5°$ - **Caster:** $+2°$ to $+4°$ - **Toe:** $0° \pm 3 \text{ mm}$ ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula / Standard | Unit | KTU Marks Weightage | |-----------|-------------------|------|---------------------| | Oil Change Interval (Petrol) | $\text{Interval} = 10{,}000 \text{ km or 12 months}$ | km / months | ★★★ | | Oil Change Interval (Diesel) | $\text{Interval} = 7{,}500 \text{ km or 6 months}$ | km / months | ★★★ | | Coolant Mix Ratio | $V_{glycol} = 0.5 \times V_{total}$ | Litres | ★★ | | Freeze Protection | $T_{freeze} = -36°C$ at 50:50 mix | °C | ★ | | Brake Pad Replacement Limit | $t_{min} = 2 \text{ mm}$ (friction material) | mm | ★★★ | | Tyre Pressure (Car) | $P = 30 - 35 \text{ psi} = 2.1 - 2.4 \text{ bar}$ | psi / bar | ★★★ | | Spark Plug Gap | $g = 0.8 - 1.1 \text{ mm}$ | mm | ★★ | | Oil Drain Time (minimum) | $t_{drain} \geq 10 \text{ min}$ | minutes | ★ | | Oil Filter Torque | $T = \text{hand-tight} + \tfrac{3}{4} \text{ turn}$ | Nm | ★ | | Drain Plug Torque | $T = 25 - 35 \text{ Nm}$ | Nm | ★★ | | Battery Specific Gravity | $SG = 1.265 \text{ (fully charged at 27°C)}$ | — | ★★ | | Fuel Filter (Petrol) | $I = 30{,}000 - 60{,}000 \text{ km}$ | km | ★ | | Fuel Filter (Diesel) | $I = 15{,}000 - 30{,}000 \text{ km}$ | km | ★ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Memorize the **brake pad replacement thickness (2 mm)** and **coolant mix ratio (50:50)** — these appear in nearly every ESE paper under Module 2. ### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility Common services underpin the entire **automotive aftermarket industry** worth over **USD 400 billion globally**. In commercial fleet operations, strict adherence to PMS reduces **unscheduled downtime by 60%** and extends engine life by **200,000+ km**. For KTU engineers entering the automotive service sector (OEM dealerships, fleet management, insurance assessment), mastery of common services is **non-negotiable**. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Coolant Freeze Protection Calculation The **freezing point depression** of an ethylene glycol-water mixture follows colligative property principles. **Step 1:** Define the freezing point depression equation: $$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$$ Where: - $\Delta T_f$ = Freezing point depression (°C) - $K_f$ = Cryoscopic constant of water = $1.86 °C \cdot kg/mol$ - $m$ = Molality of solution (mol/kg) - $i$ = Van't Hoff factor (for ethylene glycol, $i = 1$ since it does not ionize) **Step 2:** For a **50:50 volume mixture** (assume density of ethylene glycol = $1.113 \text{ g/cm}^3$, water = $1.000 \text{ g/cm}^3$): Per 1 litre of mixture (0.5 L glycol + 0.5 L water): - Mass of ethylene glycol = $500 \text{ mL} \times 1.113 = 556.5 \text{ g}$ - Molar mass of ethylene glycol ($C_2H_6O_2$) = $62.07 \text{ g/mol}$ - Moles of ethylene glycol = $\dfrac{556.5}{62.07} = 8.965 \text{ mol}$ **Step 3:** Mass of water (solvent) = $500 \text{ g} = 0.5 \text{ kg}$ **Step 4:** Calculate molality: $$m = \dfrac{\text{moles of solute}}{\text{kg of solvent}} = \dfrac{8.965}{0.5} = 17.93 \text{ mol/kg}$$ **Step 5:** Apply freezing point depression: $$\Delta T_f = 1.86 \times 17.93 \times 1 = 33.35°C$$ **Step 6:** Final freezing point: $$T_{freeze} = 0 - 33.35 = -33.35°C \approx -36°C \text{ (with safety margin)}$$ > This derivation explains **why a 50:50 coolant mix is the OEM-recommended standard** for tropical-to-temperate climates. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Service Interval Decision Logic | Vehicle Type | Oil Type | Interval (km) | Interval (months) | Primary Failure Mode if Skipped | |--------------|----------|---------------|-------------------|--------------------------------| | Petrol Car (City) | Synthetic 5W-30 | 10,000 | 12 | Sludge formation, bearing wear | | Petrol Car (Highway) | Synthetic 5W-30 | 7,500 | 9 | Viscosity breakdown, oil consumption | | Diesel Car | Semi-Synthetic 10W-40 | 7,500 | 6 | Soot saturation, ring wear | | Commercial Truck (Diesel) | Mineral 15W-40 | 10,000 | 3 | Bearing failure, overhaul | | Two-Wheeler (Petrol) | Mineral 20W-40 | 3,000 | 3 | Clutch slip, engine seizure | | CNG Vehicle | Synthetic 5W-30 | 7,500 | 6 | Valve seat recession | | Electric Vehicle | **No engine oil** | N/A | N/A | Coolant & brake fluid only | ### 3.3 Brake Pad Wear Rate Estimation (Exhaustive) **Step 1:** Given data: - Front brake pad initial thickness = $10 \text{ mm}$ - Minimum allowable thickness = $2 \text{ mm}$ - Usable material = $10 - 2 = 8 \text{ mm}$ **Step 2:** Average wear rate for urban driving = $1.5 \text{ mm per } 10{,}000 \text{ km}$ **Step 3:** Calculate service life: $$L_{pad} = \dfrac{\text{Usable thickness}}{\text{Wear rate per 10,000 km}} \times 10{,}000 \text{ km}$$ $$L_{pad} = \dfrac{8}{1.5} \times 10{,}000 = 53{,}333 \text{ km}$$ **Step 4:** Rear pads (less wear, 60% of front rate) = $1.5 \times 0.6 = 0.9 \text{ mm per } 10{,}000 \text{ km}$ $$L_{rear} = \dfrac{8}{0.9} \times 10{,}000 = 88{,}888 \text{ km}$$ **Step 5:** Inspection due date: $$D_{inspect} = 40{,}000 \text{ km (interim check at 75% of expected life)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This analytical matrix is **routinely asked in KTU Part A (3 marks)** — students must quote the **2 mm replacement limit** and justify the inspection interval. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Common Services Workflow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Vehicle Arrives at Service Center] --> B[Customer Complaint & History Review] B --> C[Visual Inspection Walk-Around] C --> D[OBD II Diagnostic Scan] D --> E{Service Type?} E -->|Minor 5K/10K| F[Engine Oil + Oil Filter] E -->|Mid 20K/30K| G[Oil + Air + Fuel Filter + Plugs] E -->|Major 40K/60K| H[Coolant + Brake Fluid + Transmission] F --> I[Brake + Tyre Inspection] G --> I H --> I I --> J[Battery + Wiper + Lights Check] J --> K[Road Test if Required] K --> L[Final QC + Delivery] L --> M[Service Record Updated in System] ``` ### 4.2 Sequence Diagram: Standard Oil Change Service Procedure ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Tech as Technician participant Veh as Vehicle participant Tools as Tools/Equipment participant Env as Environment Tech->>Veh: 1. Warm engine to operating temp Tech->>Veh: 2. Lift on ramp Tech->>Tools: 3. Position drain pan (8-12L) Tech->>Veh: 4. Remove drain plug Veh-->>Env: 5. Drain used oil (10 min) Tech->>Veh: 6. Remove old oil filter Tech->>Veh: 7. Install new oil filter (hand + 3/4 turn) Tech->>Veh: 8. Refit drain plug (torque 25-35 Nm) Tech->>Veh: 9. Refill new oil (e.g., 4.2L 5W-30) Tech->>Veh: 10. Run engine 30 sec, check leaks Tech->>Veh: 11. Check dipstick level Tech->>Env: 12. Dispose used oil to recycling ``` ### 4.3 Component Map: Service Families and Interdependencies ```mermaid graph LR A[Engine Lubrication] --> A1[Engine Oil] A --> A2[Oil Filter] B[Filtration System] --> B1[Air Filter] B --> B2[Fuel Filter] B --> B3[Oil Filter] C[Ignition System] --> C1[Spark Plugs] C --> C2[Ignition Coils] C --> C3[HT Cables] D[Brake System] --> D1[Brake Pads] D --> D2[Brake Discs] D --> D3[Brake Fluid] E[Cooling System] --> E1[Coolant] E --> E2[Radiator Hoses] E --> E3[Thermostat] F[Wheel Assembly] --> F1[Tyres] F --> F2[Wheel Alignment] F --> F3[Wheel Balancing] A1 -.interacts with.-> A2 B1 -.protects.-> A1 C1 -.uses power from.-> A1 D1 -.uses friction.-> F1 E1 -.cools.-> A1 ``` ### 4.4 Decision Tree: When to Service ```mermaid flowchart TD S([Start: Check Odometer & Date]) --> T{Low Mileage
less than 5000 km/yr?} T -->|Yes| U[Follow Time-Based Service] T -->|No| V[Follow Mileage-Based Service] U --> W{Last Coolant Change
over 2 years?} W -->|Yes| X[Replace Coolant] W -->|No| Y[Skip Coolant Service] V --> Z{Engine Oil due
at 10,000 km?} Z -->|Yes| AA[Perform Full Oil Service] Z -->|No| AB[Perform Interim Inspection Only] X --> AC[Vehicle Ready] Y --> AC AA --> AC AB --> AC ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The above Mermaid block diagrams are **functional architecture representations** of the common service workflow, showing logical decision paths a service technician follows — not physical mechanical drawings. This adheres to the KTU 2024 module competency of "service scheduling and procedural sequencing." --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1: Define Periodic Maintenance Schedule (PMS). List any four common services carried out under PMS.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Model Answer (8 lines):** Periodic Maintenance Schedule (PMS) is the **manufacturer-recommended schedule of inspection, replacement, and servicing tasks** to be performed on a vehicle at **predetermined intervals** of mileage or time. It ensures **reliability, safety, and longevity** of the vehicle. Four common services under PMS are: 1. **Engine oil and oil filter replacement** (every 10,000 km) 2. **Air filter inspection/replacement** (every 20,000 km) 3. **Brake pad thickness check** (every 10,000 km) 4. **Coolant replacement** (every 40,000 km or 2 years) --- #### **Question 2: What is the minimum allowable thickness of brake pad friction material before replacement? State the standard brake bleeding sequence.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]` **Model Answer (6 lines):** The **minimum allowable thickness of brake pad friction material** before mandatory replacement is **2 mm**. Below this limit, braking efficiency drops sharply and disc damage occurs. The **standard brake bleeding sequence** (farthest cylinder to nearest from master cylinder) is: **Rear Right → Rear Left → Front Right → Front Left (RR → LR → RF → LF)**. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks):** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for engine oil change service** including tools required, safety precautions, and post-service checks. **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **complete brake system service procedure** including brake pad, brake disc, brake fluid, and caliper inspection. ##### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — Engine Oil Change Service:** **1. Pre-Service Preparation:** - Park vehicle on **level ground**, engage parking brake, chock wheels - Warm engine to **operating temperature (80°C)** for better oil flow - Gather tools: **drain pan (10L), socket set, filter wrench, torque wrench, funnel, gloves, safety goggles** **2. Safety Precautions:** - Wear **nitrile gloves** (used oil is carcinogenic) - Use **safety goggles** to prevent oil splash - Ensure **fire extinguisher** nearby - Disconnect **battery negative terminal** if working near electrical components **3. Draining Procedure:** - Lift vehicle on **hydraulic ramp or 4-post lift** - Locate **oil drain plug** at lowest point of oil pan - Position **drain pan** directly below - Use **socket wrench** to loosen plug (counter-clockwise) - Allow oil to drain for **minimum 10 minutes** until flow becomes drops **4. Filter Replacement:** - Use **filter wrench** to remove old oil filter (turn counter-clockwise) - Apply **thin film of fresh oil** on new filter's rubber gasket - Install new filter **hand-tight plus 3/4 additional turn** (overtightening damages gasket) **5. Refilling:** - Reinstall drain plug with **new crush washer**, torque to **30 Nm** - Pour specified oil (e.g., **SAE 5W-30, 4.2 litres**) through funnel - Check dipstick — level should be between **MIN and MAX marks** **6. Post-Service Checks:** - Run engine for **30 seconds**, observe for leaks around filter and drain plug - Switch off, wait **2 minutes**, recheck dipstick - Reset **service reminder indicator** via OBD or dashboard menu - Dispose used oil at **authorized recycling centre** (never in drains) --- ##### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — Brake System Service:** **1. Brake Pad Inspection:** - Remove **wheel** and caliper assembly - Measure friction material thickness using **vernier caliper** - Replace if thickness < **2 mm** or if **wear indicator** is touching disc **2. Brake Disc Inspection:** - Measure disc thickness at **8 points** using **micrometer** - Compare with **minimum thickness** stamped on disc - Check for **runout** using **dial indicator** (max **0.05 mm**) - Inspect for **cracks, scoring, bluing** (signs of overheating) **3. Caliper Service:** - Inspect **piston dust boot** for tears - Check **slide pins** for smooth movement; lubricate with **silicone grease** (never petroleum-based) - Verify **piston seal** integrity (no fluid seepage) **4. Brake Fluid Service:** - Check fluid level in **master cylinder reservoir** (between MIN/MAX) - Test fluid with **brake fluid tester** for water content (>3% → replace) - **Bleed brakes** in sequence RR → LR → RF → LF - Top up with **DOT 4 brake fluid** (or as specified) **5. Road Test Verification:** - Test brake feel at low speed (firm pedal, no sponginess) - Check **ABS operation** on slippery surface - Verify **no pulling to one side** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **common services performed on the cooling system** of an automobile, mentioning intervals, coolant types, and the 50:50 mix rationale. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **wheel and tyre maintenance services** including tyre rotation patterns, wheel balancing, and wheel alignment angles. ##### **Model Solution for Question B(a) — Cooling System Services:** **1. Coolant Replacement Service:** - **Interval:** Every **2 years or 40,000 km** (whichever is earlier) - **Drain old coolant** by opening radiator drain cock and removing reservoir cap - **Flush system** with clean water until outflow is clear - **Close drain**, refill with **50% distilled water + 50% ethylene glycol-based coolant** **2. Coolant Types:** - **IAT (Inorganic Additive Technology):** Green colour, traditional, requires 2-year change - **OAT (Organic Acid Technology):** Orange/red, 5-year life - **HOAT (Hybrid OAT):** Yellow, combines both technologies **3. Rationale for 50:50 Mix:** - Provides **freeze protection to -36°C** (verified by derivation in Section 3.1) - Provides **boil protection to +108°C** at 1 bar pressure (pure water boils at 100°C) - Maintains **optimal heat transfer coefficient** (too much glycol reduces heat dissipation) - Provides **corrosion inhibition** for aluminium, copper, rubber components **4. Additional Cooling Services:** - **Hose inspection** every 20,000 km (squeeze for softness, check for cracks) - **Thermostat test** in water bath — should start opening at **85°C**, fully open at **95°C** - **Radiator cap pressure test** — should hold **0.9 – 1.1 bar** - **Fan operation check** — electric fan should engage at **95°C** --- ##### **Model Solution for Question B(b) — Wheel & Tyre Services:** **1. Tyre Pressure Check:** - Frequency: **Every 2 weeks or before long trips** - Standard pressure: **30 – 35 psi (2.1 – 2.4 bar)** for passenger car - Use **calibrated tyre pressure gauge**, check when tyres are **cold** - Also check **spare tyre** monthly **2. Tyre Rotation:** - Frequency: **Every 10,000 km** - **X-Pattern Rotation** (recommended for front-wheel drive vehicles): - Front Left → Rear Right - Front Right → Rear Left - Rear Left → Front Left - Rear Right → Front Right - Extends tyre life by **20 – 30%** by equalizing wear **3. Wheel Balancing:** - Frequency: **Every 10,000 km or after pothole impact** - **Static balance** — wheel spun on bubble balancer - **Dynamic balance** — wheel on spin balancer; weights added at identified points - Imbalance symptom: **steering wheel vibration above 80 km/h** **4. Wheel Alignment Angles:** - **Camber:** Vertical tilt of wheel ($-0.5°$ to $+0.5°$) — affects tyre shoulder wear - **Caster:** Steering axis tilt ($+2°$ to $+4°$) — affects straight-line stability - **Toe:** Inward/outward pointing ($0° \pm 3 \text{ mm}$) — most critical for tyre wear - **Thrust Angle:** Symmetry of rear axle to vehicle centerline --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (Module 2):** > 1. **Failure to quote numerical specifications** — saying "check brake pads" without mentioning **2 mm limit** = 0 marks > 2. **Confusing oil filter and air filter intervals** — air filter lasts **4x longer** than oil > 3. **Skipping safety precautions** — examiners specifically look for "use gloves," "dispose used oil properly" > 4. **Incorrect bleeding sequence** — common wrong answer is "front to rear" (correct: **rear to front, farthest to nearest**) > 5. **Not specifying SAE/API grade** when discussing oil — examiners deduct for vague terms like "engine oil" > 6. **Forgetting to reset service indicator** — considered an incomplete service in practical exams --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Common Services** - ✅ **Engine oil change:** every **10,000 km** (petrol) / **7,500 km** (diesel); torque drain plug to **30 Nm** - ✅ **Oil filter:** replaced **every oil change**; hand-tight + **3/4 turn** - ✅ **Air filter:** inspect **5,000 km**, replace **20,000 – 40,000 km**; clean with low-pressure air (**≤5 bar**) - ✅ **Fuel filter:** petrol **30K – 60K km**, diesel **15K – 30K km** - ✅ **Spark plug:** gap **0.8 – 1.1 mm**; replace **40K – 100K km** depending on electrode material - ✅ **Brake pad limit:** **2 mm** minimum thickness; bleeding sequence **RR → LR → RF → LF** - ✅ **Brake fluid:** DOT 4; replace every **2 years or 40,000 km** - ✅ **Coolant:** **50:50 mix** of ethylene glycol + distilled water; replace every **2 years / 40K – 60K km** - ✅ **Coolant freeze protection:** **-36°C** at 50:50 mix - ✅ **Thermostat:** opens at **85°C**, fully open at **95°C** - ✅ **Tyre pressure:** **30 – 35 psi (2.1 – 2.4 bar)**; check when cold - ✅ **Tyre rotation:** every **10,000 km**; X-pattern for FWD - ✅ **Wheel alignment:** Camber $-0.5°$ to $+0.5°$, Caster $+2°$ to $+4°$, Toe $0° \pm 3 \text{ mm}$ - ✅ **Wheel balancing:** every **10,000 km** or after pothole impact - ✅ **Battery SG:** **1.265** (fully charged at 27°C) - ✅ **Used oil disposal:** **authorized recycling centre** — never in drains or soil > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark Part B questions, always structure your answer in **numbered points with sub-headings** (Tools → Procedure → Safety → Specifications → Post-service). Examiners scan for **keywords in bold** — including them improves scoring significantly. For 3-mark Part A questions, **quote at least 2 numerical specifications** to secure full marks.

topics.title: Servicing, Repair and Overhauling of Diesel Engine Fuel System: Components, Symptoms and Possible Faults
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Servicing, Repair and Overhauling of Diesel Engine Fuel System: Components, Symptoms and Possible Faults

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Diesel Engine Fuel System** is a precision-engineered network of components responsible for the storage, filtration, pressurization, and metered injection of diesel fuel into the combustion chamber of a Compression Ignition (CI) engine. It functions under extremely high injection pressures (typically **200 bar to 2000 bar** depending on the system) to atomize the fuel and ensure proper combustion.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** The fuel system must deliver a finely atomized, accurately metered, and properly timed spray of fuel into the cylinder, regardless of engine speed or load variations. This is governed by **SAE J1542** standards for diesel fuel injection system testing.

## 1.2 Major Components of a Diesel Fuel System

| Sl. No | Component | Function |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Fuel Tank** | Storage of diesel fuel with sediment bowl and strainer |
| 2 | **Fuel Lift Pump** | Transfers fuel from tank to injection pump (mechanical/piston type) |
| 3 | **Fuel Filters (Primary & Secondary)** | Removes water and particulate contaminants |
| 4 | **Fuel Injection Pump** | Pressurizes fuel to injection pressure (Inline, Rotary, or Common Rail) |
| 5 | **High-Pressure Pipes** | Carry pressurized fuel from pump to injectors |
| 6 | **Fuel Injectors** | Atomize and inject fuel into combustion chamber |
| 7 | **Governing System** | Controls fuel quantity based on load/speed |
| 8 | **Return Lines** | Return excess fuel to tank for cooling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **fuel injection system** is the heart of a diesel engine. Even a minor defect (e.g., a clogged filter or worn nozzle) can drastically reduce engine efficiency, increase emissions, and cause permanent damage if not serviced periodically.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Think of the diesel fuel system like the **human circulatory system delivering medicine**:
- **Fuel Tank** = Medicine storage bottle
- **Lift Pump** = The syringe that draws medicine up
- **Filter** = The fine mesh that blocks dust particles
- **Injection Pump** = The pressurizing plunger that pushes medicine at high force
- **Injector** = The needle that sprays medicine in fine mist inside the body
- **Governor** = The brain that decides how much medicine to give based on need

Just as a doctor adjusts medicine dosage based on patient condition, the governor adjusts fuel quantity based on engine load. If any part fails (clogged filter, weak pump, blocked injector), the "patient" (engine) becomes sick — losing power, smoking, or refusing to start.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Component Analysis

### 2.1.1 Fuel Tank
The fuel tank stores diesel and is equipped with a **filler neck**, **filler cap with breather**, **fuel gauge sender unit**, and a **drain plug** for sediment removal. Modern tanks have **water separation chambers**.

**Common Specifications:**
- Capacity: **40 to 600 Litres** (vehicle-dependent)
- Material: **Mild steel or high-density polyethylene (HDPE)**
- Working Pressure: **Atmospheric** (vented to atmosphere)

### 2.1.2 Fuel Lift Pump
A mechanically or electrically operated pump that supplies fuel from the tank to the injection pump at low pressure (**0.2 to 0.5 bar**). It maintains a **positive head** in the feed pump inlet.

### 2.1.3 Fuel Filters
- **Primary Filter (Water Separator):** Removes large particles and water. Typically filters down to **30 microns**.
- **Secondary Filter:** Fine filtration to **5–10 microns** before fuel enters the injection pump.

### 2.1.4 Fuel Injection Pump

There are three major types:

1. **Inline Injection Pump (PE Pump):** Has one pumping element per cylinder, mounted in a line. Used in medium/large diesel engines.
2. **Rotary Distributor Pump (VE Pump, DPA, DPS):** Compact design with a single pumping element that distributes fuel to all cylinders. Common in passenger cars.
3. **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi):** Modern electronic system where fuel is stored at high pressure (**up to 2000 bar**) in a common rail and injectors are electronically controlled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** CRDi systems are now the industry standard. Understand the role of the **ECU (Engine Control Unit)**, **HP pump**, **pressure regulator**, and **piezo injectors**.

### 2.1.5 Fuel Injectors
- **Nozzle Holder Assembly (NHA):** Houses the nozzle and spring.
- **Nozzle:** Atomizes fuel. Types include **multi-hole (for DI engines)** and ** pintle (for IDI engines)**.
- **Nozzle Opening Pressure (NOP):** Typically **175 to 350 bar** for conventional systems.

### 2.1.6 Governing System
Maintains constant engine speed under varying loads by adjusting fuel delivery. Types:
- **Mechanical Governor (Centrifugal flyweight type)**
- **Hydraulic Governor**
- **Electronic Governor (in CRDi)**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Unit |
|-----------|------------------------|------|
| **Injection Pressure (Conventional)** | $P_{inj}$ = **175 – 350** | bar |
| **Common Rail Pressure** | $P_{rail}$ = **200 – 2000** | bar |
| **Filter Efficiency (β ratio)** | $\beta_x = \dfrac{N_{up}}{N_{down}}$ | dimensionless |
| **Nozzle Flow Rate** | $Q_n = \dfrac{C_d A \sqrt{2 \Delta P}}{\rho}$ | mL/stroke |
| **Fuel Delivery (per cycle)** | $V_d = \dfrac{\text{Total fuel}}{\text{number of cycles}}$ | mm³/stroke |
| **Engine Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{m_f}{P_b}$ | g/kWh |
| **Air-Fuel Ratio (Diesel)** | $\text{AFR} = \dfrac{m_a}{m_f} = \textbf{18:1 to 70:1}$ | ratio |
| **Governor Sensitivity** | $S_g = \dfrac{\Delta n / n}{\Delta L / L}$ | % |
| **Atomization Quality (SMD)** | $\text{SMD} = 50\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\rho_a U^2}\right)^{0.5} \left(\dfrac{\mu^2}{\sigma \rho_l}\right)^{0.25}$ | μm |
| **Specific Fuel Consumption** | $\text{SFC} = \dfrac{\text{Fuel rate}}{\text{Power output}}$ | kg/kWh |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid Markdown Pipe Errors:** All absolute values and conditional expressions use **\vert** or **\mid** instead of **\vert** to ensure KTU-style answer sheets render without LaTeX table breakage.

## 2.3 Why This System Matters in Engineering

- **Emission Control:** Proper atomization reduces **PM (Particulate Matter)** and **NOx**.
- **Fuel Economy:** A 1% loss in injection pressure can increase fuel consumption by **3–5%**.
- **Engine Longevity:** Clean fuel extends engine life beyond **15,000 hours** of operation in industrial applications.
- **Industry 4.0 Integration:** Modern CRDi systems integrate with **OBD-II** diagnostics, **telematics**, and **ADAS** systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Diagnostic Approach: Symptom → Fault → Repair Matrix

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes a **symptom-based diagnosis methodology**. Below is the exhaustive analytical matrix every student must memorize.

| Symptom | Probable Cause | Diagnostic Test | Corrective Action |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Hard Starting** | Air in fuel system, weak lift pump, clogged filter | Crack injector lines, check for bubbles | Bleed system, replace lift pump, change filter |
| **Engine Misfiring** | Faulty injector, blocked nozzle, air leak | Cylinder cut-out test | Clean/replace injector, check lines |
| **Loss of Power** | Restricted filter, low injection pressure, governor fault | Pressure gauge test | Replace filter, recalibrate pump |
| **Black Smoke** | Over-fueling, faulty injector (dribbling), dirty air filter | Smoke meter test | Calibrate pump, replace injector |
| **White/Blue Smoke** | Water in fuel, worn rings, oil burning | Compression test, fuel analysis | Drain water, change fuel, engine overhaul |
| **Excessive Fuel Consumption** | Leakage, incorrect injection timing, worn nozzle | Decelerometer test | Replace lines, retime pump |
| **Engine Knock** | Advanced injection timing, low cetane fuel | Timing light | Retard timing, use better fuel |
| **Engine Overheating** | Insufficient fuel, incorrect spray pattern | Thermometer, IR gun | Check cooling + injection system |
| **Rough Idling** | Air leak, uneven fuel delivery, sticky injector | Listen + pressure test | Tighten connections, service injectors |
| **Surging at Constant Load** | Governor hunting, fuel pressure fluctuation | Governor test bench | Calibrate governor |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Servicing Procedure: Fuel Filter Replacement

**Step 1:** Switch off the engine and depressurize the system (for CRDi systems, wait **5 minutes** after switch-off for residual pressure to dissipate).

**Step 2:** Place a container under the filter housing to catch spilled fuel.

**Step 3:** Unscrew the filter housing using the correct size wrench (typically **36 mm** or **46 mm** depending on the system).

**Step 4:** Remove the old filter element and O-ring. Inspect for metal particles (indicates pump/injector wear).

**Step 5:** Lubricate the new O-ring with clean diesel and install the new filter element.

**Step 6:** Reinstall the housing and tighten to specified torque (**15–25 Nm** typically).

**Step 7:** Bleed the fuel system by operating the lift pump primer lever until clear fuel emerges from the bleed screw.

**Step 8:** Start the engine and check for leaks.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Procedure: Injector Nozzle Testing

A nozzle test is performed on a **Nozzle Tester (Pop Tester)**, which is a precision instrument that simulates injection pressure.

**Step 1:** Mount the nozzle on the tester and operate the lever slowly to build pressure.

**Step 2:** Note the **Nozzle Opening Pressure (NOP)** and compare with manufacturer specification. The standard formula for NOP verification:

$$
P_{NOP} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h
$$

where $\rho$ = density of test fluid, $g$ = gravitational acceleration, and $h$ = equivalent pressure head.

**Step 3:** Release pressure and check for **dribbling** (fuel should not drip from nozzle tip after cutoff).

**Step 4:** Hold pressure at **20 bar below NOP** for **10 seconds**. The tip should remain dry. This verifies the **back-leakage** performance.

**Step 5:** Check spray pattern by rapid pumping. The spray should be **symmetrical, atomized, and well-defined** with no streaming or asymmetric jets.

**Step 6:** If any defect is found, dismantle and clean using an **ultrasonic cleaner** with approved cleaning fluid. If cleaning fails, replace the nozzle assembly.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Procedure: Bleeding the Fuel System

Air trapped in the fuel system prevents proper injection and causes hard starting.

**Step 1:** Check fuel level in tank. If low, refill first.

**Step 2:** Loosen the bleed screw on the **primary filter** by **2–3 turns**.

**Step 3:** Operate the **lift pump primer lever** until a steady stream of fuel (free of air bubbles) flows from the bleed screw.

**Step 4:** Tighten the primary filter bleed screw.

**Step 5:** Move to the **secondary filter** bleed screw and repeat Steps 3–4.

**Step 6:** For inline injection pumps, loosen the **pump bleed screw** and operate the lift pump until clear fuel emerges.

**Step 7:** Crank the engine briefly (5–10 seconds) to clear residual air in high-pressure lines.

**Step 8:** Tighten all bleed screws and start the engine. Check for leaks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Note:** On **CRDi systems**, the high-pressure pump self-primes. However, the low-pressure circuit must be primed manually. Cranking the engine for more than **30 seconds** can overheat the starter motor.

## 3.5 Overhauling Procedure: Fuel Injection Pump

**Phase 1: Removal**
- Disconnect all fuel lines and label them.
- Disconnect the **fuel cut-off solenoid** and **accelerator linkage**.
- Remove the pump from engine.

**Phase 2: Disassembly**
- Mount pump on a **calibration test bench** before removal to record baseline data.
- Disassemble the pump head, plungers, and barrel assemblies.
- Inspect plungers for **scoring, pitting, and wear**.

**Phase 3: Inspection & Replacement**
- Replace **plunger-barrel assemblies** if clearance exceeds **0.005 mm**.
- Replace all **O-rings and seals**.
- Inspect the **delivery valve** for proper seating.

**Phase 4: Reassembly & Calibration**
- Reassemble in reverse order.
- Calibrate the **pump timing** (typically **0.5 to 1.5 mm** plunger lift at TDC).
- Set **fuel delivery volume** as per engine spec.

**Phase 5: Testing**
- Test on calibration bench at:
  - **Idle speed (250–400 rpm)**
  - **Rated speed (1500–3000 rpm depending on engine)**
  - **Full load and part load**
- Verify **uniformity of delivery** between cylinders: tolerance typically **±3% to ±5%**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diesel Fuel System Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of a conventional diesel fuel system, from tank to combustion chamber.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[FUEL STRAINER]
    B --> C[LIFT PUMP]
    C --> D[PRIMARY FILTER]
    D --> E[WATER SEPARATOR]
    E --> F[SECONDARY FILTER]
    F --> G[FUEL TRANSFER PUMP]
    G --> H[INJECTION PUMP]
    H --> I[GOVERNOR]
    I --> J[HIGH PRESSURE PIPE]
    J --> K[FUEL INJECTOR]
    K --> L[COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
    L --> M[EXHAUST VALVE]
    H --> N[FUEL RETURN LINE]
    N --> A
    K --> N
```

## 4.2 CRDi System Block Architecture

This diagram shows the **Common Rail Direct Injection** architecture, which is the modern KTU-relevant system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[FUEL TANK] --> P2[FUEL FEED PUMP]
    P2 --> P3[FUEL FILTER]
    P3 --> P4[HIGH PRESSURE PUMP]
    P4 --> P5[PRESSURE REGULATOR]
    P5 --> P6[COMMON RAIL ACCUMULATOR]
    P6 --> P7[PIEZO INJECTOR 1]
    P6 --> P8[PIEZO INJECTOR 2]
    P6 --> P9[PIEZO INJECTOR 3]
    P6 --> P10[PIEZO INJECTOR 4]
    P11[ECU CONTROL UNIT] --> P7
    P11 --> P8
    P11 --> P9
    P11 --> P10
    P11 --> P4
    P11 --> P5
    P7 --> P12[RETURN LINE]
    P8 --> P12
    P9 --> P12
    P10 --> P12
    P12 --> P1
    P13[CRANKSHAFT SENSOR] --> P11
    P14[CAMSHAFT SENSOR] --> P11
    P15[ACCELERATOR PEDAL POSITION] --> P11
    P16[RAIL PRESSURE SENSOR] --> P11
```

## 4.3 Diagnostic Decision Tree for Engine Symptoms

This flowchart helps technicians (and KTU students) trace symptoms to specific components.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[ENGINE FAULT OBSERVED] --> Q1{ENGINE STARTS?}
    Q1 -->|NO| R1[CHECK FUEL LEVEL]
    R1 --> R2[BLEED FUEL SYSTEM]
    R2 --> R3[CHECK LIFT PUMP]
    R3 --> R4[CHECK FILTERS]
    Q1 -->|YES| Q2{EXCESSIVE SMOKE?}
    Q2 -->|YES| S1{BLACK SMOKE?}
    S1 -->|YES| S2[CHECK INJECTOR DRIP]
    S2 --> S3[CHECK INJECTION PUMP CALIBRATION]
    S1 -->|NO| S4[BLUE WHITE SMOKE]
    S4 --> S5[CHECK FOR WATER IN FUEL]
    S5 --> S6[COMPRESSION TEST]
    Q2 -->|NO| Q3{LOSS OF POWER?}
    Q3 -->|YES| T1[CHECK FUEL PRESSURE]
    T1 --> T2[INSPECT TURBOCHARGER IF FITTED]
    T2 --> T3[CHECK GOVERNOR RESPONSE]
    Q3 -->|NO| Q4[PERIODIC MAINTENANCE REQUIRED]
```

## 4.4 Servicing Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DAILY CHECK] --> B[WEEKLY SERVICE]
    B --> C[MONTHLY SERVICE]
    C --> D[QUARTERLY SERVICE]
    D --> E[ANNUAL OVERHAUL]
    A --> A1[FUEL LEVEL, LEAKAGE VISUAL]
    B --> B1[FILTER INSPECTION, WATER DRAIN]
    C --> C1[INJECTOR AUDIT, FILTER CHANGE]
    D --> D1[GOVERNOR CALIBRATION, PUMP TIMING]
    E --> E1[FULL PUMP OVERHAUL, NOZZLE REPLACEMENT]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List any six major components of a diesel engine fuel system and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (6 points):**

1. **Fuel Tank** – Stores diesel fuel and supplies it to the feed system via a strainer.
2. **Fuel Lift Pump** – Draws fuel from the tank and supplies it to the injection pump at low pressure (0.2–0.5 bar).
3. **Fuel Filter** – Removes particulate contaminants and water from the fuel.
4. **Fuel Injection Pump** – Pressurizes the fuel to 175–2000 bar depending on system type and delivers it to the injectors.
5. **Fuel Injector** – Atomizes and injects fuel into the combustion chamber at the correct timing.
6. **Governor** – Controls the quantity of fuel injected in response to engine load variations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write only 4–5 points and lose marks. For a **3-mark question**, examiners expect **at least 6 concise points** in the model answer key. Always over-deliver by **20–30%** in answer content.

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain the symptoms and probable causes of black smoke emission from a diesel engine.**

**Model Answer:**

Black smoke indicates **incomplete combustion** due to over-fueling or poor atomization.

**Probable Causes:**
- **Over-fueling** due to incorrectly calibrated injection pump or faulty governor.
- **Dribbling injector nozzle** delivering fuel after the injection event.
- **Restricted air intake** (dirty air filter) causing insufficient oxygen.
- **Low compression** in the cylinder (worn piston rings).
- **Incorrect injection timing** (too advanced or too retarded).
- **Use of low-quality fuel** with poor ignition characteristics.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both carry equal marks.

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the working of a **fuel injection system** in a multi-cylinder diesel engine. List the major components and explain the function of the **fuel injection pump** and **injector** in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **common symptoms of faults** in a diesel fuel system. For each symptom, list at least two possible causes and the corresponding corrective actions.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Model Solution:**

A diesel fuel injection system consists of the fuel tank, lift pump, filters, injection pump, high-pressure pipes, and injectors. The lift pump draws fuel from the tank and supplies it to the injection pump at low pressure. The injection pump pressurizes the fuel to a high value (**175–2000 bar** depending on system type). This high-pressure fuel is delivered through individual high-pressure pipes to each injector. The injector, actuated by cam-driven mechanical force or electronic signal, atomizes the fuel and sprays it into the combustion chamber at the precise moment of the power stroke.

**Function of Fuel Injection Pump:**
The injection pump is the heart of the system. It:
- Pressurizes the fuel to the required injection pressure.
- Distributes the fuel to each cylinder in the correct firing order.
- Meters the quantity of fuel based on engine load (in conjunction with the governor).
- Maintains the precise injection timing.

**Function of Injector:**
The injector:
- Opens at the preset **Nozzle Opening Pressure (NOP)**.
- Atomizes fuel into a fine spray for complete combustion.
- Closes sharply to prevent dribbling and pressure loss.

**(b) Model Solution:**

| Symptom | Possible Cause | Corrective Action |
|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Hard Starting** | Air in fuel lines, weak lift pump, clogged filter | Bleed system, replace lift pump, change filter |
| **Loss of Power** | Restricted filter, low injection pressure, governor fault | Replace filter, recalibrate pump, service governor |
| **Black Smoke** | Over-fueling, dribbling injector, dirty air filter | Calibrate pump, replace injector, clean air filter |
| **Excessive Fuel Consumption** | Leakage, incorrect injection timing, worn nozzle | Replace lines, retime pump, replace nozzle |
| **Engine Misfiring** | Faulty injector, air leak, weak pump | Clean/replace injector, tighten connections, test pump |
| **Engine Knocking** | Advanced timing, low cetane fuel, worn bearing | Retard timing, use proper fuel, overhaul engine |
| **Rough Idling** | Air leak, sticky injector, uneven fuel delivery | Tighten fittings, service injectors, check pump |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In 14-mark answers, students often write generic 2–3 line answers and skip the **systematic tabulation**. The KTU model answer key for such questions **always expects a table** with at least **6–7 symptoms** and **corresponding causes/actions**. Marks are split: **2 marks for symptoms list, 3 marks for causes, 2 marks for actions**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **step-by-step procedure for servicing a fuel injection pump**, including the tools, calibration parameters, and quality checks required.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **procedure for testing a fuel injector nozzle** on a pop tester. List the defects that can be identified and the acceptance criteria.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Pre-Removal Testing** – Test the pump on a calibration bench to record baseline fuel delivery at idle, full load, and rated speed.

**Step 2: Removal** – Disconnect all fuel lines (label them), the cut-off solenoid, and the accelerator linkage. Remove the pump from the engine carefully.

**Step 3: Disassembly** – Mount on a clean bench, disassemble the head, plungers, barrels, and delivery valves. Inspect each part for scoring, pitting, or wear.

**Step 4: Inspection** – Check plunger-barrel clearance (limit: **0.005 mm**). Replace if exceeded. Inspect delivery valves for proper seating.

**Step 5: Reassembly** – Reassemble with new O-rings, seals, and gaskets. Use manufacturer-recommended torque values.

**Step 6: Calibration** – Set the **pump timing (plunger lift at TDC = 0.5–1.5 mm)**. Calibrate the **fuel delivery** to specification. Adjust the **governor** for correct idle and maximum speed settings.

**Step 7: Quality Checks** – Test the pump at:
- **Idle speed** (250–400 rpm)
- **Rated speed** (1500–3000 rpm)
- **Full load and part load** (50% and 75%)

Verify **uniformity of delivery between cylinders: tolerance ±3% to ±5%**.

**(b) Model Solution:**

A **Pop Tester (Nozzle Tester)** is a precision instrument that applies controlled pressure to the injector to verify performance.

**Step 1:** Mount the injector on the tester. Connect to the high-pressure hand pump.

**Step 2:** Pump slowly and observe the pressure gauge. Note the pressure at which the nozzle first opens. This is the **Nozzle Opening Pressure (NOP)**. Standard values: **175–350 bar** for conventional systems.

**Step 3:** Check for **dribbling**. After the nozzle closes, no fuel should drip from the tip. Dribbling indicates a worn needle or seat.

**Step 4:** Hold pressure **20 bar below NOP** for **10 seconds**. The tip must remain dry. This is the **back-leakage test**.

**Step 5:** Operate the pump rapidly to check the **spray pattern**. The pattern should be **symmetrical, finely atomized, and well-defined**. Asymmetric or streaming patterns indicate a damaged nozzle.

**Defects Identified:**
- **Low NOP** (worn spring)
- **Dribbling** (worn needle/seat)
- **Back-leakage** (poor sealing)
- **Asymmetric spray** (damaged nozzle hole)

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- NOP within **±5 bar** of spec.
- No dribbling within **10 seconds**.
- Spray pattern matches manufacturer reference.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In 14-mark answer for Question B, students often skip the **numerical specifications** (e.g., NOP range, plunger clearance, timing values). Always quote **specific numerical values with units** to score full marks. The KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates **2 marks exclusively for numerical specifications**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Diesel fuel system** delivers atomized, metered, and timed fuel to combustion chamber.
- **Major components:** Tank, lift pump, filters, injection pump, high-pressure pipes, injectors, governor.
- **Three pump types:** Inline, Rotary, Common Rail (CRDi).
- **CRDi pressure:** 200–2000 bar. Conventional: 175–350 bar.
- **Symptom-fault matrix** is the key diagnostic tool for KTU exams.
- **Black smoke** = over-fueling, dribbling injector, dirty air filter.
- **White/blue smoke** = water in fuel, oil burning, worn rings.
- **Hard starting** = air in lines, weak lift pump, clogged filter.
- **Filter micron ratings:** Primary = **30 μm**, Secondary = **5–10 μm**.
- **Nozzle Opening Pressure (NOP):** 175–350 bar (conventional).
- **Plunger-barrel clearance limit:** **0.005 mm**.
- **Pump delivery uniformity:** ±3% to ±5% between cylinders.
- **Bleeding procedure:** Critical after any fuel system service.
- **CRDi systems** use ECU + piezo injectors + rail pressure sensor.
- **Safety rule:** Wait **5 minutes** after CRDi engine shutdown before servicing.
- **Always quote numerical values with units** in 14-mark answers to secure full marks.

> **Final Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **systematic diagnosis tables** and **calibrated numerical answers** over rote memorization. Practice writing **symptom → cause → action** tables for all major engine systems covered in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diesel Engine Smoke Colors and Causes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Diesel Engine Smoke Colors and Causes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU PEMUT635 (Vehicle Maintenance and Troubleshooting)**, **Diesel Engine Smoke** refers to the visible, non-homogeneous, particulate-laden exhaust emission produced by a compression-ignition (CI) engine as a direct consequence of **incomplete, partial, or non-ideal combustion** of the diesel fuel-air charge inside the cylinder. Under the **Bharat Stage (BS-VI) emission norms** mandated by the Government of India and aligned with the European Euro-6 standard, smoke is quantified as a regulated pollutant and is fundamentally classified by its **color**, which is a direct diagnostic indicator of the underlying mechanical, fuel-system, or combustion-chamber fault.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Smoke analysis is a **non-destructive diagnostic technique** used by service engineers to identify engine faults rapidly without dismantling components. The color, density, and odor of exhaust smoke are mapped to specific system failures.

The four canonical smoke colors recognized in standard automotive diagnostic practice (SAE J2150, ISO 5677) are:

1. **White Smoke** — Indicates unburnt fuel, water vapor, or low cylinder temperature.
2. **Blue Smoke** — Indicates the presence of **lubricating oil** in the combustion chamber.
3. **Black Smoke** — Indicates an **excess of fuel** relative to available oxygen (rich mixture) or poor atomization.
4. **Grey Smoke** — A transitional category indicating **partial combustion** and often a precursor to black smoke under load.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Campfire Test:**
> Imagine lighting a campfire. If the wood is **dry and the air supply is strong**, the smoke rises as a thin, almost invisible white plume (mostly water vapor) and you hear a healthy *crackle* — this is analogous to a **well-tuned cold diesel**. If you pour **used motor oil** onto the fire, it produces a **thick, bluish, foul-smelling smoke** — this is exactly what happens in a cylinder with worn piston rings or valve guides. If you **stack the wood too densely** and starve the fire of air, you get **dark, black, sooty smoke** from incomplete combustion — the same chemistry occurs in a diesel engine with a clogged air filter, faulty injector, or overloaded condition. **The smoke is the engine's "voice" — learning to listen to its color saves diagnostic time.**

## 1.3 Standard Smoke Measurement Metrics

The following standardized metrics are used in KTU-recommended textbooks and Indian emission regulations (CMVR / AIS-137):

- **Opacity (%, percent)**: The percentage of a light beam blocked by the smoke. Measured by an **opacimeter** per ISO 11614.
- **Bosch Smoke Number (BSN, 0–10 scale)**: A filter-paper darkness scale where **0 = no smoke** and **10 = heavy black smoke**.
- **Hartridge Smoke Unit (HSU, 0–100)**: Measured using the Hartridge smokemeter, commonly used in workshop practice.
- **Particulate Mass (g/kWh or mg/m³)**: The gravimetric mass of solid particulates per unit work output, the regulatory metric under BS-VI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Always state the **color, density, and engine condition** (cold start, idle, part load, full load) when describing smoke. Partial marks are awarded for incomplete descriptions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Diesel Combustion

A diesel engine operates on the **Compression Ignition (CI) principle** developed by Rudolf Diesel (1893). Unlike a spark-ignition (SI) petrol engine, the diesel engine compresses only air to a high pressure and temperature (compression ratio typically **15:1 to 22:1**, peak cylinder temperature **700 °C to 900 °C**), then injects fuel directly into the hot compressed air. The fuel auto-ignites due to the high temperature.

For a properly tuned engine, the combustion follows:

$$ \text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left( x + \frac{y}{4} \right) \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat} $$

This is **stoichiometric, complete combustion**, requiring no visible smoke. Smoke appears when this ideal condition is violated, which happens in three principal failure modes:

### Mode 1 — Fuel-Rich (Insufficient Air → Black Smoke)

When the actual air-fuel ratio falls below the stoichiometric value, there is insufficient oxygen to fully oxidize the carbon in the fuel. Carbon then forms **soot particles (elemental C)** rather than CO₂.

$$ 2\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CO} $$

$$ \text{C} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \text{(preferred, requires excess O}_2\text{)} $$

$$ \text{C (unburnt)} \longrightarrow \text{Soot particles (black smoke)} $$

### Mode 2 — Oil Contamination (Blue Smoke)

When lubricating oil enters the combustion chamber (past worn piston rings, valve stem seals, or turbo seals), it burns incompletely, producing a characteristic blue tint from the high molecular-weight hydrocarbon intermediates in the flame.

### Mode 3 — Cold Combustion / Liquid Fuel Droplets (White Smoke)

When the cylinder walls and combustion gases are below the auto-ignition temperature of the fuel, the injected fuel does not fully vaporize or combust. The unburnt fuel droplets and water vapor are exhausted as **white smoke**. This is normal during cold start but abnormal at operating temperature.

## 2.2 The Four Smoke Colors — Diagnostic Matrix

| **Smoke Color** | **Primary Cause** | **Secondary Causes** | **Engine State** | **Severity** |
|-----------------|-------------------|----------------------|------------------|--------------|
| **White (light, dissipates)** | Normal cold-start condensation | None | Cold engine, first 1–2 min | Normal |
| **White (persistent, thick, sweet smell)** | Coolant leak into combustion chamber | Blown head gasket, cracked cylinder head, cracked block | Hot engine | **High** |
| **White (milky, persistent)** | Water in fuel | Contaminated diesel, condensation in fuel tank | Any state | **High** |
| **Blue (under load)** | Oil burning — worn piston rings | Worn cylinder bore, stuck rings, high mileage | Hot, loaded | **Medium–High** |
| **Blue (on startup only)** | Oil past valve stem seals | Worn valve guides, hardened seals | After long idle | **Medium** |
| **Blue (constant, all conditions)** | Oil overfill, turbo seal failure | PCV system failure, oil in intake | Any | **Medium–High** |
| **Black (under acceleration)** | Rich mixture — over-fueling | Faulty injector, incorrect pump timing, restricted air filter, EGR valve stuck open | Loaded | **High** |
| **Black (constant, all loads)** | Severely restricted air intake | Clogged air filter, blocked intake manifold, turbo failure | Any | **High** |
| **Grey (light, under load)** | Marginal rich combustion | Marginal injector issue, slight overloading | Loaded | **Low–Medium** |
| **Grey (after long idle, sudden acceleration)** | Oil residue on cylinder walls | Worn rings, marginal compression | Transition state | **Medium** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Table:** White smoke is the only color that is **acceptable under one condition** (cold-start) and **critical under another** (persistent white = head gasket failure). The KTU examiner expects students to distinguish between these.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are essential for KTU Module 2 examination questions on diesel smoke and combustion diagnostics. **Memorize these.**

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Unit / Range** | **Application** |
|--------------|-------------|--------------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Stoichiometric AFR (diesel, C₁₂H₂₆)** | $(A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{4.76\,(x + y/4)}{M_{fuel}/M_{air}} \times 28.97$ | $x, y$ = C, H atoms in fuel | $\approx 14.5 : 1$ | Theoretical minimum air for complete combustion |
| **Actual AFR (diesel, typical)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | $\lambda$ = excess air factor | $\lambda \in [1.2, 2.0]$ for diesel | Lean-burn operation; $\lambda < 1$ = rich = black smoke |
| **Smoke Opacity** | $N = \left( 1 - \dfrac{I}{I_0} \right) \times 100\,\%$ | $I_0$ = incident light, $I$ = transmitted light | $0\% \leq N \leq 100\%$ | Measured by opacimeter (ISO 11614) |
| **Light Transmittance** | $T = \dfrac{I}{I_0}$ | Ratio of transmitted to incident light | $0 \leq T \leq 1$ | Used in photoelectric smokemeters |
| **Bosch Smoke Number** | $BSN = 10\,(1 - R_d)$ | $R_d$ = filter reflectance (white = 1, black = 0) | $0 \leq BSN \leq 10$ | Bosch smokemeter (workshop standard) |
| **Particulate Mass (regulatory)** | $PM = \dfrac{m_{filter}}{V_{sampled}}$ | $m_{filter}$ = mass collected, $V$ = sampled volume | $\text{mg/m}^3$ | BS-VI / Euro-6 limit: **10 mg/kWh** for HD diesel |
| **Smoke Density (Hartridge)** | $HSU = 100 \times (1 - T)$ | $T$ = transmittance through exhaust column | $0 \leq HSU \leq 100$ | Hartridge smokemeter |
| **Carbon Monoxide Emission (mass)** | $CO = \dfrac{11 \times (CO\%) \times 10^{-2} \times M_{exh}}{V_{exh}}$ | $CO\%$ = measured CO concentration | $\text{g/kWh}$ | Strongly correlated with black smoke (rich mix) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL — No Pipe Symbol in Tables:** In all KTU answer scripts and notes, mathematical absolute-value bars like $\vert x \vert$ must be written using **\vert** or **\mid** in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. The formulas above use $I/I_0$ (fraction form) instead of $\vert I/I_0 \vert$ to remain table-safe.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The diagnostic logic of smoke color analysis is actively used in:

- **Road-side vehicle inspection** by RTO / traffic police under BS-VI compliance.
- **Fleet maintenance depots** of KSRTC, KSRTC-SWIFT, Kerala State Road Transport, and private bus operators.
- **Workshop troubleshooting** before dismantling an engine, saving **labour cost and downtime**.
- **Engine R&D** at manufacturers like Tata Motors, Mahindra, Ashok Leyland, and Eicher for emission calibration.
- **Emission testing centres** authorized by the Kerala Motor Vehicle Department (MVD).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Connection:** Modern diesel vehicles (post-2017, BS-IV onward) are equipped with **DPF (Diesel Particulate Filters)** and **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)** systems. Smoke analysis is no longer just a mechanical diagnostic — it is a regulatory check. Persistent smoke under BS-VI can trigger the **OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics)** dashboard warning and reduce engine power via **limp-home mode**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Diesel (C₁₂H₂₃ as Average Diesel)

Diesel fuel is a complex mixture of paraffins (CₙH₂ₙ₊₂), with an average empirical formula approximated as **C₁₂H₂₃** (cetane, C₁₆H₃₄, is also commonly used). We will derive the stoichiometric AFR for the representative average **C₁₂H₂₆** (dodecane, a diesel surrogate).

### Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion equation

$$ \text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{26} + a\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

### Step 2 — Balance the Carbon atoms

For Carbon (C): 12 atoms on the left $\Rightarrow$ 12 CO₂ on the right.

$$ b = 12 $$

### Step 3 — Balance the Hydrogen atoms

For Hydrogen (H): 26 atoms on the left $\Rightarrow$ 13 H₂O on the right.

$$ 2c = 26 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; c = 13 $$

### Step 4 — Balance the Oxygen atoms

For Oxygen (O): $2a$ atoms on the left $\Rightarrow$ $2b + c$ atoms on the right.

$$ 2a = 2b + c $$

Substituting the known values:

$$ 2a = 2(12) + 13 $$

$$ 2a = 24 + 13 $$

$$ 2a = 37 $$

$$ a = \dfrac{37}{2} = 18.5 $$

### Step 5 — Final balanced equation

$$ \text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{26} + 18.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 12\,\text{CO}_2 + 13\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

### Step 6 — Convert O₂ requirement to Air requirement

Air is approximately **21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume**, so to supply 18.5 moles of O₂, we need:

$$ \text{Air moles} = \dfrac{18.5}{0.21} = 88.095 \text{ moles of air per mole of fuel} $$

### Step 7 — Convert to Mass Air-Fuel Ratio

Molar masses:
- Air $\approx$ **28.97 g/mol**
- C₁₂H₂₆ $\approx$ $12(12) + 26(1) = 170$ g/mol

Mass of air required:

$$ m_{air} = 88.095 \times 28.97 = 2552.1 \text{ g} $$

Mass of fuel:

$$ m_{fuel} = 1 \times 170 = 170 \text{ g} $$

### Step 8 — Stoichiometric AFR

$$ (A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{2552.1}{170} $$

$$ \boxed{(A/F)_{stoich} \approx 15.01 : 1} $$

This matches the KTU textbook value of approximately **14.5 : 1 to 15.0 : 1** for diesel fuel. The small variation is due to the actual H/C ratio of real diesel (typically 1.8 to 1.9, not exactly 2.17 of dodecane).

### Step 9 — Excess Air Factor for Diesel

Diesel engines normally operate **lean** (excess air). Typical operating excess air factor:

$$ \lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} $$

For $\lambda = 1.5$ (typical mid-load diesel):

$$ (A/F)_{actual} = 1.5 \times 15.01 = 22.5 : 1 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smoke Threshold:** Black smoke (soot) begins to form when $\lambda$ drops below approximately **1.2 to 1.3** for a typical DI diesel engine. Below $\lambda = 1.0$ (rich), soot emissions rise exponentially.

## 3.2 Derivation: Smoke Opacity from Light Transmittance

The opacimeter passes a light beam of intensity $I_0$ through a column of exhaust smoke and measures the transmitted intensity $I$. The relationship between transmittance, absorbance, and opacity is derived from the **Beer–Lambert Law**.

### Step 1 — Define Transmittance

$$ T = \dfrac{I}{I_0} $$

### Step 2 — Apply Beer–Lambert Law

$$ I = I_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot L \cdot c} $$

where $k$ = extinction coefficient, $L$ = optical path length, $c$ = soot concentration.

### Step 3 — Solve for Concentration

$$ T = e^{-k L c} $$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$ \ln(T) = -k L c $$

$$ c = -\dfrac{\ln(T)}{kL} $$

### Step 4 — Define Opacity (N)

Opacity $N$ is the fraction of light blocked:

$$ N = 1 - T $$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$ N\,\text{(\%)} = \left(1 - \dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100 $$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

A diesel engine emits smoke that reduces transmitted light from $I_0 = 100$ units to $I = 35$ units.

$$ T = \dfrac{35}{100} = 0.35 $$

$$ N = (1 - 0.35) \times 100 = 65\% $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Opacity values above **N = 50%** are usually considered **excessive** and would fail the BS-VI / CMVR (Central Motor Vehicle Rules) free-acceleration test. KTU expects you to state the regulatory threshold (typically **N ≤ 50%** for in-service vehicles, **N ≤ 30%** for new type approval) when discussing smoke.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Smoke Color → Fault → Diagnostic Action

This consolidated **KTU-style decision table** is a high-yield revision tool. **Memorize the fault column** as it is the most commonly asked sub-part.

| **Smoke Color** | **Likely Fault Component** | **Confirmatory Diagnostic Test** | **Repair Action** |
|-----------------|----------------------------|-----------------------------------|-------------------|
| White (cold, clears in 2 min) | None — normal condensation | Visual + temperature check | No action |
| White (persistent, hot engine) | Head gasket, head, block | Coolant pressure test, compression + leak-down test, CO₂ in coolant test | Replace head gasket, skim head, weld/replace block |
| White (milky, fuel side) | Water in diesel, tank contamination | Drain fuel, check water separator, lab test of fuel sample | Drain tank, replace fuel filter, refill from certified source |
| Blue (under acceleration) | Worn piston rings, glazed cylinder | Compression test, leak-down test, borescope inspection | Rebore + new rings, or engine overhaul |
| Blue (on startup only) | Valve stem seals hardened/worn | Vacuum test on intake, oil consumption measurement | Replace valve stem seals (decel job, cylinder head removal) |
| Blue (constant, all loads) | Turbocharger oil seal, oil overfill | Visual turbo inspection, oil level check, crankcase pressure | Replace turbo seals, drain excess oil |
| Black (under load) | Over-fueling, injector drip, pump timing off | Injector pop test, pump timing check with dial gauge, scan tool for rail pressure | Re-time pump, recondition/replace injectors |
