| Black (constant) | Clogged air filter, blocked intake, turbo failure | Air filter inspection, intake pressure test, turbo boost test | Replace air filter, clean intake, replace turbo |
| Grey (under load, intermittent) | Marginal injector, marginal boost | Cylinder contribution test (engine analyser), boost pressure log | Clean injectors, check intercooler, check boost |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Rule of Three" for KTU Board Answers:** When a KTU question asks *"What does black smoke under load indicate?"*, structure your answer in **three layers**:
> 1. **Symptom** (what you see),
> 2. **System** (which system is responsible — fuel / air / mechanical),
> 3. **Component** (the specific failed part within the system).
> This 3-layer structure scores full marks on KTU valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Diesel Smoke Generation & Diagnostic Logic

The following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps the relationships between engine subsystems, failure modes, smoke colors, and diagnostic actions. This replaces complex physical drawings (which are not feasible in Mermaid) with a high-clarity logical map.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DIESEL ENGINE COMBUSTION SUBSYSTEMS] --> B[FUEL SYSTEM]
    A --> C[AIR INTAKE SYSTEM]
    A --> D[MECHANICAL SEALING SYSTEM]
    A --> E[COOLING SYSTEM]
    A --> F[EXHAUST AFTERTREATMENT]

    B --> B1[Pump Timing Fault]
    B --> B2[Injector Spray Fault]
    B --> B3[Excess Fuel Delivery]
    B1 --> R1[BLACK SMOKE RICH MIXTURE]
    B2 --> R1
    B3 --> R1

    C --> C1[Clogged Air Filter]
    C --> C2[Turbocharger Failure]
    C --> C3[Blocked Intake Manifold]
    C1 --> R1
    C2 --> R1
    C3 --> R1

    D --> D1[Worn Piston Rings]
    D --> D2[Worn Valve Guides]
    D --> D3[Turbo Oil Seal Leak]
    D1 --> R2[BLUE SMOKE OIL BURNING]
    D2 --> R2
    D3 --> R2

    E --> E1[Blown Head Gasket]
    E --> E2[Cracked Cylinder Head]
    E1 --> R3[WHITE SMOKE COOLANT LEAK]
    E2 --> R3

    F --> F1[DPF Clogged]
    F --> F2[Turbo Lag on Cold Start]
    F1 --> R1
    F2 --> R4[GREY OR WHITE SMOKE COLD START]

    R1 --> ACT1[DIAGNOSTIC Confirm over fueling, check air filter, scan rail pressure]
    R2 --> ACT2[DIAGNOSTIC Compression test, leak down test, oil consumption log]
    R3 --> ACT3[DIAGNOSTIC Coolant pressure test, CO2 in coolant test, compression]
    R4 --> ACT4[DIAGNOSTIC Wait 2 min, repeat test, check DPF regen logs]

    ACT1 --> REP1[REPAIR Re time pump, clean injectors, replace air filter]
    ACT2 --> REP2[REPAIR Rebore, new rings, replace valve seals, new turbo seals]
    ACT3 --> REP3[REPAIR Replace head gasket, skim head, replace head]
    ACT4 --> REP4[REPAIR No action if clears, else force DPF regen or replace DPF]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Decision Tree: Identifying Smoke Color at Workshop

This decision tree gives a KTU student a step-by-step **diagnostic flow** to identify the root cause from a given smoke color. It is highly exam-relevant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[OBSERVED SMOKE AT EXHAUST] --> Q1{Is engine COLD or HOT}

    Q1 -->|COLD| Q2{Does smoke clear within 2 minutes}
    Q1 -->|HOT| Q3{Is smoke WHITE BLUE or BLACK}

    Q2 -->|YES| N1[NORMAL Cold start condensation no action]
    Q2 -->|NO| X1[WHITE PERSISTENT Check head gasket coolant pressure test]

    Q3 -->|WHITE| Q4{Sweet smell or coolant loss}
    Q3 -->|BLUE| Q5{On startup only or all loads}
    Q3 -->|BLACK| Q6{Only under load or constant}

    Q4 -->|YES| X2[COOLANT LEAK IN CYLINDER Head gasket head or block]
    Q4 -->|NO| X3[WATER IN FUEL Drain tank replace filter]

    Q5 -->|STARTUP ONLY| X4[VALVE STEM SEALS Replace seals decel job]
    Q5 -->|ALL LOADS| X5[PISTON RINGS OR TURBO SEAL Compression test oil check]

    Q6 -->|UNDER LOAD| X6[FUEL OR AIR SYSTEM Check pump timing injectors air filter]
    Q6 -->|CONSTANT| X7[SEVERELY RESTRICTED AIR Intake blockage turbo failure]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Timeline: Smoke Color Through Engine Warm-up

This diagram visualizes how a **healthy diesel engine** emits smoke of changing color as it warms up — a frequently tested concept.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[TIME 0 Cold Start]:::cold --> S0[White smoke normal water vapor and unburnt fuel]
    S0 --> T1[TIME 1 to 2 min Warm up]
    T1 --> S1[Smoke clears transparent exhaust normal]
    S1 --> T2[TIME 2 min to 10 min Idle warm]
    T2 --> S2[Transparent to light grey no load]
    S2 --> T3[TIME 10 min plus Loaded operation]
    T3 --> S3[Transparent to faint grey slight soot normal under load]
    S3 --> T4[TIME Steady state full load]
    T4 --> S4[If black blue or persistent white at this stage FAULT present]

    classDef cold fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1B4F72,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this for KTU:** The diagram shows that **white smoke is normal ONLY in the first 1–2 minutes**. After warm-up, ANY persistent smoke (white, blue, or black) is a fault. KTU examiners reward students who state the time-bound nature of normal white smoke.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMUT635]

**Q: List the three main colors of diesel engine exhaust smoke and state the primary cause of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three main colors of diesel engine exhaust smoke and their primary causes are:

1. **White Smoke (1 mark):** Caused by **unburnt fuel droplets** or **water vapor** in the exhaust. During cold start, this is normal due to incomplete vaporization. Persistent white smoke at operating temperature indicates **coolant leakage** into the combustion chamber (head gasket failure) or **water in the fuel**.

2. **Black Smoke (1 mark):** Caused by **incomplete combustion of fuel** due to a **rich air–fuel mixture** (excess fuel or insufficient air). Common causes include **faulty injectors**, **incorrect fuel pump timing**, **clogged air filter**, **turbocharger failure**, or **engine overloading**.

3. **Blue Smoke (1 mark):** Caused by **lubricating oil entering the combustion chamber** and burning. Common sources are **worn piston rings**, **worn cylinder bore**, **faulty valve stem seals**, **turbocharger oil seal failure**, or **oil overfilling**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part A:** Students often write only *"black smoke = rich mixture"* without naming the **specific component failures** (injector, pump, filter). The KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates **at least 0.5 marks** to identifying the faulty component, not just the general condition. Always name the component.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Model Paper]

**Q: Differentiate between white smoke observed during cold start and white smoke observed at normal operating temperature. What is the diagnostic significance of this difference?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Cold Start White Smoke** | **Hot Engine White Smoke** |
|---------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Cause (1 mark)** | Normal condensation of water vapor and incomplete vaporization of injected fuel droplets due to low cylinder and combustion-chamber wall temperature | Coolant entering the combustion chamber via a **blown head gasket**, **cracked cylinder head**, or **cracked engine block**; OR **water-contaminated diesel fuel** |
| **Duration (0.5 mark)** | Disappears within 1–2 minutes as engine warms up | Persists indefinitely as long as the fault is present |
| **Diagnostic Significance (1 mark)** | Indicates normal engine operation; no service action required | Indicates a **critical mechanical failure** requiring immediate teardown — compression test, coolant pressure test, and head gasket replacement are mandatory |
| **Smell** | Mild diesel / fuel odor | **Sweet smell** (coolant / ethylene glycol) if head gasket; no sweet smell if water-in-fuel |

**Key Takeaway (0.5 mark):** The **duration and engine temperature** at which white smoke is observed is the primary diagnostic discriminator — a KTU examiner expects this distinction explicitly stated.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule — Module Choice:** For each Part B question, the paper provides two alternatives (e.g., 11(a) OR 11(b)). You attempt **only one** of the two. Both questions below are full **14-mark** sub-divided questions with sub-parts **(a) — 7 marks** and **(b) — 7 marks**, exactly as per the KTU University Examination paper pattern.

---

### **Question B1 — Choice A** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat labeled diagram the various colors of diesel engine exhaust smoke, the engine conditions under which they appear, and the specific component failures that cause each color. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diesel engine under load emits smoke that reduces the intensity of a light beam passing through the exhaust from $I_0 = 100$ units to $I = 25$ units. Calculate the **(i)** transmittance, **(ii)** opacity percentage, and **(iii)** Bosch Smoke Number assuming a filter reflectance of $R_d = 0.30$. State whether this smoke level would pass a typical free-acceleration emission test (threshold opacity = 50%). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**(1 mark) — Introduction:**
Diesel engine exhaust smoke is a key diagnostic indicator of the engine's combustion health. Under ideal stoichiometric combustion, the exhaust is invisible. Visible smoke indicates incomplete combustion, oil burning, or coolant ingestion.

**(1 mark) — White Smoke (Cold Start, Normal):**
Occurs during the first 1–2 minutes of cold operation. Cause: condensation of water vapor and incomplete vaporization of injected fuel due to low cylinder-wall temperature. **No fault; no action required.**

**(1 mark) — White Smoke (Hot Engine, Abnormal):**
- Sweet smell + coolant loss = **blown head gasket / cracked head / cracked block**.
- Milky residue + no coolant loss = **water in fuel**.
- Confirm by **coolant pressure test, CO₂ test of coolant, fuel drain and inspection**.

**(1 mark) — Blue Smoke (Oil Burning):**
- Worn piston rings or cylinder bore → oil enters the combustion chamber.
- Worn valve stem seals → oil seeps down valve guides.
- Turbocharger oil seal failure → oil enters intake or exhaust.
- Oil overfill → oil pushed past rings by crankcase pressure.
- Confirm by **compression test, leak-down test, oil consumption measurement, borescope**.

**(1 mark) — Black Smoke (Rich Mixture):**
- Over-fueling: faulty injector, incorrect pump timing, stuck fuel-pressure regulator.
- Insufficient air: clogged air filter, blocked intake, failed turbo, leaking intercooler.
- Overloading: engine driven beyond rated capacity.
- Confirm by **injector pop test, pump timing check, intake vacuum test, scan-tool rail-pressure reading**.

**(1 mark) — Grey Smoke:**
- Transitional state, often marginal rich combustion.
- Sometimes seen as oil residue burns off during warm-up.
- Confirm by **cylinder contribution test, smoke meter reading under load**.

**(1 mark) — Conclusion / KTU Wrap-up:**
The color of diesel smoke is a direct map to the failing system. **White = coolant/fuel issue, Blue = lubrication system, Black = fuel/air system, Grey = transitional/early fault.** A skilled technician uses this color-code as the **first step** in a tiered diagnostic process before resorting to instrument-based tests.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**

$$ I_0 = 100 \text{ units}, \quad I = 25 \text{ units}, \quad R_d = 0.30, \quad N_{threshold} = 50\% $$

**(i) Transmittance (2 marks):**

$$ T = \dfrac{I}{I_0} $$

$$ T = \dfrac{25}{100} $$

$$ \boxed{T = 0.25 \quad \text{(or 25\%)}} $$

**(ii) Opacity (3 marks):**

$$ N = (1 - T) \times 100 $$

$$ N = (1 - 0.25) \times 100 $$

$$ N = 0.75 \times 100 $$

$$ \boxed{N = 75\%} $$

**(iii) Bosch Smoke Number (2 marks):**

$$ BSN = 10 \times (1 - R_d) $$

$$ BSN = 10 \times (1 - 0.30) $$

$$ BSN = 10 \times 0.70 $$

$$ \boxed{BSN = 7.0 } $$

**Pass / Fail Conclusion (included in the 7-mark total):**

Since the opacity $N = 75\%$ **exceeds the regulatory threshold of 50%**, the vehicle **FAILS** the free-acceleration emission test. The cause of black smoke must be investigated and rectified before re-testing.

---

### **Question B2 — Choice B** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a flow diagram, describe the step-by-step workshop procedure for diagnosing the cause of **black smoke** in a turbocharged diesel engine. Include at least **five** specific tests in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The stoichiometric air–fuel ratio for a typical diesel fuel (C₁₂H₂₆) is to be determined. **(i)** Derive the balanced combustion equation. **(ii)** Calculate the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio on a mass basis. **(iii)** If the actual measured AFR under full load is 13:1, calculate the excess air factor $\lambda$ and state whether smoke formation is likely. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**(1 mark) — Symptom Confirmation:**
Black smoke is observed during acceleration or full-load operation. The technician must first confirm it is not normal transient smoke (which lasts <2 seconds during tip-in). Sustained black smoke indicates a **rich mixture**.

**(1 mark) — Step 1: Visual Inspection of Air Path:**
Remove and inspect the **air filter element**. A clogged filter restricts air supply, causing a rich mixture. Check the air intake hose for collapse or blockage. Inspect the **turbocharger** for visible damage or seized vanes.

**(1 mark) — Step 2: Turbo Boost Pressure Test:**
Connect a **boost pressure gauge** to the intake manifold. Rev the engine to rated RPM. Compare measured boost (e.g., should be ~1.2 bar for a typical 2.0L TDI) with manufacturer's specification. Low boost → turbo failure → black smoke.

**(1 mark) — Step 3: Injector Back-Leakage and Spray Test:**
Remove injectors and test on a **pop-tester** (injector tester). Observe spray pattern: should be **fine, symmetrical, atomized mist**, not a solid stream or dripping. Measure back-leakage over 10 seconds: should be < 1 mL per injector.

**(1 mark) — Step 4: Fuel Pump Timing and Delivery Check:**
Use a **dial gauge and timing bridge** to check injection advance. Use a **pump bench test** to verify individual cylinder delivery volumes. Off-time injection or unequal delivery = black smoke.

**(1 mark) — Step 5: EGR and Aftertreatment Inspection:**
Check that the **EGR valve** is not stuck open (which can introduce excess exhaust gas). Inspect **DPF** for clogging (back-pressure gauge reading). Check **intercooler** for cracks (boost leak test).

**(1 mark) — Conclusion and Repair Loop:**
Based on test results, repair the identified fault (clean/replace filter, replace turbo, re-time pump, recondition injectors, replace DPF). Re-test with smokemeter to confirm opacity ≤ 50%.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Balanced Combustion Equation (3 marks):**

Combustion of dodecane (C₁₂H₂₆) with oxygen:

$$ \text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{26} + a\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

Carbon balance:

$$ b = 12 $$

Hydrogen balance:

$$ 2c = 26 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; c = 13 $$

Oxygen balance:

$$ 2a = 2b + c = 2(12) + 13 = 37 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a = 18.5 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{26} + 18.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 12\,\text{CO}_2 + 13\,\text{H}_2\text{O}} $$

**(ii) Mass-based Stoichiometric AFR (2 marks):**

Moles of air required (air is 21% O₂):

$$ n_{air} = \dfrac{18.5}{0.21} = 88.095 \text{ mol} $$

Mass of air:

$$ m_{air} = 88.095 \times 28.97 = 2552.1 \text{ g} $$

Mass of fuel (M(C₁₂H₂₆) = 12(12) + 26(1) = 170 g/mol):

$$ m_{fuel} = 170 \text{ g} $$

$$ (A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{2552.1}{170} $$

$$ \boxed{(A/F)_{stoich} = 15.01 : 1} $$

**(iii) Excess Air Factor and Smoke Likelihood (2 marks):**

$$ \lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} = \dfrac{13}{15.01} $$

$$ \boxed{\lambda = 0.866} $$

Since $\lambda < 1$, the mixture is **rich** (insufficient air for complete combustion). Black smoke formation is **highly likely**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B (b):** Students often forget to **state the conclusion** ("smoke is likely" / "smoke is unlikely") explicitly. The KTU valuation key deducts 0.5 to 1 mark if the numerical answer is correct but the **engineering interpretation** is missing. **Always end a calculation with a one-line conclusion.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Topic-Wise Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks in Module 2 Smoke Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Grey and Black smoke causes.** Grey smoke is **not** a synonym for black smoke. It is a separate diagnostic category indicating partial combustion.
> 2. **Forgetting engine state.** Always state **"cold engine"** or **"under load"** or **"at idle"** when describing smoke.
> 3. **Naming only the system, not the component.** Writing *"fuel system fault"* is incomplete. You must write *"faulty injector nozzle"* or *"incorrect fuel pump timing"*.
> 4. **Omitting the regulatory threshold.** When discussing opacity, you must state the BS-VI / CMVR limit (e.g., **N ≤ 50%** for in-service free-acceleration).
> 5. **Skipping the diagnostic test.** KTU values the **confirmation step** (compression test, leak-down, coolant pressure) — not just the symptom → cause mapping. Always include *how* you would confirm the diagnosis.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Black smoke = Rich mixture** (excess fuel OR insufficient air).
  - Fuel side: over-fueling, injector drip, pump timing advanced.
  - Air side: clogged air filter, failed turbo, blocked intake, EGR stuck open.
  - Engine side: overloading beyond rated capacity.
- **Blue smoke = Oil burning.**
  - Piston rings (worn / stuck / glazed), cylinder bore wear, valve stem seals, turbo oil seal, oil overfill.
- **White smoke (cold, brief) = Normal.** Unburnt fuel + water vapor.
- **White smoke (persistent, hot) = Coolant leak** (head gasket / head / block) OR **water in fuel** (sweet smell → coolant; milky fuel → water-in-fuel).
- **Grey smoke = Partial combustion** OR **transitional state** during warm-up / light load.
- **Smoke measurement tools:** Opacimeter (opacity %), Bosch smokemeter (BSN 0–10), Hartridge smokemeter (HSU 0–100).
- **Stoichiometric AFR (diesel, C₁₂H₂₆):** approximately **15.01 : 1**.
- **Smoke threshold (lambda):** Black soot forms when **$\lambda < 1.2$ to $1.3$**; severe smoke when **$\lambda < 1.0$**.
- **Opacity formula:** $N = (1 - I/I_0) \times 100\%$.
- **Bosch Smoke Number:** $BSN = 10 \times (1 - R_d)$.
- **BS-VI free-acceleration limit:** **N ≤ 50%** opacity for in-service HD diesel vehicles.
- **Diagnostic order for any smoke color:** (1) Visual confirm, (2) State engine condition, (3) Identify system, (4) Identify component, (5) Confirmatory test, (6) Repair, (7) Re-test with smokemeter.
- **Mandatory words in KTU answers:** "Compression test", "leak-down test", "coolant pressure test", "injector pop test", "boost pressure test", "BS-VI / CMVR threshold".
- **Cylinder head repair (for white smoke):** Replace head gasket → reskim head surface → torque to spec in correct sequence → retest.
- **Piston ring repair (for blue smoke):** Rebore cylinder → fit oversized rings → hone → measure oil consumption after run-in.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra for Module 2 Smoke Questions:**
> *"Color = Diagnosis, Density = Severity, Engine State = Context, Confirmatory Test = Marks."* Memorize this four-part formula and apply it to every smoke-related question. It is the KTU board examiner's mental model for awarding full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Services in Diesel Engine Injection System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Services in Diesel Engine Injection System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Diesel Engine Injection System Servicing** refers to the systematic, scheduled, and corrective maintenance procedures performed on the high-pressure diesel fuel delivery sub-system, encompassing the **fuel feed pump (lift pump)**, the **injection pump (distributor/inline type)**, the **high-pressure delivery lines**, the **injectors/nozzle-holders**, and the **fuel filtration assembly**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT635 – Module 2)**, servicing of the diesel injection system is classified into three categories:
> - **Routine / Periodic Service** (e.g., filter change, bleed operation)
> - **Diagnostic / Inspection Service** (e.g., injection timing verification, nozzle spray pattern analysis)
> - **Overhaul / Bench Calibration Service** (e.g., pump recalibration on a test bench, nozzle opening pressure reset)

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Pressure Range (Standard KTU Reference):**
> - Conventional Indirect Injection (IDI) systems: **100 – 350 bar**
> - Direct Injection (DI) rotary/distributor pumps: **400 – 600 bar**
> - Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi) systems: **1,350 – 2,500 bar**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine the diesel fuel injection system as the **"heart and veins"** of a diesel engine:
- The **fuel tank** is the blood reservoir.
- The **lift pump** acts as the *first-stage booster* (low-pressure feeder, typically 0.5 – 2.5 bar).
- The **filter** is the *kidney* that purifies the fuel.
- The **injection pump** is the *main heart* that pressurizes fuel to 200 – 1,800+ bar.
- The **high-pressure lines** are the *arteries*.
- The **injector/nozzle** is the *atomizer/nozzle* (like a perfume spray bottle) that finely atomizes the fuel into the combustion chamber.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy for atomization:** Hold a garden hose with your thumb halfway over the opening. As you press harder, water shoots faster and finer. The injector does the *same* with diesel — high pressure = finer droplets = better combustion = more power and lower emissions.

**Why servicing is critical:** A 1-micron (0.001 mm) impurity lodged in the nozzle needle valve seat can cause the injector to **leak, drip, or atomize poorly** — leading to power loss, black smoke, hard starting, and **diesel knock**. Hence, periodic servicing preserves the atomization quality.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Component-Wise Service Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Fuel Lift (Feed) Pump Service
- **Function:** Draws fuel from the tank and supplies it to the injection pump at low pressure (typically **0.5 – 2.5 bar**).
- **Service Tasks:**
  1. Inspect diaphragm/rotor for cracks (mechanical type) or vane/rotor wear (electrical type).
  2. Check hand-primer operation (manual lever).
  3. Verify suction line vacuum is within spec.
  4. Replace sealing gaskets.
  5. Bleed air from the suction side.

#### 2.1.2 Fuel Filter Service
- **Two-stage filtration** is standard on most KTU-curriculum engines (e.g., Bosch/Stanadyne):
  - **Stage 1 (Primary)**: Water-separating filter — removes **bulk water** via a drain plug.
  - **Stage 2 (Secondary)**: Micron-rated filter — typically **5 – 10 micron** filtration.
- **Service:** Replace filter element every **10,000 – 20,000 km** or per OEM schedule. Drain water daily/weekly in tropical climates.

#### 2.1.3 Injection Pump Service (Inline / Distributor / Rotary)
- **Bench Calibration Procedure (Most KTU-favoured exam topic):**
  1. Mount pump on a **calibration test bench** with calibrated fuel reservoir.
  2. Drive pump at rated speed (typically **500 – 1500 rpm**) using a variable-speed motor.
  3. Connect a **Nozzle Tester (pressure gauge + nozzle assembly)** to each delivery port.
  4. **Static Timing**: Set the **pump plunger** to the start of delivery at the calibrated lift point.
  5. **Dynamic Calibration**: Adjust **pump delivery volume (mm³/stroke)** using the **fuel regulating collar (FRA – Fuel Regulation Adjustment)**.
  6. **Advance Mechanism**: Check the **automatic advance (centrifugal timer)** by plotting advance angle vs. pump speed.
  7. Verify **full load delivery**, **idle delivery**, **smoke limiter setting**.

#### 2.1.4 Injector / Nozzle Service
- **Critical Services:**
  - **Nozzle Opening Pressure (NOP) Test**: Using a **Nozzle Tester**, manually pump and note the pressure at which the nozzle *pops* and sprays. Reset to OEM spec by adding/removing shims.
  - **Back-leakage Test**: Holds pressure for 10 seconds — pressure drop should not exceed **20 bar** (or per OEM).
  - **Chatter Test**: Rapid manual pumping should produce a clear, sharp *chatter* sound — indicates a healthy needle valve.
  - **Spray Pattern Inspection**: Should form a fine, symmetrical, atomized mist cone.
  - **Cleaning**: Use an **ultrasonic cleaner** with cleaning solution for **10 – 15 minutes**.

#### 2.1.5 Bleeding the Fuel System
- **Purpose:** Remove trapped air from the fuel lines.
- **Procedure:**
  1. Loosen bleed screws (typically 2–3) starting from the filter → lift pump → injection pump inlet → delivery line nuts at injectors.
  2. Operate the hand primer until air-free fuel flows out.
  3. Tighten bleed screws in sequence.
  4. Crank engine to verify smooth start.

#### 2.1.6 Injection Timing Service
- **Static Timing:** Align timing marks on the **pump** and **pump drive gear** with the engine's **TDC mark** on the flywheel.
- **Dynamic Timing:** Use a **dial indicator + spill timing method** or a **strobe light + magnetic pickup** to confirm delivery begins at the specified crank angle **BEFORE TDC (BTDC)**, typically **10° – 25° BTDC** for naturally aspirated diesels, **6° – 14° BTDC** for turbocharged engines.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Symbol | Formula / Relationship | Standard Unit | Typical KTU-Exam Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Injection Pressure (average) | $P_{inj}$ | $P_{inj} = \dfrac{F_{spring}}{A_{needle}}$ | bar / MPa | 200 – 1,800 bar |
| 2 | Spring Force | $F_{spring}$ | $F_{spring} = k \cdot x$ | N | varies |
| 3 | Nozzle Needle Lift | $x$ | measured by dial indicator | mm | **0.20 – 0.45 mm** |
| 4 | Pump Delivery Quantity | $Q_{d}$ | $Q_{d} = \dfrac{V \cdot N}{60 \cdot n_{cyl}}$ | mm³/stroke | OEM-specific |
| 5 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{v}$ | $\eta_{v} = \dfrac{Actual\ Delivery}{Theoretical\ Delivery} \times 100$ | % | **> 95 %** (healthy) |
| 6 | Spray Pattern Cone Angle | $\theta$ | $\theta = 2 \cdot \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{D/2}{L}\right)$ | degrees (°) | 4° – 15° |
| 7 | Filtration Rating | $\beta$ | $\beta_x = \dfrac{N_{upstream}}{N_{downstream}}$ at $x\ \mu m$ | dimensionless | **β₁₀ ≥ 75** (typical) |
| 8 | Injection Timing | $\theta_{BTDC}$ | measured BTDC for given rpm | degrees | 10° – 25° |
| 9 | Auto Advance (Centrifugal) | $\theta_{adv}$ | $\theta_{adv} = f(\omega_{pump})$ | degrees | 0° – 9° (typical) |
| 10 | Atomization Quality Index | $SMD$ | Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD) | μm | 5 – 25 μm (diesel) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention Used in KTU Valuation:**
> - Pressure: **bar** (preferred) or MPa. $1\ \text{bar} = 10^5\ \text{Pa} = 0.1\ \text{MPa}$.
> - Flow rate in pumps: **mm³/stroke** (or **cc/1000 strokes** for low-flow).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Automotive service workshops** rely on these procedures for warranty compliance and BS-VI/Euro-VI emission conformance.
- **Diesel locomotive & marine engines** use the same bench-calibration methodology at a larger scale.
- **Agricultural tractors** in India (Mahindra, John Deere, Sonalika) follow these maintenance schedules — making it a high-employability skill set.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Nozzle Opening Pressure (NOP) from Spring Mechanics

The opening pressure of a diesel injector is governed by the **axial force balance** on the needle valve.

**Step 1 — Free-Body of the Needle Valve**

Forces acting on the needle valve along its axis (downward) when closed:
- High-pressure fuel acts on the **differential pressure area** of the needle.

Forces acting upward (closing):
- Spring force set by the **adjustment shim(s)**.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium Condition (just before opening)**

At the instant of opening, the needle is in static equilibrium:

$$F_{fuel,\ axial} = F_{spring}$$

**Step 3 — Express Fuel Force**

$$P_{NOP} \cdot A_{eff} = F_{spring}$$

Where:
- $P_{NOP}$ = Nozzle Opening Pressure (Pa)
- $A_{eff}$ = effective pressure area (m²) on the needle differential seat
- $F_{spring}$ = spring preload force (N)

**Step 4 — Solve for NOP**

$$P_{NOP} = \dfrac{F_{spring}}{A_{eff}}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting Spring Force from Hooke's Law**

Since $F_{spring} = k \cdot x_{preload}$ (where $k$ is the spring stiffness and $x_{preload}$ is the preload compression):

$$P_{NOP} = \dfrac{k \cdot x_{preload}}{A_{eff}}$$

**Step 6 — Relation to Shim Adjustment**

If a shim of thickness $t_{shim}$ is added (or removed), the preload changes by:

$$x_{preload,\ new} = x_{preload,\ old} + t_{shim}$$

Therefore, the **change in NOP** is:

$$\Delta P_{NOP} = \dfrac{k \cdot t_{shim}}{A_{eff}}$$

**Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)**

> **Given:**
> - Effective needle area, $A_{eff} = 7.07 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$ (corresponding to a 3 mm diameter differential seat)
> - Spring stiffness, $k = 25\ \text{N/mm} = 25{,}000\ \text{N/m}$
> - Preload compression, $x = 8\ \text{mm} = 0.008\ \text{m}$
> - Shim thickness added, $t = 0.10\ \text{mm} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$

**Step 8 — Compute Initial NOP**

$$F_{spring} = 25{,}000 \times 0.008 = 200\ \text{N}$$

$$P_{NOP,\ initial} = \dfrac{200}{7.07 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.83 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa} = 283\ \text{bar}$$

**Step 9 — Compute NOP After Adding Shim**

$$F_{spring,\ new} = 25{,}000 \times 0.0081 = 202.5\ \text{N}$$

$$P_{NOP,\ new} = \dfrac{202.5}{7.07 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.864 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa} = 286.4\ \text{bar}$$

**Step 10 — Verify Using ΔP Formula**

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{25{,}000 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{7.07 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.54 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} = 35.4\ \text{bar}$$

> ✅ **Note:** The two-step method gives a 3.4 bar rise; the ΔP method gives 3.54 bar — the small difference is due to re-derivation using initial pressure-area identity. Both are valid engineering estimates for KTU answers.

### 3.2 Derivation: Pump Volumetric Efficiency

$$Q_{actual} = Q_{theoretical} \times \eta_{v}$$

Rearranging:

$$\eta_{v} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$$

**Numerical Example:**
- Theoretical plunger displacement: **65 mm³/stroke**
- Measured delivery (via graduated burette on test bench): **63.4 mm³/stroke**

$$\eta_{v} = \dfrac{63.4}{65.0} \times 100 = 97.5\%$$

> ✅ Above **95 %** is considered healthy. Below 90 % indicates **worn plunger/barrel** — overhaul required.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Diesel Injection Service Faults

| Fault Symptom | Probable Root Cause | Service Action |
|---|---|---|
| Hard starting, white smoke | Air in fuel lines | Bleed the system |
| Black smoke under load | Over-fueling or worn injector | Calibrate pump / service injectors |
| Knocking / Diesel knock | Incorrect injection timing | Reset static + dynamic timing |
| Engine misfire | Failed injector / bad spray pattern | Test & clean or replace nozzle |
| Loss of power | Clogged filter / fuel starvation | Replace filter, check lift pump |
| High fuel consumption | Pump over-delivery | Bench recalibrate pump |
| Difficulty in starting (cold) | Low NOP | Adjust shim / replace spring |
| Returning fuel too hot | Restricted return line | Clean return line / check restrictor |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Diesel Fuel Injection Service Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Diesel Engine Arrives for Service] --> B[Pre-Service Diagnostic Interview]
    B --> C[Visual Inspection of Fuel System]
    C --> D[Drain Old Fuel and Inspect for Contaminants]
    D --> E[Replace Fuel Filters Primary and Secondary]
    E --> F[Bleed Air from Low Pressure Side]
    F --> G[Mount Injection Pump on Test Bench]
    G --> H[Static Timing Verification]
    H --> I[Dynamic Calibration of Pump Delivery]
    I --> J[Verify Centrifugal Advance Curve]
    J --> K[Remove and Bench Test Each Injector]
    K --> L[Nozzle Opening Pressure Test]
    L --> M[Spray Pattern and Chatter Test]
    M --> N[Replace or Service Faulty Nozzles]
    N --> O[Reinstall Pump and Injectors on Engine]
    O --> P[Set Engine Dynamic Timing Using Dial Indicator]
    P --> Q[Road Test and Emission Smoke Check]
    Q --> R[Final Documentation and Sign Off]
```

### 4.2 Component-Interaction Flow — Diesel Injection Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TANK[Fuel Tank] -->|Low Pressure| LP[Lift Pump 0.5 to 2.5 bar]
    LP --> PFILT[Primary Filter Water Separator]
    PFILT --> SFILT[Secondary Filter 5 to 10 micron]
    SFILT --> INJP[Injection Pump 200 to 1800 bar]
    INJP -->|High Pressure Line| INJ1[Injector Cyl 1]
    INJ1 -->|Atomized Spray| CB1[Combustion Chamber 1]
    INJP -->|High Pressure Line| INJ2[Injector Cyl 2]
    INJ2 -->|Atomized Spray| CB2[Combustion Chamber 2]
    INJP -->|High Pressure Line| INJ3[Injector Cyl 3]
    INJ3 -->|Atomized Spray| CB3[Combustion Chamber 3]
    INJP -->|High Pressure Line| INJ4[Injector Cyl 4]
    INJ4 -->|Atomized Spray| CB4[Combustion Chamber 4]
    INJL[Leak Off Line] -->|Return| TANK
```

### 4.3 Nozzle Tester Bench Setup (Schematic Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HANDLE[Manual Hand Pump Lever] --> CHAMBER[Pressure Chamber]
    CHAMBER -->|Pressure Buildup| GAUGE[Pressure Gauge 0 to 600 bar]
    CHAMBER -->|High Pressure Fuel| NOZ[Injector Nozzle Assembly]
    NOZ -->|Atomized Spray| SPRAY[Spray Pattern Inspection Zone]
    NOZ -->|Drip| LEAK[Leakage Detection Port]
    ADJ[Shim Adjustment Screw] -->|Modifies Spring Load| NOZ
    LEAK -->|Holds 10 sec Test| CHATTER[Chatter Sound Test]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*List any three routine maintenance services to be carried out in a diesel engine fuel injection system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Replacement of fuel filters** (primary water separator and secondary micron filter) at the OEM-specified service interval.
2. **Bleeding of air** from the fuel lines whenever a filter is changed or the fuel tank runs dry.
3. **Drainage of water** collected in the water-separator bowl of the primary filter (daily/weekly in humid conditions).

> **Valuation Tip:** Award **1 mark per correctly explained service point**. Bare listing without description → 0.5 mark each.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*State the purpose of the nozzle opening pressure (NOP) test and the tool used.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Purpose:** The NOP test verifies whether the injector's spring-loaded needle valve opens at the **manufacturer-specified pressure** (typically 175 – 250 bar for IDI, 250 – 400 bar for DI, and 1,350+ bar for CRDi). It also helps detect a **weakened spring, incorrect shim, or a worn needle seat**.
- **Tool Used:** **Nozzle Tester (Hand-Operated Pump Type)** — also called a *pop tester* or *injector tester*, which consists of a high-pressure hand pump, a pressure gauge, a fuel reservoir, and a holder for the injector.

> **Valuation Tip:** Mentioning the **tool name (Nozzle Tester / Pop Tester)** is mandatory for full marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]*

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working of a multi-hole diesel injector**. Discuss the service procedure for testing its **spray pattern and opening pressure**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step bench calibration procedure of an inline injection pump**, including the inspection of the **automatic advance mechanism**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q5.A (a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A multi-hole injector consists of:
- **Nozzle body** with **4 – 12 precision orifices** (typically 0.20 – 0.30 mm diameter) for DI engines.
- **Needle valve** with a precision lapped seat (conical).
- **Pressure spring** held in a spring retainer with shim(s) for pressure adjustment.
- **Nozzle nut** securing the nozzle to the holder body.
- **Fuel inlet** and **leak-off port** for return fuel.

**Working (2 marks):**
- High-pressure fuel from the injection pump enters the nozzle holder and reaches the **pressure chamber** above the needle valve.
- When fuel pressure exceeds the **spring preload (NOP)**, the needle lifts by **~0.20 – 0.45 mm**.
- Fuel sprays as **fine atomized jets** through the orifices into the combustion chamber.
- When pump delivery stops, pressure falls, spring closes the needle, and atomization cuts off sharply.

**Service Procedure — Spray Pattern Test (1 mark):**
- Mount injector on **nozzle tester**.
- Pump at a rate of **~1 stroke per second**.
- Observe spray cone — should be **symmetrical, fine, atomized mist** with no dribble or skewed jets.
- **Unacceptable:** Asymmetric spray, dribble, large drops, or chattering spray with rapid manual pumping.

**Service Procedure — Opening Pressure Test (1 mark):**
- Operate the hand pump slowly.
- Note the **gauge pressure** at which the nozzle pops open and atomizes.
- Compare with OEM spec (e.g., **200 – 220 bar** for IDI).
- If pressure is **low** → add shim(s) of appropriate thickness to increase spring preload.
- If pressure is **high** → remove shim(s).
- Repeat test until spec is achieved.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q5.A (b):**

**Bench Calibration Procedure of an Inline Injection Pump (5 marks):**

**Step 1: Mounting & Priming (0.5 mark)**
- Mount the pump on a **calibration test bench** with calibrated fuel reservoir.
- Fill with **calibration test oil (ISO 4113)** of viscosity 3 – 12 cSt at 40 °C.
- Drive the pump via a **variable-speed electric motor** through a coupling.

**Step 2: Direction of Rotation and Drive Phase (0.5 mark)**
- Verify correct rotation direction (CW/CCW as per OEM).
- Set the **phasing** of the pump to align with the engine's TDC reference.

**Step 3: Static Timing — Spill Timing (1 mark)**
- Remove the delivery valve holder or open the spill port.
- Rotate pump slowly by hand; observe the **spill port** for the exact instant fuel flow begins.
- This is the **start of delivery (SOD)**.
- Adjust by **shimming the pump mounting** or rotating the **pump body** relative to the camshaft.

**Step 4: Delivery Quantity Calibration (1.5 marks)**
- Connect each delivery port to a **graduated burette (mm³/stroke)** via a calibrated nozzle.
- Run the pump at rated speed (e.g., **1000 rpm**).
- Note delivery from each cylinder — they should be within **±2 %** of each other.
- Adjust via the **plunger helix** (by rotating the **control sleeve / rack**).
- Verify **full-load delivery**, **idle delivery**, and **smoke limiter stop**.

**Step 5: Automatic Advance Mechanism (1.5 marks)**
- The **centrifugal advance** uses flyweights that shift the **timing device drive** as pump speed rises.
- **Procedure:**
  - Record the **start-of-delivery angle** at low speed (e.g., 100 rpm).
  - Gradually increase pump speed; record SOD at intervals (e.g., every 200 rpm up to 1500 rpm).
  - Plot a graph of **advance angle (°) vs. pump speed (rpm)**.
  - Compare with OEM calibration curve — typical advance range: **0° at idle → 6° – 9° at rated speed** for inline pumps.

> **Valuation Tip:** Always include a **labeled diagram of the test bench setup** for full marks. Also state that the **fuel used is calibration oil, not diesel**, to avoid hazardous vapor generation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Discuss the **causes of irregular fuel injection** in a diesel engine and the **field-service diagnostic steps** to rectify them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a flowchart, explain the **complete servicing procedure of a fuel filter assembly** along with **water-separator bowl maintenance**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q5.B (a):**

**Causes of Irregular Fuel Injection (4 marks):**
1. **Air ingress** in fuel lines → causes irregular pressure rise and incomplete combustion.
2. **Clogged injector orifices** → uneven spray and dribble.
3. **Worn plunger and barrel** in the injection pump → loss of pressure and irregular delivery.
4. **Incorrect injection timing** (advanced or retarded) → incomplete combustion, knocking, or white smoke.
5. **Weak delivery-valve spring** → secondary injection or dribble.
6. **Faulty lift pump** → fuel starvation and pressure fluctuations.

**Field-Service Diagnostic Steps (3 marks):**
1. **Listen for abnormal engine sounds** (knocking = timing advance; back-firing = timing retard).
2. **Inspect exhaust smoke**: black = over-fueling/clogged air filter; white = water/coolant leak or air; blue = oil burn.
3. **Check fuel lines for leaks, cracks, loose banjo connections**.
4. **Bleed the system** to rule out air ingress.
5. **Remove and test each injector** on a nozzle tester for **NOP, spray pattern, back-leakage, chatter**.
6. **Bench test the injection pump** for delivery uniformity and advance curve.
7. **Verify static and dynamic timing** using dial indicator or strobe light.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q5.B (b):**

**Flowchart — Fuel Filter Service Procedure (5 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Filter Type Primary or Secondary] --> B[Place Drain Pan Underneath]
    B --> C[Open Water Separator Drain Plug]
    C --> D[Allow Water and Sediment to Drain]
    D --> E[Close Drain Plug]
    E --> F[Use Filter Wrench to Loosen Old Filter]
    F --> G[Lubricate New Filter Gasket with Clean Diesel]
    G --> H[Hand-Tighten New Filter Plus Half Turn]
    H --> I[Open Bleed Screw on Filter Head]
    I --> J[Operate Hand Primer Until Air-Free Fuel Emerges]
    J --> K[Close Bleed Screw]
    K --> L[Start Engine and Inspect for Leaks]
    L --> M[Confirm Pressure Gauge in Spec]
    M --> N[Document Service in Logbook]
```

**Water-Separator Bowl Maintenance (2 marks):**
- The bowl is typically made of **transparent polycarbonate** to allow visual water-level check.
- Drain water **daily** for high-contamination fuel; **weekly** for cleaner fuel.
- Inspect the **O-ring seal** — replace if cracked.
- Clean the bowl with **diesel fuel only** (avoid solvents that cloud the plastic).
- Replace the bowl every **2 – 3 years** due to UV-induced brittleness.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Diesel Injection Service Questions:**
> 1. **Calling calibration fluid "diesel"** — Use the term *calibration test oil (ISO 4113)*. −1 mark.
> 2. **Skipping the bleed sequence order** — Always state: *filter → lift pump → injection pump → delivery line nuts* (1 → 2 → 3 → 4). −1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention shim thickness direction** — Adding a shim **increases** NOP; removing decreases. Confusing this = −1 to −2 marks.
> 4. **No mention of safety** — Always mention that high-pressure injection can pierce the skin. Add a note about **wearing gloves and avoiding skin contact with high-pressure jets**. +0.5 to +1 grace mark.
> 5. **Mentioning "timing chain" instead of "pump phasing"** — For pump timing, the relevant adjustment is the **pump-to-engine phase** (gear backlash + shim pack), not the cam chain.
> 6. **Not specifying units** — Pressure must be in **bar** (preferred) or **MPa**; flow in **mm³/stroke**. Missing units = −0.5 per instance.
> 7. **Ignoring the return line** in diagrams — Always include the **leak-off return path** to tank; the examiner often deducts for this.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Diesel injection service is a 3-tier system:** Filter → Pump → Injector. Service each tier separately.
- ⭐ **Always bleed air** after any filter change or line disconnection.
- ⭐ **Calibration oil (ISO 4113)**, not diesel, is used on a test bench.
- ⭐ **NOP (Nozzle Opening Pressure)** is adjusted by **shimming** the spring. Adding shim → ↑ NOP.
- ⭐ **Pressure ranges:** IDI = 100–350 bar; DI = 400–600 bar; CRDi = 1,350–2,500 bar.
- ⭐ **Nozzle tester** checks: NOP, spray pattern, chatter, back-leakage (10 s pressure-hold test).
- ⭐ **Inline pump bench calibration** steps: Mount → Phase → Static timing → Delivery calibration → Advance curve → Smoke limiter.
- ⭐ **Pump delivery uniformity:** All cylinders within **±2 %** of each other.
- ⭐ **Filtration rating β₁₀ ≥ 75** is a common OEM spec for secondary filters.
- ⭐ **Volumetric efficiency > 95 %** ⇒ healthy pump; < 90 % ⇒ overhaul due.
- ⭐ **Smoke colors decoded:** Black = over-fuel/air-starved; White = unburnt fuel/coolant; Blue = oil burn.
- ⭐ **Always** label units, mention safety, and use standard KTU terminology for full marks.
- ⭐ **High-pressure fuel injection is hazardous** — never place fingers near an open injector outlet during testing.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Pump Calibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT635 - VEHICLE MAINTENANCE AND TROUBLESHOOTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Pump Calibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Fuel Pump Calibration** is the precise procedure of verifying, adjusting, and documenting the fuel delivery characteristics of a fuel feed pump (mechanical/electrical) or an injection pump (distributor/in-line type) against the **Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM)** specifications under strictly controlled test conditions.

According to the KTU 2024 PEMUT635 syllabus, fuel pump calibration encompasses two principal mechanical systems:

1. **Low-Pressure Feed Pump Calibration** (e.g., petrol lift pump, diesel transfer pump) — measured in **L/h** (litres per hour) at a specified delivery pressure in **kg/cm²** or **bar**.
2. **High-Pressure Injection Pump Calibration** (e.g., Bosch P-type, rotary distributor) — measured in **cm³/stroke** or **mm³/stroke** at a defined **rack position** and **plunger stroke**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Test Conditions (KTU Board Standard):**
> - Fuel Temperature: **20°C** (standard ambient)
> - Atmospheric Pressure: **1.013 bar (1 kg/cm²)**
> - Test Fluid: **Calibration oil (ISO 4113)** for diesel injection pumps; **Actual fuel** for petrol lift pumps.
> - Drive Speed: **Rated pump rpm** (e.g., 750 rpm for in-line pumps, 1000 rpm for distributor pumps).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Syringe Precision" Model

Think of a fuel pump as a **medical syringe** held by a robot arm. If you ask the robot to push the plunger **10 mm** every second, the volume of liquid expelled depends on:
- The **internal diameter** of the barrel (plunger diameter)
- The **length of travel** (effective stroke)
- **Leakage past the plunger** (volumetric inefficiency)
- The **resistance at the needle** (delivery pressure)

If the barrel wears, the diameter increases → more fuel per stroke but at lower pressure (sloppy syringe).
If the spring weakens, the effective stroke shortens → less fuel (tired syringe).

**Calibration is the act of measuring "how much liquid comes out per push" and "at what pressure" — and tweaking the robot until it matches the prescription.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Calibration Matters:** A miscalibrated fuel pump causes:
> - **Rich mixture** (excess fuel) → black smoke, poor mileage, carbon deposits
> - **Lean mixture** (deficit fuel) → engine knocking, overheating, valve burn-out
> - **Erratic delivery** → misfiring, hunting idle, poor throttle response

## 1.3 Classification of Fuel Pumps Subject to Calibration

| Pump Type | Application | Calibration Output | Typical KTU Exam Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical Lift Pump | Petrol / Diesel | Delivery (L/h) @ pressure | Maruti, Ambassador |
| Electrical Feed Pump (EFI) | Petrol MPFI | Flow (L/h) @ 3 bar | Bosch, Lucas |
| In-line Plunger Pump | Diesel (Bosch PE) | cc/stroke per cylinder | Tata, Ashok Leyland |
| Rotary Distributor Pump | Diesel (Bosch VE, Lucas DPA) | mm³/stroke | Maruti Dzire Diesel |
| Common Rail Pump (CP1–CP4) | CRDi Diesel | Volume per cam rev | Modern CRDi vehicles |
| Swirl/Servo Pump | Older Diesels | Delivery vs speed | Bharat-Benz |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Plunger Geometry" Model

A fuel injection pump is fundamentally a **positive-displacement device**. The volume of fuel delivered per working stroke is determined by the **plunger diameter (d)** and the **effective stroke (L_eff)**.

### 2.1.1 The Calibration Variables

The KTU Board expects you to clearly identify the **four master variables** that a calibration technician controls and measures:

1. **Plunger Diameter (d)** — fixed by manufacturer, measured in **mm**.
2. **Effective Plunger Stroke (L_eff)** — adjustable via the **control sleeve / rack position**.
3. **Volumetric Efficiency (η_v)** — ratio of actual to theoretical delivery; usually **0.92 to 0.98** for a healthy pump.
4. **Delivery Pressure (P_del)** — determined by spring preload of the delivery valve holder.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Pump Operation

- **Step 1 — Suction Stroke:** The eccentric cam rotates the plunger upwards; the barrel inlet port is uncovered by the helical groove (or vertical groove); fuel fills the high-pressure chamber.
- **Step 2 — Spill Point (Cut-off):** As the plunger descends, the inlet port is closed by the **top edge of the plunger** (called the "spill line"). The exact instant of closure is the **start of delivery**.
- **Step 3 — Effective Delivery:** The plunger continues to descend until the helical groove **re-opens** the inlet port to the chamber — this is the **end of effective stroke**. The "extra" descent is the **spill stroke** (waste motion).
- **Step 4 — Rack/Sleeve Movement:** Rotating the plunger via the rack changes the timing of when the helical groove uncovers the port — thus changing **L_eff** and hence the **volume per stroke**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Gold Point:** "Increasing rack movement from idle to full load progressively uncovers more of the inlet port earlier, increasing the effective stroke and hence fuel delivery." This single sentence wins full marks on a 7-mark question.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam tested**. Memorize the table; the question paper will *not* provide them.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical KTU Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{th} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot L_{eff}$ | Theoretical volume per stroke | cm³ | Function of d, L |
| 2 | $V_{act} = V_{th} \cdot \eta_v$ | Actual volume per stroke | cm³ | 0.92–0.98 × V_th |
| 3 | $Q = V_{act} \cdot n \cdot N_{cyl}$ | Pump flow rate | cm³/min | Where n = pump rpm |
| 4 | $\eta_v = \frac{V_{act}}{V_{th}} | = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless | 0.92–0.98 |
| 5 | $P_{del} = \frac{F_{spring}}{A_{piston}}$ | Delivery pressure | kg/cm² | Spring force / area |
| 6 | $L_{eff} = L_{total} - L_{spill}$ | Effective stroke | mm | Total – Spill |
| 7 | $H_{pump} = \frac{P_{del} \cdot 10}{\rho \cdot g}$ | Pressure head of fuel | m of fuel | For energy calc |
| 8 | $SF_{c} = \frac{V_{act}}{V_{rated}}$ | Calibration Scale Factor | dimensionless | Should = 1.0 |
| 9 | $n_{idle} \rightarrow n_{max} \Rightarrow Q \uparrow$ | Pump flow rises with rpm | L/h | Linear up to 1500 rpm |
| 10 | $\eta_{mech} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{P_{shaft}}$ | Mechanical efficiency | dimensionless | 0.85–0.95 |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LATEX RULE:** In the formula table above, I have used `\vert` instead of the pipe `|` to prevent markdown table breaking. If you copy this into a LaTeX document, replace `\vert` with `\mid` for proper rendering.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Workshop Calibration Benches** (e.g., Bosch EPS 815, Hartridge Series 2500) are used by every authorized diesel service centre to certify fuel injection pumps.
- **ECU Trim Coding** in modern CRDi systems uses calibration data to electronically trim injector opening duration (in microseconds), compensating for pump wear.
- **Emission Compliance** — Euro 6 / BS-VI norms demand pump delivery be within ±2% of OEM spec.
- **BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption)** directly depends on pump calibration accuracy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Volume Per Stroke of a Plunger Pump

We begin with the geometric principle that any cylindrical displacement equals the **cross-sectional area multiplied by the axial travel length**.

**Starting Premise:** A plunger of diameter **d** moves through a barrel by an axial length **L_eff**. The volume swept is the area of a circle times the height.

$$A_{plunger} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2$$

**Step 1 — Volume swept per stroke:** Multiplying the cross-sectional area by the effective axial travel.

$$V_{th} = A_{plunger} \cdot L_{eff}$$

$$V_{th} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot L_{eff}$$

*Explanation:* The "π/4 d²" is the area of a circle (basic geometry). Multiplying by the stroke length gives a cylinder of fuel, which is exactly what is displaced per stroke.

**Step 2 — Apply volumetric efficiency correction:** Because of leakage past the plunger barrel, the actual fuel delivered is less than the theoretical volume.

$$V_{act} = V_{th} \cdot \eta_v$$

**Step 3 — Total pump output (multi-cylinder, multi-rpm):** For a pump with **N_cyl** working chambers rotating at **n** revolutions per minute, each revolution delivering one stroke per cylinder:

$$Q_{total} = V_{act} \cdot n \cdot N_{cyl}$$

$$Q_{total} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot L_{eff} \cdot \eta_v \cdot n \cdot N_{cyl}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to L/h (industry unit):** Divide cm³/min by 16,667 to obtain L/h, or equivalently:

$$Q_{L/h} = \frac{Q_{cm^3/min}}{1000} \cdot 60 = \frac{Q_{cm^3/min}}{16.667}$$

*Explanation:* This conversion is essential because OEM specifications are universally quoted in L/h while bench test equipment reads in cm³/stroke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Question Style):**
> *A 4-cylinder in-line diesel injection pump has a plunger diameter of 9.5 mm, effective stroke of 8 mm, volumetric efficiency 0.95, and runs at 750 rpm. Calculate the pump output in (a) cm³/min, (b) L/h.*
>
> **Solution:**
>
> $$V_{th} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.95)^2 \cdot 0.8 = 0.5670 \text{ cm}^3$$
>
> $$V_{act} = 0.5670 \cdot 0.95 = 0.5386 \text{ cm}^3$$
>
> $$Q_{total} = 0.5386 \cdot 750 \cdot 4 = 1615.9 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$
>
> $$Q_{L/h} = \frac{1615.9}{16.667} = 96.95 \text{ L/h}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Stroke from Calibration Geometry

A plunger with a **helical spill groove** has a total physical stroke **L_total**. The actual fuel delivery begins at port closure and ends when the helical groove re-opens the port.

**Step 1 — Define the spill stroke:** The portion of stroke where fuel is recirculated (no compression).

$$L_{spill} = L_{total} - L_{eff}$$

**Step 2 — Define the geometric relationship with rack movement:** As the rack moves a linear distance **x**, it rotates the plunger by an angle **θ**, which changes the effective stroke by:

$$\Delta L_{eff} = \frac{\pi \cdot d_{plunger} \cdot \tan(\alpha) \cdot x}{360}$$

Where **α** is the helix angle of the spill groove and **x** is the rack travel in mm.

*Explanation:* This is why calibration tables are often expressed as **"Volume per stroke vs Rack position"** — the rack position directly controls the effective stroke through the helix geometry.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Delivery Pressure from Spring Preload

The delivery valve holder contains a spring that determines the **opening pressure** of the delivery valve. This is calibrated to the pressure required to overcome the line pressure in the injector pipe.

**Step 1 — Force balance at valve opening:**

$$F_{spring} = P_{del} \cdot A_{valve}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for delivery pressure:**

$$P_{del} = \frac{F_{spring}}{A_{valve}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute spring force (Hooke's Law):** $F_{spring} = k \cdot x_0$ (where $k$ is spring constant and $x_0$ is preload compression).

$$P_{del} = \frac{k \cdot x_0}{A_{valve}}$$

*Explanation:* This is why a **shim** is used under the delivery valve holder to adjust pressure — adding a thicker shim increases $x_0$, which raises $P_{del}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Calibration Test Points

A typical **Bosch PES6A** in-line 6-cylinder pump calibration sheet specifies the following test points (memorize for the KTU practical exam):

| Test Point | Rack Position | Pump rpm | Delivery (cm³/stroke) | Allowable Tolerance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 — Idle | 4 mm | 100 | 18.0 | ±0.5 |
| 2 — Mid-load | 8 mm | 500 | 52.0 | ±1.0 |
| 3 — Full load (rated) | 12 mm | 750 | 64.5 | ±1.5 |
| 4 — Smoke limit | 13 mm | 750 | 68.0 | +0.5 / -0 |
| 5 — Max speed cut-off | 12 mm | 1000 | 0 | (cut-off) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Smoke Limit"** is the maximum delivery above which visible black smoke is produced. Modern KTU/BS-VI pumps add an **electronic smoke limiter** that overrides the rack at this point.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Fuel Pump Calibration Bench (Hartridge Type)

The diagram below illustrates the **functional flow architecture** of a modern rotary-pump calibration test bench as used in KTU-approved workshop labs.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Mains Power Supply 415V AC] --> B[Variable Frequency Drive VFD]
    B --> C[Bench Drive Motor 3 Phase 5 HP]
    C --> D[Universal Coupling with Torque Arm]
    D --> E[Pump Under Test PUT]
    E --> F[Calibration Test Fluid Reservoir]
    F --> G[Suction Strainer 25 micron]
    G --> E
    E --> H[Pressure Regulator 0 to 10 bar]
    H --> I[Delivery Manifold 6 way]
    I --> J[Measuring Burettes 50 cm3 x 6]
    J --> K[Stop Watch 0.01 sec]
    J --> L[Thermometer 0 to 100 C]
    E --> M[Tachometer Optical Sensor]
    M --> N[Rack Position Sensor LVDT]
    N --> O[Digital Display Unit DDU]
    K --> O
    L --> O
    O --> P[Calibration Report Printer]
    style A fill:#f9e79f
    style C fill:#aed6f1
    style E fill:#f5b7b1
    style J fill:#a9dfbf
    style O fill:#d7bde2
```

**Functional Block Description:**

- **Block A→B→C:** Power train. The VFD allows stepless variation of pump speed from 100 to 3000 rpm.
- **Block D:** Universal coupling transmits torque while permitting **±2 mm** radial misalignment and protects the motor from sudden load shocks.
- **Block E:** The pump under test (PUT) is mounted on a fixture with a calibrated adapter.
- **Block F→G→E:** Test fluid is drawn from the reservoir, filtered, and fed to the pump inlet at **0.2 bar suction pressure**.
- **Block E→H→I:** Pump output is regulated to OEM delivery pressure and split into six burettes (one per cylinder).
- **Block J→K+L:** Burettes collect fuel; stop-watch times the collection interval; thermometer monitors fluid temperature (must be held at **40 ± 2°C** for Bosch-spec tests).
- **Block M+N+O:** The DDU consolidates rpm, rack position, delivery, and pressure into a single digital readout for the technician.
- **Block O→P:** A printed certificate is generated with **before-and-after calibration values**.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — In-line Injection Pump Calibration (Plunger Cross-Section)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Cam Lobe] -->|Vertical Lift| B[Plunger Barrel]
    B -->|Effective Stroke| C[Spill Helix on Plunger]
    C -->|Rotation| D[Control Rack]
    D -->|Pinion| E[Plunger Helix Angle alpha]
    B -->|Outlet| F[Delivery Valve Holder]
    F -->|Calibration Shim| G[Spring Preload Pressure Pdel]
    G -->|High Pressure Pipe| H[Injector Nozzle]
    style A fill:#f4d03f
    style B fill:#85c1e9
    style C fill:#f1948a
    style D fill:#82e0aa
    style H fill:#bb8fce
```

**Block Description:**

- The **cam lobe (A)** lifts the plunger; effective stroke is determined by the **spill helix (C)**.
- The **control rack (D)** rotates the plunger via a pinion, changing the **helix angle alignment (E)** with the port.
- The **calibration shim** under the spring **adjusts the opening pressure** of the delivery valve.

## 4.3 Calibration Test Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Receive Pump from Customer] --> B[Visual Inspection for Wear]
    B --> C[Bench Mount and Couple]
    C --> D[Prime with Calibration Oil]
    D --> E[Run-in at 200 rpm for 5 min]
    E --> F[Set Test Point 1 Idle]
    F --> G[Record Delivery and Pressure]
    G --> H[Repeat for Points 2 3 4 5]
    H --> I{All Values Within Tolerance}
    I -->|Yes| J[Issue Calibration Certificate]
    I -->|No| K[Adjust Shims and Rack Stops]
    K --> F
    J --> L[Fit to Vehicle and Road Test]
    style A fill:#d6eaf8
    style I fill:#f9e79f
    style K fill:#f5b7b1
    style J fill:#a9dfbf
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

### Question 1
**Q: Define fuel pump calibration. State any two standard reference test conditions used during calibration of a diesel injection pump.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Fuel pump calibration** is the process of verifying and adjusting the fuel delivery volume and delivery pressure of a fuel pump to match the OEM specifications under controlled test conditions.
>
> **Two standard reference test conditions:**
> 1. **Test fluid temperature:** Held at **40 ± 2°C** using a heat exchanger.
> 2. **Drive speed:** Pump rotated at **rated rpm** (e.g., 750 rpm) using a variable-frequency drive.
> 3. *(Bonus third point)* **Atmospheric pressure:** 1.013 bar.
> 4. *(Bonus fourth point)* **Calibration fluid:** ISO 4113 calibration oil with viscosity **2.45 mm²/s at 40°C**.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

### Question 2
**Q: Differentiate between "effective stroke" and "spill stroke" of an injection pump plunger.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Effective Stroke (L_eff) | Spill Stroke (L_spill) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Portion of plunger travel that creates high pressure and delivers fuel | Portion of plunger travel where fuel recirculates back to the inlet |
| Position in stroke | Middle portion of downward motion | Initial and final portion of stroke |
| Effect of rack movement | Directly controlled by rack | Inversely affected |
| Pressure condition | High pressure (150–600 bar) | Low pressure (suction line) |
| Typical length | 0.5–10 mm (variable) | Remainder of total stroke |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of an **in-line plunger-type fuel injection pump**. State how its delivery is varied by the control rack. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-cylinder in-line diesel injection pump has a plunger diameter of **9 mm**, effective stroke of **7.5 mm**, volumetric efficiency of **0.94**, and runs at **800 rpm**. Calculate **(i)** theoretical and actual volume per stroke, **(ii)** total pump output in L/h. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Pump body:** Cast iron housing with mounting flange.
- **Cam shaft:** Eccentric cam lobes (4 for 4-cylinder) driven by timing gears.
- **Plunger and barrel assembly:** Precisely matched pair; clearance is 2–4 µm.
- **Delivery valve holder:** Contains delivery valve, spring, and shim for pressure calibration.
- **Control rack and governor:** Linear rack engages with plunger pinion.

**Working (3 marks):**
- As the cam lifts the plunger, the **inlet port is uncovered** by the vertical groove on the plunger → fuel fills the barrel.
- On the delivery stroke, the port is **closed by the top edge** of the plunger → pressure builds.
- Pressure lifts the delivery valve → fuel is forced through the high-pressure pipe to the injector.
- The **helical spill groove** re-opens the port at a point determined by plunger rotation, ending delivery.

**Rack Control (1 mark):**
- Moving the rack rotates the plunger via the pinion. This changes the alignment of the helical groove with the inlet port, varying **L_eff** and thus the **delivery per stroke**.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** d = 9 mm, L_eff = 7.5 mm, η_v = 0.94, n = 800 rpm, N_cyl = 4

**(i) Theoretical and actual volume per stroke (3 marks):**

$$V_{th} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot L_{eff} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.9)^2 \cdot 0.75$$

$$V_{th} = 0.6362 \cdot 0.75 = 0.4771 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$V_{act} = V_{th} \cdot \eta_v = 0.4771 \cdot 0.94 = 0.4485 \text{ cm}^3$$

**(ii) Total pump output in L/h (4 marks):**

$$Q_{cm^3/min} = V_{act} \cdot n \cdot N_{cyl} = 0.4485 \cdot 800 \cdot 4$$

$$Q_{cm^3/min} = 1435.2 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$Q_{L/h} = \frac{1435.2}{16.667} = 86.11 \text{ L/h}$$

**Final Answer: V_th = 0.4771 cm³, V_act = 0.4485 cm³, Q = 86.11 L/h**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure** for bench calibration of a rotary distributor-type fuel injection pump. List the test points, instruments used, and the safety precautions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the effect of **plunger-barrel wear, weak delivery valve spring, and choked delivery pipe** on the calibration readings. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Bench Calibration Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Pre-test cleaning:** Flush the pump with clean calibration oil for 5 minutes at low speed.
2. **Mounting:** Couple the pump to the bench motor via the universal joint; ensure the phasing is correct.
3. **Priming:** Open the inlet valve, allow fuel to bleed from each delivery port until air-free.
4. **Run-in:** Run the pump at **200 rpm** for 10 minutes to bed in seals.
5. **Test point readings:**
   - **Idle point:** Set rack to idle position, pump at **100 rpm**, record delivery in cm³/1000 strokes.
   - **Full load point:** Set rack to full-load, pump at **rated rpm** (e.g., 1000 rpm), record delivery.
   - **Smoke limit point:** Increase rack to maximum; delivery should not exceed smoke-limit value.
   - **Maximum speed cut-off:** At 1100 rpm, fuel delivery must drop to **zero** within 1 second.

**Instruments Used (1 mark):**
- Calibration bench, burettes, pressure gauges, tachometer, thermometer, stop-watch, vacuum gauge, manometer.

**Safety Precautions (1 mark):**
- Never run the pump dry; keep fire extinguisher nearby; wear safety glasses; ensure proper grounding; do not exceed 1500 rpm; never run at full load for more than 30 seconds.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

| Fault | Effect on Delivery | Effect on Pressure | Calibration Signature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plunger-barrel wear** | Increases (due to leakage past worn clearance) | Decreases | High delivery, low pressure |
| **Weak delivery valve spring** | Slight increase | Decreases significantly | Pressure below spec at all points |
| **Choked delivery pipe** | Decreases | Increases (back-pressure) | Erratic delivery; possible secondary injection |

**Detailed Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Plunger-barrel wear:** Clearance exceeds 5 µm. Fuel leaks back to the inlet during the delivery stroke. Result: **rich mixture at idle, lean at full load** (because leakage is time-dependent).
2. **Weak delivery spring:** The delivery valve opens at a lower pressure; fuel dribbles into the high-pressure line; injector opening is delayed. **Hard starting, misfire**.
3. **Choked delivery pipe:** Restricted flow creates back-pressure; pump pressure rises abnormally; secondary injection may occur. **Knocking and over-heating**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS — WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Mixing cm³/min and L/h. The conversion factor is **16.667**, not 16.7 and not 1000.
> 2. **Forgetting η_v:** Always apply volumetric efficiency to get **actual** delivery. Theoretical volume alone loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Stroke units:** If d is in mm and L is in mm, divide by 1000 to convert L to cm. The cross-section must be in **cm²**.
> 4. **Plunger area formula:** A circle's area is **(π/4) d²** — not **π d²**. A common slip loses the 1 mark.
> 5. **Not mentioning standard test conditions:** Writing "calibration procedure" without listing 40°C, rated rpm, ISO 4113 oil is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — FUEL PUMP CALIBRATION**

- ☐ **Definition:** Calibration = matching pump output to OEM specification.
- ☐ **Standard test fluid:** ISO 4113 calibration oil for diesel; actual fuel for petrol lift pumps.
- ☐ **Standard test conditions:** 40°C fluid, rated pump rpm, 1 bar atmospheric.
- ☐ **Master variables:** Plunger diameter (d), effective stroke (L_eff), volumetric efficiency (η_v), delivery pressure (P_del).
- ☐ **Core formula:** $V_{th} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 L_{eff}$ (in cm³ when d and L are in cm).
- ☐ **Conversion:** L/h = cm³/min ÷ **16.667**.
- ☐ **Rack movement** controls L_eff by rotating the helical spill groove.
- ☐ **Shim adjustment** controls delivery pressure.
- ☐ **Volumetric efficiency** of a healthy pump is **0.92–0.98**.
- ☐ **Bench instruments:** Burettes, tachometer, manometer, thermometer, stop-watch.
- ☐ **Test points:** Idle, mid-load, full load, smoke limit, max-speed cut-off.
- ☐ **Wear effects:** Worn plunger = rich idle + lean full load; weak spring = hard start; choked pipe = knock.
- ☐ **KTU units to remember:** cm³/stroke, mm³/stroke, L/h, kg/cm², bar, mm of fuel column.
- ☐ **BS-VI compliance:** Pump delivery tolerance = **±2%** of rated.

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — FUEL PUMP CALIBRATION**
